How does it differ from ADALINE in terms of architecture and capability?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **MADALINE (Many ADALINE)** is a multilayer feedforward neural network architecture proposed by **Bernard Widrow**, formed by interconnecting **multiple ADALINE units** in the first (hidden) layer, whose outputs are combined by a second-layer logic unit (or another ADALINE) to produce the final output. > > **Differences from ADALINE:** > - **Architecture:** ADALINE is a **single neuron**; MADALINE contains **many ADALINEs** organized in two (or more) layers. > - **Capability:** A single ADALINE can only solve **linearly separable** problems; MADALINE can solve **non-linearly separable** problems such as the **XOR** function. > - **Training rule:** ADALINE uses the simple **LMS (delta) rule**; MADALINE uses the **Madaline Rule I (MR1)** or **Madaline Rule II (MR2)** that selectively updates only those hidden ADALINEs whose sign contradicts the target. > > *(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — architecture difference, 1 mark — capability/training difference)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **State and explain the Madaline Rule I (MR1) algorithm.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Madaline Rule I (MR1)** is a training algorithm used for MADALINE networks where the hidden ADALINEs produce **bipolar outputs** $\{-1, +1\}$ and the output layer is a fixed logic unit. > > **Procedure:** > 1. For a misclassified input pattern, identify the **set of hidden ADALINEs** whose individual bipolar output is **opposite in sign to the desired target**. > 2. Among this set, select the ADALINE whose **net input magnitude is closest to zero** (i.e., the one that is *most uncertain / nearest to flipping*). > 3. **Invert (flip the sign of) all the weights and bias** of that selected ADALINE. > 4. This effectively toggles that ADALINE's response and moves the decision boundary in the desired direction. > > MR1 is **simple and fast** but limited to networks with a single output layer of fixed-logic type. *(1 mark — statement, 1 mark — selection criterion, 1 mark — weight-flipping operation)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]* **Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **architecture of MADALINE** with a neat block diagram. Compare MADALINE with ADALINE. **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Solution:** **1. MADALINE Architecture (4 marks):** MADALINE consists of: - **Input layer:** $n$ input nodes $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ (no computation, just fan-out). - **Madaline (hidden) layer:** $m$ parallel **ADALINE units**. Each ADALINE $j$ computes: $$\text{net}_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{ij} x_i + b_j, \quad h_j = \text{net}_j$$ The hidden outputs are **linear (analog)** to preserve the smooth quadratic error surface that the LMS rule exploits. - **Output layer:** Either a **fixed-logic AND/OR/MAJORITY unit** (MADALINE I) or an **adaptive ADALINE** (MADALINE II). The output is: $$y = \text{sgn}\!\left( \sum_{j=1}^{m} c_j h_j \right) \in \{-1, +1\}$$ **2. Block Diagram (2 marks):** [Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 — to be hand-drawn in exam] **3. Comparison Table (1 mark):** [Refer to the Analytical Matrix in Section 3.3] --- **Q. (b)** A MADALINE network is to be designed to solve the **XOR** problem. Using **two ADALINEs in the hidden layer** with AND and NOR logic, and an OR combiner, demonstrate one complete epoch of training using the **Madaline Rule II** with learning rate $\eta = 1$. Initial weights are $w_{11} = w_{12} = 1, b_1 = -0.5$ (AND unit); $w_{21} = w_{22} = -1, b_2 = 1.5$ (NOR unit). **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Solution:** **Setup:** $c_1 = c_2 = +1$ (OR combiner). All four patterns and one full epoch are shown. *(Sample epoch table format — student is expected to complete it in exam):* | Pattern $k$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $d$ | $h_1 = \text{net}_1$ | $h_2 = \text{net}_2$ | $y = \text{sgn}(h_1 + h_2)$ | Error? | Update? | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0 | 0 | -1 | -0.5 | 1.5 | +1 | **Yes** | ADALINE 2 flips sign | | 2 | 0 | 1 | +1 | 0.5 | 0.5 | +1 | No | None | | 3 | 1 | 0 | +1 | 0.5 | 0.5 | +1 | No | None | | 4 | 1 | 1 | +1 | 1.5 | -0.5 | +1 | No | None | **MR2 update for Pattern 1 (since $d=-1$ but $y=+1$):** ADALINE 2 has $h_2 = +1.5$ which opposes $d=-1$. Apply LMS: $$w_{21}^{\text{new}} = -1 + (1)(-2)(0) = -1$$ $$w_{22}^{\text{new}} = -1 + (1)(-2)(0) = -1$$ $$b_2^{\text{new}} = 1.5 + (1)(-2) = -0.5$$ After this update, the network must be re-tested for convergence. *(2 marks — table, 3 marks — explicit update, 2 marks — final verification & conclusion that with a few more epochs, all four patterns become correctly classified.)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q. (a)** Derive the **LMS (Widrow–Hoff) weight update rule** for an ADALINE from first principles using gradient descent on the squared error. **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Solution:** [Complete derivation already shown in Section 3.1 of these notes — student must reproduce all 5 steps with the explicit gradient computation. The final boxed result is:] $$w_i(k+1) = w_i(k) + \eta \, e(k) \, x_i(k)$$ $$b(k+1) = b(k) + \eta \, e(k)$$ *(1 mark — loss function definition, 2 marks — gradient computation, 2 marks — update derivation, 1 mark — bias update, 1 mark — final compact form & convergence remark)* --- **Q. (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Madaline Rule II (MR2) training algorithm** in detail. State one real-world application. **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Solution:** **1. Diagram (2 marks):** [Refer to training-loop flowchart in Section 4.3] **2. MR2 Algorithm Steps (4 marks):** 1. Initialize all $m \times n$ weights of hidden ADALINEs to small random values. Set learning rate $\eta$. 2. For each training pattern $(\mathbf{x}, d)$: - Compute $h_j = \mathbf{w}_j^T \mathbf{x} + b_j$ for all $j = 1, \ldots, m$. - Compute $y = \text{sgn}(\sum_j c_j h_j)$. 3. If $y \neq d$, identify the set $S = \{j \mid \text{sgn}(h_j) \neq d\}$ (ADALINEs whose individual sign opposes the target). 4. For each $j \in S$, apply the LMS update: $$w_{ij}(k+1) = w_{ij}(k) + \eta \, d \, x_i(k) \quad \forall i$$ $$b_j(k+1) = b_j(k) + \eta \, d$$ *(Note: $d$ is used as the error surrogate because in the bipolar case, the difference $d - h_j$ is approximated by $d$ for the ADALINEs in $S$.)* 5. Repeat over all patterns and many epochs until the total error falls below tolerance $\varepsilon$. **3. Real-World Application (1 mark):** **Adaptive Noise Cancellation (ANC)** in biomedical ECG machines — the MADALINE learns to subtract maternal ECG from fetal ECG using a reference noise input. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places students lose marks in MADALINE questions:** > > 1. **Confusing MR1 and MR2.** MR1 **flips the signs** of weights of the nearest-to-zero ADALINE; MR2 performs an **LMS-style additive update** on all misfiring ADALINEs. Examiners specifically test this distinction. > > 2. **Forgetting the bias update.** Numerical MR2 problems always require explicit computation of $b_j(k+1) = b_j(k) + \eta\, d$. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks. > > 3. **Not writing the bipolar conversion.** When targets are given as $\{0, 1\}$, students must explicitly state: $0 \equiv -1, \; 1 \equiv +1$ for the bipolar MADALINE formulation. Examiners deduct 1 mark if this is skipped. > > 4. **Drawing only the perceptron-style diagram instead of the parallel-ADALINE Madaline layer.** Use distinct boxes for each ADALINE in the hidden layer with separate weight labels. > > 5. **Writing a generic "backpropagation" answer when asked about MADALINE.** MADALINE predates backpropagation and uses Madaline Rule, **not** gradient backpropagation. Examiners award 0 marks if backpropagation is mentioned where MR1/MR2 was asked. > > 6. **Failing to mention that MR1 is used with bipolar hidden outputs.** Always pair the rule name with its activation assumption. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — MADALINE** > > - **MADALINE = Many ADALINEs** (Widrow, 1960s). > - **Two canonical architectures:** MADALINE I (fixed-logic combiner) and MADALINE II (adaptive combiner). > - **Hidden ADALINEs use linear activation**; the **output combiner uses a hard-limit (signum) function**. > - **Training rules:** Madaline Rule I (MR1) — flips weights of nearest-to-zero misfiring ADALINE; Madaline Rule II (MR2) — LMS-style update of all misfiring ADALINEs. > - **LMS weight update:** $w_i(k+1) = w_i(k) + \eta\, e(k)\, x_i(k)$ — derived from gradient descent on $\frac{1}{2} e^2$. > - **Bias update:** $b(k+1) = b(k) + \eta\, e(k)$ — same rule with a permanent input of $+1$. > - **Error $e \in \{-2, 0, +2\}$** in bipolar MADALINE (since $d, y \in \{-1, +1\}$). > - **Key advantage over single ADALINE:** Solves **XOR** and other **non-linearly separable** problems. > - **Convergence caveat:** Multiple hidden ADALINEs create a **non-convex error surface** — local minima are possible, unlike single ADALINE's convex surface. > - **Engineering applications:** Adaptive noise cancellation, adaptive beamforming, channel equalization, adaptive control. > - **Historical importance:** Predecessor of modern backpropagation-trained MLPs; foundational in soft computing curriculum. > - **Bipolar encoding is mandatory** for MR1/MR2 problems — always convert $0 \to -1$ explicitly. > - **Parameters to set:** learning rate $\eta \in [0.1, 0.5]$, tolerance $\varepsilon \in [0.01, 0.1]$, max epochs $\in [100, 1000]$.

topics.title: Radial Basis Neural Networks: Concept and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT635 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Radial Basis Neural Networks: Concept and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Radial Basis Function Neural Network (RBFNN)** is a specialized class of **feedforward artificial neural network** that employs **radial basis activation functions** (typically Gaussian) in its hidden layer, as opposed to the sigmoid or ReLU activations used in standard Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs). It is formally defined as a universal approximator that performs **localized function approximation** by mapping an input space $\mathbb{R}^n$ onto a scalar or vector output through a three-layer architecture: an **input layer**, a **hidden layer** containing neurons with radially symmetric (distance-based) activation functions, and a **linear output layer**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Statement:** An RBFNN is a two-stage network where the hidden layer performs a **non-linear transformation** by computing the distance between the input vector and stored center vectors, and the output layer performs a **linear combination** of these radial responses to produce the final output.

The fundamental mathematical expression of a single RBF neuron is:

$$\phi(\mathbf{x}) = \phi(\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c} \Vert)$$

where $\mathbf{x}$ is the input vector, $\mathbf{c}$ is the **center** (prototype) vector of the neuron, and $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ denotes the **Euclidean norm**.

> [!NOTE]
> The term "radial" indicates that the function's response depends solely on the **radial distance** from a fixed center point, not on the directional orientation of the input — it is **rotationally invariant**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **cartographer** tasked with predicting the elevation at any point on a vast, unexplored island. You cannot measure every square inch of land, so you strategically place **a small number of survey poles** at representative locations. Each pole records a "bump" of influence — closer to a pole, the more its recorded elevation weighs into your prediction; farther away, the less it matters.

The RBF network works exactly like this:

- The **survey poles** are the **RBF centers** ($\mathbf{c}_i$).
- The **spread of influence** of each pole is the **width** or **radius** ($\sigma_i$).
- The **final elevation** at any queried point is a **weighted linear combination** of these influences — this is the output layer.

So, if you query a point very close to Pole 1, the network output is dominated by Pole 1's information. If you query midway between Pole 1 and Pole 2, both poles contribute roughly equally. This **local-to-global interpolation** is the defining strength of RBF networks.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever asked to "explain intuitively," use the analogy of "lighthouse beams" or "influence zones" — each RBF neuron lights up a spherical region in the input space, and the final output is a weighted sum of these illuminated responses.

### 1.3 Key Terminology and Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

The following parameters are **essential for KTU examinations** and should be memorized:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Range / Typical Value | Role in Network |
|-----------|--------|-------------------------------|-----------------|
| Center | $\mathbf{c}_i$ | Randomly selected from input data | Prototype vector |
| Width / Spread | $\sigma_i$ | $\mathbf{0.1}$ to $\mathbf{3.0}$ (empirical) | Controls locality of neuron |
| Number of hidden neurons | $M$ | $\leq N$ (where $N$ is training samples) | Determines model capacity |
| Output weight | $w_i$ | Learned via linear regression | Combines hidden responses |
| Bias (optional) | $b_0$ | Learned | Shifts output |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The **Gaussian function** is the most commonly used RBF in practice due to its smooth, differentiable, and localized response. Its general form is $\phi(r) = e^{-r^2/(2\sigma^2)}$, where $r = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c} \Vert$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Network Architecture — Structured Logical Breakdown

The RBFNN architecture consists of **three distinct layers**, each with a clearly defined mathematical role:

1. **Input Layer (Dimension $n$):**
   - Receives the input vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
   - Performs **no computation** — purely a passive distributor.
   - Number of neurons equals the number of input features.

2. **Hidden Layer (Dimension $M$):**
   - Each of the $M$ neurons computes a **radial basis function** $\phi_i(\mathbf{x})$.
   - For the $i$-th hidden neuron, the response is:
     $$\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \exp\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right)$$
   - This produces a **non-linear transformation** of the input into an $M$-dimensional feature space.
   - The transformation is **localized**: output is high when $\mathbf{x}$ is near $\mathbf{c}_i$, low otherwise.

3. **Output Layer (Dimension $p$):**
   - Performs a **linear weighted sum** of the hidden layer activations.
   - For a single output neuron:
     $$y(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_i \, \phi_i(\mathbf{x}) + b_0$$
   - For multiple outputs, this extends to a matrix multiplication $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{\Phi} + \mathbf{b}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this design is powerful:** The hidden layer **non-linearly warps** the input space into a higher-dimensional feature space where the problem becomes (approximately) **linearly separable**. The output layer then solves a simple linear problem. This **decoupling** is what makes RBF training fast and stable.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Component

- **Why radial?** Because most real-world patterns are **locally correlated** — nearby inputs produce similar outputs. Radial functions exploit this by having **strong responses only in a local region**.
- **Why Gaussian specifically?** Because it is:
  - **Smooth** (continuous, infinitely differentiable).
  - **Localized** (decays to zero rapidly with distance).
  - **Normalizable** (integrates to one).
  - **Theoretically well-grounded** in Cover's theorem on separability of patterns.
- **Why linear output?** Because the network reduces to a **linear regression problem** in the hidden space, which has a **closed-form solution** (pseudo-inverse) — no backpropagation needed!

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Hall Ready Formula Table — Memorize This!**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Gaussian RBF | $\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \exp\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right)$ | Most common RBF kernel |
| 2 | Inverse Multiquadric | $\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^2 + \sigma_i^2}}$ | Alternative kernel |
| 3 | Multiquadric | $\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \sqrt{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^2 + \sigma_i^2}$ | Alternative kernel |
| 4 | Thin Plate Spline | $\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^2 \ln(\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert)$ | Used in surface fitting |
| 5 | Network Output | $y(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_i \phi_i(\mathbf{x}) + b_0$ | Linear combination of hidden activations |
| 6 | Euclidean Distance | $\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n}(x_j - c_{ij})^2}$ | Distance metric in input space |
| 7 | Squared Distance | $\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^2 = (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i)^T(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i)$ | Vectorized form |
| 8 | Hidden Matrix | $\mathbf{\Phi} = [\phi_i(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})], \quad i=1..M, \, k=1..N$ | $M \times N$ activation matrix |
| 9 | Weight Solution (Pseudo-inverse) | $\mathbf{w} = (\mathbf{\Phi}\mathbf{\Phi}^T)^{-1}\mathbf{\Phi}\mathbf{Y}$ | Closed-form least squares |
| 10 | Output in Matrix Form | $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{\Phi} + \mathbf{b}$ | Compact vector form |
| 11 | Cover's Theorem | Probability of linear separability increases with $M$ in higher-dimensional space | Theoretical foundation |
| 12 | Width Effect (Large $\sigma$) | $\phi \to 1$ globally | Neuron becomes non-local |
| 13 | Width Effect (Small $\sigma$) | $\phi \to$ Kronecker delta | Neuron is ultra-localized |
| 14 | Universal Approximation | RBFNN with sufficient $M$ can approximate any continuous function on compact set | Theoretical guarantee |
| 15 | Regularization Term | $E = \frac{1}{2}\sum_k \Vert \mathbf{y}_k - \mathbf{t}_k \Vert^2 + \frac{\lambda}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ | Prevents overfitting |

> **Critical Reminder:** In the formulas above, $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ denotes the **Euclidean norm** and should never be confused with the absolute value $\vert \cdot \vert$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

RBF networks are widely deployed in engineering systems where **fast, accurate function approximation** from sparse data is critical:

- **Time-Series Prediction:** Financial forecasting, load forecasting in power systems.
- **Control Systems:** Adaptive inverse dynamics compensation in robotics.
- **Signal Processing:** Channel equalization, noise cancellation, speech recognition.
- **Geostatistics:** Spatial interpolation (e.g., ore-grade estimation in mining).
- **Medical Diagnosis:** Pattern classification from biomedical signals (ECG, EEG).
- **Image Processing:** Function approximation for image restoration and compression.
- **Aerospace:** Real-time system identification for flight control.

> [!TIP]
> **Why engineers prefer RBFNN over MLP:** Training is **orders of magnitude faster** because the output weights have a closed-form solution. No iterative backpropagation, no learning rate tuning, no vanishing gradient issues.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the RBF Network Output Equation

We derive the **complete output equation** of an RBF network from first principles, showing every algebraic transition.

**Step 1 — Define the input.**
Let the input to the network be a vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

**Step 2 — Define the centers of the hidden layer.**
The hidden layer has $M$ neurons, each with a center $\mathbf{c}_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, M$. These centers are the **prototype vectors** around which the radial functions are localized.

**Step 3 — Compute the squared Euclidean distance.**
For the $i$-th hidden neuron, the distance between the input and its center is:

$$d_i = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert$$

Squaring this distance (to avoid the square root in computation):

$$d_i^2 = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^2 = (x_1 - c_{i1})^2 + (x_2 - c_{i2})^2 + \ldots + (x_n - c_{in})^2$$

In compact vector notation:

$$d_i^2 = (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i)^T (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Gaussian RBF.**
The activation of the $i$-th hidden neuron is:

$$\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \exp\left(-\frac{d_i^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right) = \exp\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right)$$

**Why $2\sigma_i^2$?** This is the standard deviation scaling of the Gaussian. When $d_i = \sigma_i$, the output drops to $e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$ of its peak; at $d_i = 3\sigma_i$, it drops to $e^{-4.5} \approx 0.0111$, effectively zero.

**Step 5 — Aggregate the hidden layer outputs.**
Collecting all $M$ hidden neuron activations into a vector:

$$\mathbf{\Phi}(\mathbf{x}) = [\phi_1(\mathbf{x}), \phi_2(\mathbf{x}), \ldots, \phi_M(\mathbf{x})]^T$$

**Step 6 — Apply the linear output layer.**
The output is a weighted sum of the hidden activations, plus a bias term:

$$y(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_i \, \phi_i(\mathbf{x}) + b_0 = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{\Phi}(\mathbf{x}) + b_0$$

where $\mathbf{w} = [w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_M]^T$ are the output weights and $b_0$ is the bias.

This completes the derivation of the **forward pass** of an RBF network.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Closed-Form Weight Solution

This is the most important derivation for the KTU exam — it shows **why RBF training is so fast**.

**Step 1 — Set up the training data.**
Suppose we have $N$ training samples: $\{(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}, t_1), (\mathbf{x}^{(2)}, t_2), \ldots, (\mathbf{x}^{(N)}, t_N)\}$, where $t_k$ is the desired (target) output for input $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the hidden layer matrix $\mathbf{\Phi}$.**
For each training input, compute the hidden activations and stack them as columns:

$$\mathbf{\Phi} = \begin{bmatrix} \phi_1(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) & \phi_1(\mathbf{x}^{(2)}) & \cdots & \phi_1(\mathbf{x}^{(N)}) \\ \phi_2(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) & \phi_2(\mathbf{x}^{(2)}) & \cdots & \phi_2(\mathbf{x}^{(N)}) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \phi_M(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) & \phi_M(\mathbf{x}^{(2)}) & \cdots & \phi_M(\mathbf{x}^{(N)}) \end{bmatrix}$$

This is an $M \times N$ matrix.

**Step 3 — Write the network's prediction for all training samples in matrix form.**

$$\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{\Phi}$$

where $\mathbf{y} = [y(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}), y(\mathbf{x}^{(2)}), \ldots, y(\mathbf{x}^{(N)})]^T$ is the $N \times 1$ vector of predictions, and $\mathbf{W}$ is $M \times p$ for $p$ outputs.

**Step 4 — Define the error (sum of squared errors).**

$$E = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{N} (y_k - t_k)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t} \Vert^2$$

where $\mathbf{t} = [t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_N]^T$ is the target vector.

**Step 5 — Substitute the matrix form of the prediction.**

$$E = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{\Phi} - \mathbf{t} \Vert^2$$

**Step 6 — Minimize the error with respect to $\mathbf{W}$.**
Take the gradient and set it to zero:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \mathbf{\Phi} (\mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{\Phi} - \mathbf{t})^T = 0$$

**Step 7 — Solve the normal equation.**

$$\mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{\Phi}^T \mathbf{W} = \mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{t}$$

$$\mathbf{W} \mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{\Phi}^T = \mathbf{t}^T \mathbf{\Phi}^T$$

Assuming $\mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{\Phi}^T$ is invertible:

$$\mathbf{W} = (\mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{\Phi}^T)^{-1} \mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{t}$$

**Step 8 — The pseudo-inverse form.**
The above is equivalent to:

$$\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{\Phi}^{+} \mathbf{t}$$

where $\mathbf{\Phi}^{+} = \mathbf{\Phi}^T (\mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{\Phi}^T)^{-1}$ is the **Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** This is a **single-shot** solution — no iteration, no learning rate, no gradient descent. This is what makes RBFNN training **exponentially faster** than MLP backpropagation.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** An RBF network has $n = 2$ input features, $M = 2$ hidden neurons with centers $\mathbf{c}_1 = [1, 1]^T$ and $\mathbf{c}_2 = [0, 0]^T$, common width $\sigma = 1$, and output weights $w_1 = 0.6$, $w_2 = 0.4$, bias $b_0 = 0$. Compute the output for input $\mathbf{x} = [2, 3]^T$.

**Step 1 — Compute distance to first center.**

$$d_1^2 = (2 - 1)^2 + (3 - 1)^2 = 1^2 + 2^2 = 1 + 4 = 5$$

$$\phi_1(\mathbf{x}) = \exp\left(-\frac{5}{2 \cdot 1^2}\right) = \exp(-2.5) \approx 0.0821$$

**Step 2 — Compute distance to second center.**

$$d_2^2 = (2 - 0)^2 + (3 - 0)^2 = 4 + 9 = 13$$

$$\phi_2(\mathbf{x}) = \exp\left(-\frac{13}{2}\right) = \exp(-6.5) \approx 0.0015$$

**Step 3 — Compute the output.**

$$y(\mathbf{x}) = w_1 \phi_1 + w_2 \phi_2 + b_0 = 0.6 \times 0.0821 + 0.4 \times 0.0015 + 0$$

$$y(\mathbf{x}) = 0.0493 + 0.0006 = 0.0499$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
The output is approximately **0.05**, very close to zero. This is because the input is far from both centers — both Gaussians have decayed almost to zero. The network correctly reflects **low confidence** in this distant region.

---

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: RBFNN vs. MLP (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Feature | RBFNN | MLP |
|---------|-------|-----|
| Hidden activation | Radial (Gaussian) | Sigmoid / ReLU / Tanh |
| Output layer | Linear | Non-linear (usually) |
| Training algorithm | Closed-form (pseudo-inverse) | Iterative (backpropagation) |
| Training speed | Very fast | Slow |
| Local vs. global | Local response | Global response |
| Universal approximation | Yes | Yes |
| Number of hidden neurons | Can be large (up to $N$) | Usually small |
| Curse of dimensionality | More sensitive | Less sensitive |
| Interpretability | High (centers = prototypes) | Low (black box) |
| Best for | Function approximation, control | Classification, large-scale DL |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RBFNN Architecture — Three-Layer Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a Radial Basis Function Neural Network, showing the flow of data from input through hidden activations to the linear output.

```mermaid
graph TD
    INP1["Input X1"] --> SUMH1
    INP2["Input X2"] --> SUMH1
    INPn["Input Xn"] --> SUMH1
    SUMH1["Distance Computation x minus c1"] --> GAU1["Gaussian RBF Phi1"]
    SUMH2["Distance Computation x minus c2"] --> GAU2["Gaussian RBF Phi2"]
    SUMHm["Distance Computation x minus cM"] --> GAUm["Gaussian RBF PhiM"]
    INP1 --> SUMH2
    INP2 --> SUMH2
    INPn --> SUMH2
    INP1 --> SUMHm
    INP2 --> SUMHm
    INPn --> SUMHm
    GAU1 --> W1["Multiply by weight W1"]
    GAU2 --> W2["Multiply by weight W2"]
    GAUm --> WM["Multiply by weight WM"]
    W1 --> ADD["Linear Summation"]
    W2 --> ADD
    WM --> ADD
    BIAS["Bias b0"] --> ADD
    ADD --> OUT["Final Output Yx"]
    CENT1["Center C1"] --> SUMH1
    CENT2["Center C2"] --> SUMH2
    CENTM["Center CM"] --> SUMHm
    SIGMA["Width Sigma"] --> GAU1
    SIGMA --> GAU2
    SIGMA --> GAUm
```

> **Reading Guide for the Diagram:**
> - The **three input nodes** ($X_1, X_2, X_n$) feed into all three distance-computation blocks.
> - Each distance block uses a **center** ($C_1, C_2, C_M$) and the **shared width** $\sigma$ to produce the Gaussian activation $\phi_i$.
> - The activated outputs are **scaled by weights** ($W_1, W_2, W_M$) and summed with bias $b_0$ at the **Linear Summation** node.
> - The final block produces the scalar output $Y(\mathbf{x})$.

---

### 4.2 RBFNN Training Flow — Two-Stage Hybrid Learning

The training of an RBF network proceeds in **two distinct stages**: unsupervised learning of the hidden layer parameters, followed by supervised learning of the output weights.

```mermaid
graph TD
    START["Start Training"] --> S1["Stage 1 Unsupervised"]
    S1 --> S1A["Choose Centers via K Means or Random Sampling"]
    S1A --> S1B["Set Widths Sigma using P Nearest Neighbors"]
    S1B --> S1C["Compute Hidden Activation Matrix Phi"]
    S1C --> S2["Stage 2 Supervised"]
    S2 --> S2A["Form Target Vector T from training outputs"]
    S2A --> S2B["Solve W via Pseudo Inverse Phi plus T"]
    S2B --> S2C["Optional Regularization with lambda"]
    S2C --> EVAL["Evaluate on Test Set"]
    EVAL --> CHECK{"Error acceptable"}
    CHECK -->|"Yes"| END["Stop Training"]
    CHECK -->|"No"| TUNE["Tune Sigma or increase M"]
    TUNE --> S1
```

> **Reading Guide for the Training Flow:**
> - **Stage 1 (Unsupervised):** The centers $\mathbf{c}_i$ and widths $\sigma_i$ are determined using **clustering algorithms** (typically $K$-means) — no target values are needed.
> - **Stage 2 (Supervised):** The output weights $\mathbf{W}$ are computed in a single shot using the **Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse**, given the targets $\mathbf{t}$.
> - If performance is unsatisfactory, the practitioner **iterates** by adjusting $\sigma$ or increasing $M$.

---

### 4.3 Gaussian Activation Response — Visual Intuition

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Input x at distance r from center"] --> B["Compute r squared"]
    B --> C["Divide by 2 sigma squared"]
    C --> D["Negate the ratio"]
    D --> E["Apply exponential function"]
    E --> F["Output Phi r between 0 and 1"]
    F --> G1["If r equals 0 then Phi equals 1 Maximum"]
    F --> G2["If r increases then Phi decreases"]
    F --> G3["If r greater than 3 sigma then Phi is near 0"]
```

> **Interpretation:** The Gaussian RBF neuron outputs a value of **1.0** when the input exactly matches the center, decays smoothly as the input moves away, and approaches **zero** when the input is more than $3\sigma$ away from the center. This is the **locality property** that makes RBFNNs so effective for interpolation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Define Radial Basis Function Neural Network. Mention any two commonly used radial basis functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Radial Basis Function Neural Network (RBFNN) is a three-layered feedforward neural network that uses **radial basis activation functions** in the hidden layer to perform local approximation of an input-output mapping. The hidden layer computes the distance of the input from a set of center vectors, applies a radially symmetric function, and the output layer linearly combines these responses.

Two commonly used radial basis functions are:

1. **Gaussian Function:** $\phi(r) = \exp(-r^2 / 2\sigma^2)$ — most widely used, smooth and localized.
2. **Multiquadric Function:** $\phi(r) = \sqrt{r^2 + \sigma^2}$ — useful for surface interpolation.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two RBFs = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Compare RBF network and Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) in terms of (i) hidden layer activation, (ii) training algorithm, and (iii) nature of response (local vs. global).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | RBF Network | MLP |
|--------|-------------|-----|
| (i) Hidden Activation | Radial basis (e.g., Gaussian) | Sigmoid / ReLU / Tanh |
| (ii) Training Algorithm | Closed-form pseudo-inverse (single shot) | Iterative backpropagation |
| (iii) Response Nature | Local (response decays with distance) | Global (response across entire input space) |

*(1 mark per correct comparison = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Radial Basis Function Neural Network. Derive the mathematical expression for the output of an RBF network with $M$ hidden neurons.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture (3 marks):**

The RBFNN consists of three layers:

1. **Input Layer:** Receives the $n$-dimensional input vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$. It performs no computation.

2. **Hidden Layer:** Contains $M$ neurons. Each neuron $i$ has:
   - A center vector $\mathbf{c}_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
   - A width parameter $\sigma_i > 0$.
   - Activation function (typically Gaussian):
     $$\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \exp\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right)$$

3. **Output Layer:** Computes a linear combination of the hidden activations:
   $$y(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_i \phi_i(\mathbf{x}) + b_0$$

**Derivation of Output Expression (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** For the $i$-th hidden neuron, compute the squared Euclidean distance:

$$d_i^2 = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{n}(x_j - c_{ij})^2$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Gaussian RBF to obtain the activation:

$$\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \exp\left(-\frac{d_i^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right) = \exp\left(-\frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n}(x_j - c_{ij})^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right)$$

**Step 3:** The output layer computes the weighted linear sum:

$$y(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_i \phi_i(\mathbf{x}) + b_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_i \exp\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right) + b_0$$

This is the final output expression of the RBF network.

---

**(b)** Explain the two-stage training procedure of an RBF network. How are the centers and output weights determined? Mention the role of the $K$-means algorithm in determining the centers.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Two-Stage Training (3 marks):**

RBFNN training is divided into two independent stages:

**Stage 1 — Unsupervised Learning of Hidden Layer Parameters:**

The centers $\mathbf{c}_i$ and widths $\sigma_i$ are determined **without** using the target outputs. This makes the process fast and decoupled from the supervised task.

- **Centers** are typically determined using one of:
  - **Random sampling** of $M$ points from the training data.
  - **$K$-means clustering** (most common) — clusters the input data into $M$ groups, and the cluster centroids become the centers $\mathbf{c}_i$.
  - **Orthogonal Least Squares (OLS)** — selects centers that contribute most to the output variance.

- **Widths** $\sigma_i$ are set using the **$P$-nearest neighbor heuristic**:

$$\sigma_i = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{P} \sum_{j=1}^{P} \Vert \mathbf{c}_i - \mathbf{c}_j \Vert$$

where the sum is over the $P$ nearest centers to $\mathbf{c}_i$.

**Stage 2 — Supervised Learning of Output Weights (4 marks):**

The hidden layer produces an $M \times N$ activation matrix $\mathbf{\Phi}$. The output weights are then determined by solving a **linear least-squares problem**:

$$\mathbf{\Phi}^T \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{t}$$

The closed-form solution is:

$$\mathbf{w} = (\mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{\Phi}^T)^{-1} \mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{t}$$

This is a **single-shot computation** — no iteration, no learning rate.

**Role of $K$-means (included in 3 marks above):**

The $K$-means algorithm partitions the $N$ training input vectors into $M$ clusters by iteratively:
1. Assigning each point to the nearest centroid.
2. Updating each centroid as the mean of points assigned to it.
3. Repeating until convergence.

The final centroids serve as the RBF centers $\mathbf{c}_i$. This ensures that the centers are **representative prototypes** of the input distribution, leading to better generalization.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** Discuss the various radial basis functions used in RBF networks. Compare the Gaussian and Multiquadric functions with respect to their locality, smoothness, and typical applications.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Various Radial Basis Functions (4 marks):**

| Function | Formula | Locality | Smoothness |
|----------|---------|----------|------------|
| Gaussian | $\phi(r) = \exp(-r^2/2\sigma^2)$ | Strictly local | Infinitely differentiable |
| Multiquadric | $\phi(r) = \sqrt{r^2 + \sigma^2}$ | Non-local (grows with $r$) | Infinitely differentiable |
| Inverse Multiquadric | $\phi(r) = 1/\sqrt{r^2 + \sigma^2}$ | Local (decays) | Infinitely differentiable |
| Thin Plate Spline | $\phi(r) = r^2 \ln(r)$ | Non-local | Twice differentiable |
| Cubic | $\phi(r) = r^3$ | Non-local (polynomial) | Twice differentiable |

**Comparison: Gaussian vs. Multiquadric (3 marks):**

| Property | Gaussian | Multiquadric |
|----------|----------|--------------|
| Locality | **Strictly local** — decays to zero as $r \to \infty$ | **Non-local** — grows without bound as $r$ increases |
| Smoothness | Infinitely differentiable | Infinitely differentiable |
| Response at $r=0$ | $\phi(0) = 1$ (maximum) | $\phi(0) = \sigma$ (minimum) |
| Symmetry | Symmetric | Symmetric |
| Typical Applications | Function approximation, time-series prediction, control | Surface fitting, terrain modeling, geostatistics |
| Computational cost | Moderate (exponential) | Lower (no exponential) |
| Condition number of $\mathbf{\Phi}$ | Usually well-conditioned | Can be ill-conditioned for large $r$ |

**Conclusion:** Gaussian is preferred for **local approximation** tasks, while Multiquadric excels at **global surface fitting** where the function's growth is itself informative.

---

**(b)** Illustrate the application of RBF networks in **function approximation** and **time-series prediction** with suitable examples. Mention at least three advantages of using RBF networks over traditional polynomial approximation.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Application 1 — Function Approximation (2.5 marks):**

RBF networks are widely used to approximate **unknown continuous functions** from sparse input-output samples. For example, in **robotics**, the inverse dynamics of a robotic manipulator (mapping joint angles, velocities, and accelerations to required torques) is often approximated using an RBF network. The training data consists of measured $(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{t})$ pairs, and the trained network provides **real-time inverse dynamics estimates** for the controller.

Mathematically, given a set of samples $\{(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}, t_k)\}_{k=1}^{N}$, the RBF network learns to approximate the function $f(\mathbf{x})$ such that $f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) \approx t_k$ for all $k$.

**Application 2 — Time-Series Prediction (2.5 marks):**

In **electrical load forecasting**, the goal is to predict the next hour's power demand based on the previous 24 hours' demand values. The RBF network is trained with sliding-window inputs:

$$\mathbf{x}^{(k)} = [y(k-1), y(k-2), \ldots, y(k-24)]^T, \quad t_k = y(k)$$

The trained network then predicts $y(k+1), y(k+2), \ldots$ in a recursive manner. This has been successfully deployed in **smart grid systems** and **financial market prediction**.

**Advantages over Polynomial Approximation (2 marks):**

1. **No curse of dimensionality in lower dimensions:** Polynomials suffer from exponential growth in terms, while RBF networks scale linearly with the number of centers.
2. **Local adaptation:** RBF neurons only contribute in their local region, so adding new training data only affects nearby neurons — no need to retrain the entire network.
3. **No oscillatory behavior (Runge phenomenon):** High-degree polynomials oscillate wildly at the edges of the interpolation interval. RBF networks with Gaussian kernels remain smooth and well-behaved.
4. **Closed-form training:** RBF weights are solved in a single matrix operation, whereas polynomial coefficient fitting also has a closed form but is numerically unstable for high degrees.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the bias term $b_0$:** Many students write $y = \sum w_i \phi_i$ without including the bias. The KTU answer key explicitly awards a mark for the bias — **don't lose it**.
>
> 2. **Confusing the squaring inside the Gaussian:** The correct form is $\exp(-r^2 / 2\sigma^2)$, NOT $\exp(-r / 2\sigma^2)$. The square is on the distance, not on the entire fraction. This is a **2-mark deduction** in most valuation schemes.
>
> 3. **Writing pseudo-inverse incorrectly:** The formula is $\mathbf{W} = (\mathbf{\Phi}\mathbf{\Phi}^T)^{-1}\mathbf{\Phi}\mathbf{t}$, not $\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{\Phi}^{-1}\mathbf{t}$. The matrix $\mathbf{\Phi}$ is **not square** in general — you must use the pseudo-inverse.
>
> 4. **Confusing RBF centers with weights:** Centers $\mathbf{c}_i$ are determined **unsupervised** (via $K$-means). Weights $w_i$ are determined **supervised** (via pseudo-inverse). Mixing these up is a classic error.
>
> 5. **Not specifying the dimensionality of layers:** Always mention "$n$ input neurons, $M$ hidden neurons, $p$ output neurons" explicitly. Vague answers like "input layer, hidden layer, output layer" without dimensions are penalized.
>
> 6. **Omitting the role of $\sigma$:** When asked about the effect of width, students often say "controls the spread" without quantifying. Always say: **"$\sigma$ controls the locality — small $\sigma$ gives sharp, localized response; large $\sigma$ gives broad, smooth response."**
>
> 7. **Forgetting units / ranges of $\sigma$:** If asked for typical values, mention $\sigma \in [0.1, 3.0]$ empirically, or $\sigma = d_{\max} / \sqrt{2M}$ as a heuristic.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read This the Night Before the Exam!**

- **Definition:** RBFNN is a **three-layer feedforward network** with **radial** (distance-based) hidden activations and a **linear** output layer.
- **Architecture:** Input layer (dimension $n$) → Hidden layer with $M$ radial basis neurons → Output layer (linear).
- **Most common RBF:** **Gaussian**: $\phi(r) = \exp(-r^2 / 2\sigma^2)$.
- **Output equation:** $y(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_i \phi_i(\mathbf{x}) + b_0$.
- **Two-stage training:**
  - **Stage 1 (Unsupervised):** Determine centers $\mathbf{c}_i$ via $K$-means or random sampling; determine widths $\sigma_i$ via $P$-nearest neighbor heuristic.
  - **Stage 2 (Supervised):** Solve for weights $\mathbf{w}$ using the pseudo-inverse: $\mathbf{w} = (\mathbf{\Phi}\mathbf{\Phi}^T)^{-1} \mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{t}$.
- **Key advantage:** Training is a **closed-form, single-shot** computation — no iteration, no learning rate.
- **Locality property:** The Gaussian response is **maximum (1.0) at the center** and **decays to near zero beyond $3\sigma$**.
- **Universal approximation:** RBFNNs with sufficient hidden neurons can approximate **any continuous function** on a compact domain to arbitrary accuracy.
- **Cover's theorem:** Non-linear transformation to a higher-dimensional hidden space increases the probability of **linear separability** of patterns.
- **Width effect:**
  - **Small $\sigma$** → sharp, localized, possibly overfitting.
  - **Large $\sigma$** → broad, smooth, possibly underfitting.
- **Bias term $b_0$** shifts the output and is **mandatory** in the formula.
- **Common RBF alternatives:** Multiquadric (non-local, good for surface fitting), Inverse Multiquadric (local, similar to Gaussian), Thin Plate Spline (used in 2D interpolation).
- **Applications:** Function approximation, time-series forecasting, control systems, signal processing, medical diagnosis, image restoration, geostatistics.
- **Comparison with MLP:** RBFNN has **local response** and **fast training**; MLP has **global response** and **iterative training**. RBFNN is preferred for **small-to-medium datasets** with smooth underlying functions.
- **Regularization:** Adding $\lambda \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ to the error function prevents overfitting when $M$ is large.
- **Notation caution:** $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ is Euclidean norm (vector magnitude), not absolute value $\vert \cdot \vert$.
- **Exam mantra:** "Radial = local, Gaussian = most common, pseudo-inverse = single shot, $K$-means = centers."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Training and Regularisation Theory: Comparison with Multilayer Perceptron
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT635 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Network Training and Regularisation Theory: Comparison with Multilayer Perceptron

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Radial Basis Function (RBF) Networks** are a class of **three-layer feedforward neural networks** that use radially symmetric activation functions in the hidden layer. They are mathematically grounded in **regularization theory** for solving ill-posed problems, where the network is constructed to minimize a cost functional that combines an empirical (data-fitting) risk term and a regularization (smoothness) term.

The **Regularization Theory** framework (Tikhonov, 1963) provides the theoretical foundation for RBF networks. It states that the unknown function $f(\mathbf{x})$ mapping input $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ to output $y \in \mathbb{R}$ can be approximated by minimizing:

$$
H[f] = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - f(\mathbf{x}_i))^2 + \lambda \Vert P f \Vert^2
$$

where $\lambda > 0$ is the **regularization parameter**, $P$ is a **differential operator** (smoothness constraint), and the second term penalizes rough (non-smooth) solutions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** RBF networks are unique because they provide a direct link between **function approximation theory** and **neural network architecture**. The output weights are linear in the network parameters, making training analytically tractable.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The "Specialist Committee" Analogy:**
Imagine you need to estimate a complex curve from scattered data points:
- A **Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)** works like a **generalist team** — every neuron in every layer learns from every part of the data globally. Training requires complex backpropagation through all layers simultaneously.
- An **RBF Network** works like a **panel of local specialists** — each hidden neuron is a "local expert" that responds only to inputs in its specific region (like a Gaussian "bump" centered at a particular point). The closer the input is to a center, the more that specialist "votes."

**The "Bell Curve Specialists" picture:**
Think of a hidden RBF neuron as a bell-shaped "spotlight" on the input space. When a data point enters this spotlight's radius, the neuron activates strongly. The output neuron then takes a **weighted sum** of all specialist votes to produce the final answer. The "spotlight" is mathematically a Gaussian kernel with a **center** $\mathbf{c}_i$ and a **width** $\sigma_i$ — two key hyperparameters per hidden neuron.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from MLP:** In MLP, hidden neurons use **sigmoidal** (global, saturating) activations and learn distributed representations. In RBF, hidden neurons use **localized radial** activations and act as receptive field detectors.

### 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **Number of hidden neurons** $M$: typically $M \leq N$ (number of training samples), but $M \ll N$ is preferred for generalization.
- **Center locations** $\mathbf{c}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$: chosen from training data or via clustering.
- **Width/spread parameter** $\sigma_i$: usually set via heuristic $\sigma = \frac{d_{\max}}{\sqrt{2M}}$ where $d_{\max}$ is the maximum distance between centers.
- **Regularization parameter** $\lambda$: balances data fit vs. smoothness, typically in range $[10^{-6}, 1]$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 RBF Network Architecture — Layered Analysis

The RBF network has a strict **three-layer structure**:

**Layer 1 — Input Layer (Source nodes):** Passes input vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$ unchanged to the hidden layer. No computation, no weights.

**Layer 2 — Hidden Layer (RBFs):** Each of the $M$ hidden neurons computes a radial basis function of the form:

$$
\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = G(\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert, \sigma_i)
$$

where $\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert$ is the **Euclidean distance** between the input and the $i$-th center. The most common choice is the **Gaussian kernel**:

$$
\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \exp\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right)
$$

**Layer 3 — Output Layer (Linear combiner):** Produces a scalar output (for regression):

$$
y(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_i \, \phi_i(\mathbf{x}) + w_0
$$

where $w_i$ are the output weights and $w_0$ is a bias term.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Component

- **Why Gaussian?** It satisfies the **Micchelli theorem** (1986) — for any set of distinct centers $\mathbf{c}_i$ and any choice of $\sigma_i > 0$, the hidden-to-output weight matrix $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ is **nonsingular**. This guarantees a unique solution to the linear output-layer problem.
- **Why linear output?** Because the output is a **linear combination** of the basis functions $\phi_i$, the unknown weights can be found by solving a **linear system of equations** — no iterative gradient descent needed for the output layer!
- **How does the network learn?** The training is split into two decoupled sub-problems:
  1. **Unsupervised stage** — determine centers $\mathbf{c}_i$ and widths $\sigma_i$ (no target outputs needed).
  2. **Supervised stage** — determine output weights $w_i$ (targets required, but solved as a linear least-squares problem).

### 2.3 Training Algorithms — Three KTU-Emphasized Approaches

#### (a) Fixed Centers Selected at Random
The $M$ centers are chosen randomly from the $N$ training inputs. Widths set by heuristic. Output weights solved by:

$$
\mathbf{w} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{+} \mathbf{y}
$$

where $\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{+}$ is the **Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse** of the $N \times M$ interpolation matrix with entries $\Phi_{ji} = \phi_i(\mathbf{x}_j)$.

#### (b) Self-Organized Selection of Centers
- Run **K-means clustering** on the training inputs to obtain $M$ cluster centers $\mathbf{c}_i$.
- Set widths via P-nearest neighbor heuristic: $\sigma_i = \frac{1}{P}\sum_{j=1}^{P} \Vert \mathbf{c}_i - \mathbf{c}_j \Vert$ (average distance to $P$ nearest cluster centers).
- Solve output weights by **regularized least squares**:

$$
\mathbf{w} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T \boldsymbol{\Phi} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} \boldsymbol{\Phi}^T \mathbf{y}
$$

#### (c) Supervised Selection of All Parameters
All parameters $\{\mathbf{c}_i, \sigma_i, w_i\}$ are optimized via **gradient descent** (backpropagation-style). Cost function:

$$
E = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{N} e_j^2, \quad e_j = y_j^d - y(\mathbf{x}_j)
$$

### 2.4 Regularization Theory — The Rigorous Foundation

The **regularized cost functional** to be minimized is:

$$
H[f] = H_{\text{emp}}[f] + \lambda H_{\text{reg}}[f] = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (d_i - f(\mathbf{x}_i))^2 + \lambda \int \frac{\vert P f(\mathbf{x}) \vert^2}{G(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}')} \, d\mathbf{x}
$$

The solution $f^*$ is a **linear combination of Green's functions** of the operator $P^*P$ (where $P^*$ is the adjoint of $P$):

$$
f^*(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \, G(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_i)
$$

This is exactly the form of an RBF network with **centers at training points** and **activation = Green's function** $G$.

### 2.5 Generalized RBF Networks (Poggio & Girosi, 1990)

The **Generalized Radial Basis Function (GRBF)** network extends the regularized solution by allowing the network to have **fewer centers than data points**:

$$
f^*(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_i \, G(\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{t}_i \Vert) + p(\mathbf{x})
$$

where $p(\mathbf{x})$ is a low-order polynomial (e.g., linear) to ensure that the regularization operator $P$ is well-defined (null space compensation). The centers $\mathbf{t}_i$ are typically a subset of training points.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Key Parameters |
|---|---------|----------|----------------|
| 1 | Gaussian RBF | $\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \exp\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right)$ | center $\mathbf{c}_i$, width $\sigma_i$ |
| 2 | RBF Network Output | $y(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_i \phi_i(\mathbf{x}) + w_0$ | weights $w_i$, bias $w_0$ |
| 3 | Tikhonov Cost Functional | $H[f] = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (d_i - f(\mathbf{x}_i))^2 + \lambda \Vert P f \Vert^2$ | $\lambda > 0$, operator $P$ |
| 4 | Regularized Linear Solution | $\mathbf{w} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T \boldsymbol{\Phi} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} \boldsymbol{\Phi}^T \mathbf{d}$ | pseudoinverse with Tikhonov damping |
| 5 | Output Weights (no bias) | $\mathbf{w} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{+} \mathbf{d}$ | $\boldsymbol{\Phi} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times M}$ |
| 6 | K-means Objective | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{M} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^2$ | minimizes within-cluster variance |
| 7 | P-NN Width Heuristic | $\sigma_i = \frac{1}{P}\sum_{j=1}^{P} \Vert \mathbf{c}_i - \mathbf{c}_{i,j} \Vert$ | $P$ nearest cluster centers |
| 8 | Maximum Width Heuristic | $\sigma = \frac{d_{\max}}{\sqrt{2M}}$ | $d_{\max} = \max_{i,j} \Vert \mathbf{c}_i - \mathbf{c}_j \Vert$ |
| 9 | Green's Function Identity | $f^*(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i G(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_i)$ | $G$ satisfies $P^*P \, G = \delta$ |
| 10 | Condition Number Bound | $\text{cond}(\boldsymbol{\Phi}) \leq \frac{(M!)^{2/N}}{N^M}$ | Micchelli's bound for Gaussian RBFs |
| 11 | GRBF Output | $f^*(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_i G(\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{t}_i \Vert) + p(\mathbf{x})$ | $p \in \mathcal{N}(P)$ (null space) |
| 12 | Effective Rank | $\rho = \text{rank}(\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi} + \lambda \mathbf{I})$ | regularization ensures $\rho = M$ |
| 13 | Error vs. Regularization | $E_{\text{gen}} = E_{\text{emp}} + \lambda \Omega[f]$ | bias-variance decomposition |
| 14 | Multiquadric RBF | $\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \sqrt{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^2 + \sigma_i^2}$ | globally supported, conditional pos. def. |
| 15 | Inverse Multiquadric | $\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^2 + \sigma_i^2}}$ | strictly positive definite |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Function approximation** in control systems where closed-form input-output mappings are needed.
- **Time-series prediction** in financial engineering (chaotic series, stock prices).
- **Channel equalization** in communication systems (adaptive RBF equalizers).
- **Sensor calibration** and inverse modeling in process control.
- **Interpolation of scattered data** in computer-aided geometric design (CAGD) — direct use of regularization theory.
- **Medical signal processing**: ECG denoising, EEG feature extraction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Regularization Theory to the RBF Network

We start with the **Tikhonov regularization problem**: find a function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ that minimizes

$$
H[f] = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (d_i - f(\mathbf{x}_i))^2 + \lambda \int_{\mathbb{R}^n} \frac{\vert P f(\mathbf{x}) \vert^2}{G(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}')} d\mathbf{x} \, d\mathbf{x}'
$$

**Step 1 — Stationarity Condition.** Set the Fréchet derivative $\frac{\delta H}{\delta f} = 0$:

$$
-2\sum_{i=1}^{N} (d_i - f(\mathbf{x}_i)) \delta(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i) + 2\lambda \, P^*P f(\mathbf{x}) = 0
$$

where $P^*$ is the **adjoint operator** of $P$ and $\delta(\cdot)$ is the Dirac delta. Rearranging:

$$
P^*P f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (d_i - f(\mathbf{x}_i)) \delta(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i)
$$

**Step 2 — Green's Function Representation.** The linear operator $P^*P$ has an inverse given by its Green's function $G(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}')$ satisfying $P^*P \, G(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \delta(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}')$. Therefore the formal solution is:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (d_i - f(\mathbf{x}_i)) G(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_i)
$$

This means the solution is a **linear combination of Green's functions** centered at the data points.

**Step 3 — Define Weights.** Let $w_i = \frac{1}{\lambda}(d_i - f(\mathbf{x}_i))$. Then:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i G(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_i)
$$

**Step 4 — Constraint at Data Points.** Substitute $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x}_j$ for each $j = 1, \ldots, N$:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}_j) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i G(\mathbf{x}_j, \mathbf{x}_i) = d_j - \lambda w_j
$$

Rearranging:

$$
d_j = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i G(\mathbf{x}_j, \mathbf{x}_i) + \lambda w_j
$$

**Step 5 — Matrix Form.** In matrix notation with $\mathbf{G}_{ji} = G(\mathbf{x}_j, \mathbf{x}_i)$ and $\mathbf{I}$ the identity:

$$
\mathbf{d} = (\mathbf{G} + \lambda \mathbf{I})\mathbf{w}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for Weights:**

$$
\mathbf{w} = (\mathbf{G} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{d}
$$

**Conclusion:** The regularized solution is an RBF network with $N$ hidden neurons, centers at training data, and Green's function activation. The matrix $(\mathbf{G} + \lambda \mathbf{I})$ is guaranteed to be **nonsingular** for $\lambda > 0$, even if $\mathbf{G}$ alone is singular.

### 3.2 Derivation: Connection to Pseudoinverse Solution (As $\lambda \to 0$)

When $\lambda \to 0$, the regularization term vanishes, and we recover the **pure interpolation** condition. Using the **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)** of $\mathbf{G} = \mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^T$:

$$
\mathbf{G} + \lambda \mathbf{I} = \mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^T + \lambda \mathbf{I}
$$

The inverse is:

$$
(\mathbf{G} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} = \mathbf{V}(\boldsymbol{\Sigma} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{U}^T = \mathbf{V}\text{diag}\left(\frac{1}{\sigma_k + \lambda}\right)\mathbf{U}^T
$$

In the limit $\lambda \to 0^+$, this becomes the **Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse**:

$$
\lim_{\lambda \to 0} (\mathbf{G} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} = \mathbf{G}^{+} = \mathbf{V}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{+}\mathbf{U}^T
$$

where $\Sigma^+_{kk} = 1/\sigma_k$ if $\sigma_k > 0$, else $0$. This is exactly the **minimum-norm least-squares solution** to the interpolation problem.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: RBF Training Stages

| Training Stage | What is learned? | Method | Computational Cost |
|----------------|------------------|--------|--------------------|
| Stage 1 (Unsupervised) | Centers $\mathbf{c}_i$ | Random selection / K-means / Clustering | $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot M \cdot I)$ for $I$ K-means iterations |
| Stage 1 (Unsupervised) | Widths $\sigma_i$ | Heuristic (P-NN, max distance) | $\mathcal{O}(M^2)$ |
| Stage 2 (Supervised) | Output weights $\mathbf{w}$ | Linear least squares / Pseudoinverse | $\mathcal{O}(N M^2)$ for Cholesky or $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ for direct |
| Stage 2 (Supervised, regularized) | Output weights $\mathbf{w}$ | Tikhonov-damped linear system | $\mathcal{O}(N M^2 + M^3)$ |
| Stage 3 (Full supervised, optional) | All parameters $\{\mathbf{c}_i, \sigma_i, w_i\}$ | Gradient descent / Backprop | $\mathcal{O}(N M n \cdot T)$ for $T$ epochs |

### 3.4 Worked Example: 1-D Function Approximation

**Problem:** Approximate $f(x) = \sin(2\pi x)$ for $x \in [0,1]$ using an RBF network with $M = 4$ Gaussian hidden units and $N = 20$ training points. Use K-means-style centers at $c_1 = 0.10, c_2 = 0.35, c_3 = 0.65, c_4 = 0.90$ with common width $\sigma = 0.20$ determined by $\sigma = d_{\max}/\sqrt{2M}$ where $d_{\max} = 0.80$.

**Step 1 — Compute Maximum Distance.** $d_{\max} = \max_{i,j} \vert c_i - c_j \vert = 0.80$ (from $c_1 = 0.10$ to $c_4 = 0.90$).

**Step 2 — Compute Width.**

$$
\sigma = \frac{d_{\max}}{\sqrt{2M}} = \frac{0.80}{\sqrt{8}} = \frac{0.80}{2.828} \approx 0.283
$$

**Step 3 — Form Interpolation Matrix $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ (sample row at $x_j = 0.50$):**

$$
\Phi_{ji} = \exp\left(-\frac{(0.50 - c_i)^2}{2 \cdot 0.283^2}\right) = \exp\left(-\frac{(0.50 - c_i)^2}{0.160}\right)
$$

- $\Phi_{j,1} = \exp(-(0.40)^2/0.160) = \exp(-1.0) = 0.368$
- $\Phi_{j,2} = \exp(-(0.15)^2/0.160) = \exp(-0.141) = 0.869$
- $\Phi_{j,3} = \exp(-(0.15)^2/0.160) = \exp(-0.141) = 0.869$
- $\Phi_{j,4} = \exp(-(0.40)^2/0.160) = \exp(-1.0) = 0.368$

**Step 4 — Stack all $N = 20$ rows** to form $\boldsymbol{\Phi} \in \mathbb{R}^{20 \times 4}$, then compute the regularized output weights:

$$
\mathbf{w} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi} + 10^{-3}\mathbf{I}_4)^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T \mathbf{d}
$$

where $\mathbf{d} = [\sin(2\pi x_1), \ldots, \sin(2\pi x_{20})]^T$ are the targets.

**Step 5 — Approximation.** The network output is:

$$
y(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i \exp\left(-\frac{(x - c_i)^2}{0.160}\right)
$$

This gives a smooth, regularized fit to the sine function with very few parameters (only 4 weights!).

### 3.5 Comparison with Multilayer Perceptron — Step-by-Step

| Property | RBF Network | Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) |
|----------|-------------|------------------------------|
| **Architecture** | 3 layers: input–RBF hidden–linear output | 2+ hidden layers with sigmoidal activations |
| **Hidden activation** | Localized (Gaussian, multiquadric) | Global saturating (logistic, tanh) |
| **Output activation** | Linear (pure) | Linear or softmax |
| **Output layer training** | **Linear problem** → exact solution | Nonlinear → backpropagation required |
| **Training algorithm** | Hybrid (K-means + pseudoinverse) or full gradient | Backpropagation, Levenberg–Marquardt, etc. |
| **No. of hidden neurons** | Often $M \gg$ MLP's hidden count | Usually small (geometric mean) |
| **Convergence** | Fast, no local minima for output weights | Slow, plagued by local minima |
| **Generalization** | Controlled by $\lambda$ (regularization) | Controlled by early stopping, dropout |
| **Theoretical basis** | Regularization theory (Tikhonov) | Universal approximation (Cybenko 1989) |
| **Function representation** | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_i w_i G(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_i)$ | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_i w_i \sigma(\mathbf{W}_i \mathbf{x})$ |
| **Input dimension scaling** | Curse of dimensionality affects center placement | Less sensitive; distributed representations |
| **Interpretability** | Each RBF = local "bump" detector | Distributed, hard to interpret |
| **Construction vs. learning** | Constructive (RBF ≈ Green's function) | Purely data-driven |
| **Parameters in hidden layer** | $\mathbf{c}_i, \sigma_i$ (nonlinear in input) | $\mathbf{W}_i, \mathbf{b}_i$ (linear in input) |
