## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and explain Tikhonov regularization theory as applied to the design of Radial Basis Function networks.**

**Model Answer:**

Tikhonov regularization theory provides a principled mathematical framework for solving ill-posed inverse problems, which directly leads to the RBF network architecture.

The regularized cost functional to be minimized is:

$$
H[f] = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (d_i - f(\mathbf{x}_i))^2 + \lambda \Vert P f \Vert^2
$$

The first term is the **empirical risk** (data-fitting error), and the second is the **regularization term** with $\lambda > 0$ as the regularization parameter and $P$ a stabilizer operator measuring the smoothness of $f$.

Setting the Fréchet derivative to zero and using Green's function $G$ of the operator $P^*P$, the solution becomes:

$$
f^*(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i G(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_i)
$$

This is mathematically identical to an RBF network with $N$ hidden neurons, centers at training data, and the activation function being the Green's function $G$. The output weights $\mathbf{w}$ are obtained by solving the regularized linear system $(\mathbf{G} + \lambda \mathbf{I})\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{d}$. The parameter $\lambda$ controls the **bias-variance tradeoff**: small $\lambda$ leads to overfitting, large $\lambda$ leads to underfitting.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Compare the architectural and training differences between RBF networks and Multilayer Perceptrons (MLP).**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | RBF Network | MLP |
|--------|-------------|-----|
| Hidden activation | Localized (Gaussian), radial | Global (sigmoidal), saturating |
| Output activation | Linear | Linear or softmax |
| Training of output weights | Linear least-squares (single-step) | Iterative backpropagation |
| Convergence | No local minima for output layer | Plagued by local minima |
| Interpretation | Each neuron is a local "specialist" | Distributed representation |
| Theoretical basis | Regularization theory (Tikhonov) | Universal approximation (Cybenko) |
| Typical use | Function approximation, interpolation | Classification, regression with large data |

The key advantage of the RBF network is that the output weights are **linear in the network outputs**, allowing a closed-form solution via the pseudoinverse or regularized least-squares, while MLP requires iterative gradient-based training of all layers.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the solution of the regularized RBF network using Tikhonov regularization theory. Show all steps from the cost functional to the final form of the network output and the linear system for the output weights. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the three training strategies for RBF networks: random center selection, self-organized center selection (K-means), and fully supervised training. Mention the advantages and limitations of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Set up the Tikhonov functional:**

$$
H[f] = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (d_i - f(\mathbf{x}_i))^2 + \lambda \int \frac{\vert P f(\mathbf{x}) \vert^2}{G(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}')} d\mathbf{x} \, d\mathbf{x}'
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Fréchet derivative** and set it to zero:

$$
\frac{\delta H}{\delta f} = 0 \implies -2\sum_{i=1}^{N} (d_i - f(\mathbf{x}_i))\delta(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i) + 2\lambda P^*P f(\mathbf{x}) = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to obtain the differential equation:**

$$
P^*P f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (d_i - f(\mathbf{x}_i))\delta(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve using Green's function** $G$ of $P^*P$:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (d_i - f(\mathbf{x}_i)) G(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i G(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_i)
$$

where $w_i = (d_i - f(\mathbf{x}_i))/\lambda$.

**Step 5 — Apply the interpolation constraint** at each $\mathbf{x}_j$:

$$
d_j = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i G(\mathbf{x}_j, \mathbf{x}_i) + \lambda w_j
$$

**Step 6 — Matrix form and solution:**

$$
\mathbf{d} = (\mathbf{G} + \lambda \mathbf{I})\mathbf{w} \implies \boxed{\mathbf{w} = (\mathbf{G} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{d}}
$$

**Final network output:**

$$
f^*(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i G(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_i)
$$

This is an RBF network with $N$ hidden neurons, centers at training points, Green's function activations, and output weights computed via the regularized linear system.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Strategy 1 — Random Center Selection:**
- $M$ centers $\mathbf{c}_i$ are chosen uniformly at random from the $N$ training inputs.
- Widths $\sigma_i$ set by a global heuristic (e.g., $\sigma = d_{\max}/\sqrt{2M}$).
- Output weights solved by $\mathbf{w} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{+}\mathbf{d}$.
- **Advantage:** Extremely simple, no clustering cost.
- **Limitation:** Random centers may leave "holes" in input space; poor coverage for non-uniform data distributions.

**Strategy 2 — Self-Organized Selection (K-means + P-NN):**
- **Stage 1:** Run K-means clustering on training inputs to obtain $M$ cluster centers $\mathbf{c}_i$. K-means minimizes $J = \sum_{i=1}^{M}\sum_{\mathbf{x}\in C_i}\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i\Vert^2$.
- **Stage 2:** Compute widths using the P-nearest neighbor heuristic: $\sigma_i = \frac{1}{P}\sum_{j=1}^{P}\Vert \mathbf{c}_i - \mathbf{c}_{i,j}\Vert$ (average distance to the $P$ nearest cluster centers).
- **Stage 3:** Solve $\mathbf{w} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\boldsymbol{\Phi} + \lambda\mathbf{I})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T\mathbf{d}$.
- **Advantage:** Centers adapt to data density; better coverage than random.
- **Limitation:** Two-stage (decoupled) optimization is not globally optimal; K-means can converge to local minima.

**Strategy 3 — Fully Supervised Training:**
- All parameters $\{\mathbf{c}_i, \sigma_i, w_i\}$ are optimized simultaneously by **gradient descent** to minimize $E = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{N}(d_j - y(\mathbf{x}_j))^2$.
- Update rules (e.g., for centers): $\Delta \mathbf{c}_i = -\eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial \mathbf{c}_i}$.
- **Advantage:** Globally optimal (in principle) since all parameters are tuned jointly.
- **Limitation:** Slow convergence; many local minima; no analytical solution for the linear part anymore.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently confuse **regularization parameter $\lambda$** with the **kernel width $\sigma$** in RBF networks. They play completely different roles: $\sigma$ controls the *locality* of each hidden unit's response, while $\lambda$ controls the *smoothness* of the fitted function (Tikhonov damping). Conflating them costs full marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the three-layer architecture of an RBF network in detail, including the role of each layer, the Gaussian activation function, and the formulation of the network output. Discuss the significance of the Micchelli theorem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable equations, describe the **Generalized Radial Basis Function (GRBF)** network proposed by Poggio and Girosi. How does it differ from the regularized RBF network? Discuss its advantages in terms of network complexity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The RBF network has a strict three-layer architecture:

**Layer 1 — Input Layer (Source nodes):** It simply distributes the input vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$ to the hidden layer. There are no weights and no computation.

**Layer 2 — Hidden Layer (Radial Basis Functions):** Contains $M$ neurons. Each neuron $i$ has an associated center $\mathbf{c}_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and computes a radially symmetric function of the distance between the input and the center:

$$
\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = G(\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert, \sigma_i)
$$

The most common choice is the **Gaussian**:

$$
\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \exp\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right)
$$

The response is maximum (equal to 1) at $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{c}_i$ and decays radially as the Euclidean distance increases. The parameter $\sigma_i$ controls the "width" of the bell curve.

**Layer 3 — Output Layer (Linear combiner):** Computes a weighted linear combination of hidden activations:

$$
y(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_i \phi_i(\mathbf{x}) + w_0
$$

The bias $w_0$ accounts for the DC offset. This is a **linear** function of the output weights, which is the central property exploited in training.

**Significance of Micchelli's Theorem (1986):**
The theorem states that for any set of $N$ distinct data points $\{\mathbf{x}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_N\} \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ and any set of $M$ distinct centers $\{\mathbf{c}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{c}_M\}$, the interpolation matrix $\boldsymbol{\Phi} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times M}$ with entries $\Phi_{ji} = \exp(-\Vert \mathbf{x}_j - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^2 / 2\sigma_i^2)$ is **nonsingular** provided $\sigma_i > 0$ for all $i$.

This guarantees that:
- The linear system $\boldsymbol{\Phi}\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{d}$ has a **unique solution**.
- Exact interpolation of all $N$ training points is possible with $M = N$ hidden units.
- The pseudoinverse $\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{+}$ is well-defined and stable.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

The **Generalized RBF (GRBF)** network was proposed by Poggio and Girosi (1990) as a practical extension of the regularized RBF network. Its output is:

$$
f^*(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_i G(\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{t}_i \Vert) + p(\mathbf{x})
$$

where:
- $M \leq N$ is the number of centers (often $M \ll N$).
- $\mathbf{t}_i$ are centers chosen as a **subset** of training points (not all $N$).
- $G$ is the Green's function of the regularization operator.
- $p(\mathbf{x})$ is a **polynomial** of low degree, typically linear: $p(\mathbf{x}) = b_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{n} b_k x_k$. This polynomial lies in the **null space** of $P$ (i.e., $Pp = 0$), which is required so that the regularization functional is well-defined (the operator $P^*P$ is not invertible on its null space).

**Differences from the regularized RBF network:**

| Aspect | Regularized RBF | GRBF |
|--------|-----------------|------|
| Number of centers | $M = N$ (one per data point) | $M \ll N$ (subset) |
| Polynomial term $p(\mathbf{x})$ | Absent | Present (null-space compensation) |
| Computational cost | $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ for weight solve | $\mathcal{O}(M^3)$, $M \ll N$ |
| Storage of $\mathbf{G}$ matrix | $N \times N$ | $M \times M$ |
| Generalization | Risk of overfitting with large $N$ | Better controlled by $M$ and $\lambda$ |

**Advantages of GRBF:**
1. **Reduced complexity** — fewer centers $\Rightarrow$ fewer weights $\Rightarrow$ smaller network, less overfitting.
2. **Faster training** — matrix inversion scales cubically with $M$ instead of $N$.
3. **Better generalization** — null-space polynomial captures linear trends; RBFs capture local nonlinearities.
4. **Flexibility** — center locations $\mathbf{t}_i$ can be optimized via gradient descent for further accuracy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** When explaining the GRBF network, students often forget the **null-space term** $p(\mathbf{x})$ and present the GRBF as identical to the standard RBF. The examiner will deduct at least 2 marks if $p(\mathbf{x})$ and its role (capturing the polynomial part of the function that lies in the null space of $P$) are not mentioned.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2):**

- **RBF Network = 3-layer architecture:** Input → Radial Hidden (Gaussian) → Linear Output. No more, no fewer layers in the classical form.
- **Activation function is local & radial:** $\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \exp(-\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^2 / 2\sigma_i^2)$ — maximum at center, decays with distance.
- **Output is linear in weights:** $y = \sum w_i \phi_i$ enables closed-form training via pseudoinverse.
- **Tikhonov functional:** $H[f] = \sum (d_i - f(\mathbf{x}_i))^2 + \lambda \Vert Pf \Vert^2$ — combines data fidelity + smoothness.
- **Solution from Euler-Lagrange:** $f^*(\mathbf{x}) = \sum w_i G(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_i)$ where $G$ is the Green's function of $P^*P$.
- **Final weight formula:** $\mathbf{w} = (\mathbf{G} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{d}$ — Tikhonov-regularized linear system.
- **Micchelli's theorem:** Gaussian $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ matrix is nonsingular for distinct centers → unique solution exists.
- **Three training strategies:** Random centers, K-means + P-NN widths, fully supervised (gradient descent on all parameters).
- **P-NN width heuristic:** $\sigma_i = \frac{1}{P}\sum_{j=1}^{P}\Vert \mathbf{c}_i - \mathbf{c}_{i,j}\Vert$ (average to $P$ nearest centers).
- **Max-distance width:** $\sigma = d_{\max}/\sqrt{2M}$ where $d_{\max}$ is the largest inter-center distance.
- **GRBF network (Poggio & Girosi):** $f^*(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_i G(\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{t}_i\Vert) + p(\mathbf{x})$, with $M \ll N$ and polynomial null-space term.
- **Null-space condition:** $Pp = 0$ — ensures the regularizer is well-defined.
- **RBF vs MLP — KEY differences:**
  - RBF: localized activations, linear output, fast training, regularization-theory grounded.
  - MLP: global activations, nonlinear everywhere, slow training (backprop), universal approximation theorem.
- **Pseudoinverse limit:** $\lim_{\lambda \to 0}(\mathbf{G} + \lambda\mathbf{I})^{-1} = \mathbf{G}^+$ — regularization → pseudoinverse (interpolation).
- **Common RBFs:** Gaussian, multiquadric, inverse multiquadric, thin-plate spline (Green's function of the biharmonic operator).
- **Applications:** Function approximation, time-series prediction, channel equalization, sensor calibration, scattered data interpolation, signal denoising.
- **Computational costs:** Center selection (K-means) $\mathcal{O}(NMI)$; weight solve $\mathcal{O}(NM^2 + M^3)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Fuzzy Logic: Introduction to Fuzzy Sets - Properties, Operations, and Relations; Membership Functions and Fuzzy Rules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT635 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basics of Fuzzy Logic: Introduction to Fuzzy Sets - Properties, Operations, and Relations; Membership Functions and Fuzzy Rules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Fuzzy Set?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuzzy Set (KTU 2024 Definition):** A fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ in a universe of discourse $X$ is a collection of ordered pairs, where each element $x \in X$ is assigned a **membership grade** $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ that indicates the *degree* to which $x$ belongs to $\tilde{A}$. Formally:
>
> $$\tilde{A} = \{\, (x,\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)) \ \vert\ x \in X \,\}$$
>
> where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}: X \rightarrow [0,1]$ is the **membership function** and $1$ denotes the highest degree of belonging, $0$ the lowest.

### Crisp Set vs. Fuzzy Set — The Paradigm Shift

| Feature | Crisp (Classical) Set | Fuzzy Set |
|---|---|---|
| Boundary | Sharply defined | Smooth, gradual |
| Membership | Binary ($0$ or $1$) | Continuous ($\in [0,1]$) |
| Logic | Aristotelian ($A \lor \lnot A$) | Multi-valued |
| Representation | $A = \{x \,\vert\, P(x)\}$ | $\tilde{A} = \{(x,\mu(x))\}$ |
| Real-world fit | Poor for ambiguity | Excellent for vagueness |

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Hot Coffee" Problem

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a cup of coffee. In a **crisp** world, coffee is either *Hot* (yes) or *Not Hot* (no) at a fixed threshold, say $80^\circ$C. So a coffee at $79.9^\circ$C is *Not Hot*, and at $80.1^\circ$C it is *Hot* — an absurd cliff-edge.
> In a **fuzzy** world, the same coffee is mapped via a membership function so that it may be $0.3$ *Hot* at $60^\circ$C, $0.7$ *Hot* at $75^\circ$C, and $1.0$ *Hot* at $90^\circ$C. The transition is **smooth and human-like**. This is the essence of fuzzy sets — they model *gradualness* rather than *absoluteness*.

## 1.2 Membership Function — The Heart of Fuzziness

A **membership function (MF)** assigns a value in $[0,1]$ to every point in the universe of discourse.

- $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1 \Rightarrow x$ **fully belongs** to $\tilde{A}$
- $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 0 \Rightarrow x$ **does not belong** to $\tilde{A}$
- $0 < \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) < 1 \Rightarrow x$ **partially belongs** to $\tilde{A}$

## 1.3 Fuzzy Rules — Linguistic Knowledge Representation

A **fuzzy rule** is a conditional *IF-THEN* statement where antecedents and consequents are expressed using linguistic variables and fuzzy sets.

> **General Form:**
> $$\text{IF } x \text{ is } \tilde{A} \text{ AND } y \text{ is } \tilde{B} \text{ THEN } z \text{ is } \tilde{C}$$
> where $\tilde{A}, \tilde{B}, \tilde{C}$ are fuzzy sets on universes $X, Y, Z$ respectively.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formal Definition of a Fuzzy Set

A fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ defined on a discrete universe $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ can be written in **Zadeh's notation** as:

$$\tilde{A} = \frac{\mu_1}{x_1} + \frac{\mu_2}{x_2} + \cdots + \frac{\mu_n}{x_n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{\mu_i}{x_i}$$

For a **continuous** universe:

$$\tilde{A} = \int_X \frac{\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)}{x}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The $+$ and $\sum$ symbols here denote **aggregation of membership pairs**, **not** arithmetic addition. The integral sign $\int$ similarly denotes a continuous aggregation of all $x \in X$.

## 2.2 Properties of Fuzzy Sets

For a fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ in universe $X$:

| # | Property | Definition | Mathematical Expression |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Support** | Set of all elements with non-zero membership | $\text{Supp}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \,\vert\, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0\}$ |
| 2 | **Core** | Set of elements with full membership | $\text{Core}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \,\vert\, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1\}$ |
| 3 | **Crossover points** | Elements with $\mu = 0.5$ | $\{x \in X \,\vert\, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 0.5\}$ |
| 4 | **Height** $h(\tilde{A})$ | Maximum membership grade | $h(\tilde{A}) = \sup_{x \in X} \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ |
| 5 | **Normal set** | A set whose height equals $1$ | $h(\tilde{A}) = 1$ |
| 6 | **Sub-normal set** | A set whose height $< 1$ | $h(\tilde{A}) < 1$ |
| 7 | **Fuzzy singleton** | A set with support at a single point | $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_0) = 1$, $\mu = 0$ elsewhere |
| 8 | **Cardinality** $\vert \tilde{A} \vert$ | Sum of all memberships (scalar cardinality) | $\vert \tilde{A} \vert = \sum_{x \in X} \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ |
| 9 | **Convexity** | All $\alpha$-cuts are intervals | $\mu(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda)x_2) \ge \min(\mu(x_1),\mu(x_2))$ |
| 10 | **$\alpha$-cut** $A_\alpha$ | Crisp set of elements with $\mu \ge \alpha$ | $A_\alpha = \{x \in X \,\vert\, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \ge \alpha\}$ |
| 11 | **Strict $\alpha$-cut** $A_{\alpha+}$ | Crisp set of elements with $\mu > \alpha$ | $A_{\alpha+} = \{x \in X \,\vert\, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > \alpha\}$ |

## 2.3 Operations on Fuzzy Sets

Let $\tilde{A}$ and $\tilde{B}$ be two fuzzy sets on universe $X$, with membership functions $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ and $\mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)$.

### 2.3.1 Union ($\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$)
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x) = \max\bigl(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x),\ \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)\bigr) = \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \lor \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)$$

### 2.3.2 Intersection ($\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$)
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x) = \min\bigl(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x),\ \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)\bigr) = \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \land \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)$$

### 2.3.3 Complement ($\tilde{A}^c$ or $\bar{\tilde{A}}$)
$$\mu_{\tilde{A}^c}(x) = 1 - \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$$

### 2.3.4 Algebraic Product
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}}(x) = \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \cdot \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)$$

### 2.3.5 Algebraic Sum (Probabilistic OR)
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \oplus \tilde{B}}(x) = \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) + \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x) - \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \cdot \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)$$

### 2.3.6 Bounded Sum / Difference
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \ominus \tilde{B}}(x) = \max\bigl(0,\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) + \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x) - 1\bigr)$$

### 2.3.7 Bounded Product
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \otimes \tilde{B}}(x) = \max\bigl(0,\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) + \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x) - 1\bigr)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **De Morgan's Laws (valid for fuzzy sets under max-min complement logic):**
> $$\overline{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}} = \bar{\tilde{A}} \cap \bar{\tilde{B}}$$
> $$\overline{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}} = \bar{\tilde{A}} \cup \bar{\tilde{B}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Law of Excluded Middle** and **Law of Non-Contradiction** are **NOT** satisfied by fuzzy sets under standard min-max complement definitions:
> - $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{A}^c \neq X$ (excluded middle fails)
> - $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{A}^c \neq \emptyset$ (non-contradiction fails)
>
> Example: If $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 0.4$, then $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \lor (1 - \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)) = 0.6 \ne 1$.

## 2.4 Standard Membership Functions

### 2.4.1 Triangular MF
Parameterised by $a < b < c$:

$$\mu_{\text{tri}}(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x \le a \\ \dfrac{x-a}{b-a}, & a \le x \le b \\ \dfrac{c-x}{c-b}, & b \le x \le c \\ 0, & x \ge c \end{cases}$$

### 2.4.2 Trapezoidal MF
Parameterised by $a < b < c < d$:

$$\mu_{\text{trap}}(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x \le a \\ \dfrac{x-a}{b-a}, & a \le x \le b \\ 1, & b \le x \le c \\ \dfrac{d-x}{d-c}, & c \le x \le d \\ 0, & x \ge d \end{cases}$$

### 2.4.3 Gaussian MF
Parameterised by centre $c$ and spread $\sigma > 0$:

$$\mu_{\text{gauss}}(x) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-c)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

### 2.4.4 Generalised Bell MF
Parameterised by $a, b, c$:

$$\mu_{\text{bell}}(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \left\vert \dfrac{x-c}{a} \right\vert^{2b}}$$

### 2.4.5 Sigmoidal MF
Parameterised by $a, c$:

$$\mu_{\text{sig}}(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \exp\!\bigl(-a(x-c)\bigr)}$$

## 2.5 Fuzzy Relations

A **fuzzy relation** $R$ between two universes $X$ and $Y$ is a fuzzy set in the Cartesian product $X \times Y$:

$$R = \{\, ((x,y),\ \mu_R(x,y)) \ \vert\ x \in X,\ y \in Y \,\}$$

For discrete sets:

$$R = \sum_{i,j} \frac{\mu_R(x_i,y_j)}{(x_i, y_j)}$$

### Composition of Fuzzy Relations
Given $R \subseteq X \times Y$ and $S \subseteq Y \times Z$, the **max-min composition** is:

$$R \circ S = \{\, ((x,z),\ \max_y \min(\mu_R(x,y),\ \mu_S(y,z))) \,\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Fuzzy relations generalize crisp binary relations — instead of *related / not related* ($0$ or $1$), we have a **degree of relatedness** in $[0,1]$. They are the algebraic backbone of fuzzy rule-based inference.

## 2.6 Fuzzy IF-THEN Rules — The Two Pillars

### 2.6.1 Mamdani Fuzzy Model
Consequent is a **fuzzy set**:
$$\text{IF } x \text{ is } \tilde{A} \text{ AND } y \text{ is } \tilde{B} \text{ THEN } z \text{ is } \tilde{C}$$

### 2.6.2 Takagi-Sugeno (TS) Fuzzy Model
Consequent is a **crisp function** of inputs:
$$\text{IF } x \text{ is } \tilde{A} \text{ AND } y \text{ is } \tilde{B} \text{ THEN } z = f(x,y)$$

Typically $f(x,y) = p x + q y + r$ (linear / zero-order / first-order TS).

| Feature | Mamdani | Takagi-Sugeno |
|---|---|---|
| Consequent | Fuzzy set | Crisp polynomial |
| Defuzzification | Required | Not required (weighted average) |
| Interpretability | High | Lower |
| Computational cost | Higher | Lower |
| Suitability for optimisation | Limited | Excellent (differentiable) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Fuzzy Set Operations on Numerical Data

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Sample Problem (Full Solution Walk-through)**
> Consider the universe $X = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$. Two fuzzy sets representing linguistic concepts *Young* and *Tall* (on age / height normalised scale) are:
>
> $$\tilde{A} = \{\, 0.1/1,\ 0.3/2,\ 0.6/3,\ 0.9/4,\ 1.0/5 \,\}$$
> $$\tilde{B} = \{\, 0.2/1,\ 0.5/2,\ 0.4/3,\ 0.7/4,\ 0.8/5 \,\}$$
>
> Compute: (i) $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$, (ii) $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$, (iii) $\tilde{A}^c$, (iv) Algebraic product $\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}$, (v) Verify De Morgan's law $\overline{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}} = \bar{\tilde{A}} \cap \bar{\tilde{B}}$.

### Step (i): Union — apply max operator

For each $x \in X$:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(1) = \max(0.1,\ 0.2) = 0.2$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(2) = \max(0.3,\ 0.5) = 0.5$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(3) = \max(0.6,\ 0.4) = 0.6$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(4) = \max(0.9,\ 0.7) = 0.9$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(5) = \max(1.0,\ 0.8) = 1.0$$

Therefore:

$$\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} = \{\, 0.2/1,\ 0.5/2,\ 0.6/3,\ 0.9/4,\ 1.0/5 \,\}$$

### Step (ii): Intersection — apply min operator

$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(1) = \min(0.1,\ 0.2) = 0.1$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(2) = \min(0.3,\ 0.5) = 0.3$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(3) = \min(0.6,\ 0.4) = 0.4$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(4) = \min(0.9,\ 0.7) = 0.7$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(5) = \min(1.0,\ 0.8) = 0.8$$

Therefore:

$$\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B} = \{\, 0.1/1,\ 0.3/2,\ 0.4/3,\ 0.7/4,\ 0.8/5 \,\}$$

### Step (iii): Complement

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}^c}(x) = 1 - \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$$

Computing each value:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu_{\tilde{A}^c}(1) &= 1 - 0.1 = 0.9 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A}^c}(2) &= 1 - 0.3 = 0.7 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A}^c}(3) &= 1 - 0.6 = 0.4 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A}^c}(4) &= 1 - 0.9 = 0.1 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A}^c}(5) &= 1 - 1.0 = 0.0
\end{aligned}$$

Hence:

$$\tilde{A}^c = \{\, 0.9/1,\ 0.7/2,\ 0.4/3,\ 0.1/4,\ 0.0/5 \,\}$$

### Step (iv): Algebraic Product

$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}}(x) = \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \cdot \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)$$

Computing each value:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu_{\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}}(1) &= 0.1 \times 0.2 = 0.02 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}}(2) &= 0.3 \times 0.5 = 0.15 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}}(3) &= 0.6 \times 0.4 = 0.24 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}}(4) &= 0.9 \times 0.7 = 0.63 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}}(5) &= 1.0 \times 0.8 = 0.80
\end{aligned}$$

Hence:

$$\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B} = \{\, 0.02/1,\ 0.15/2,\ 0.24/3,\ 0.63/4,\ 0.80/5 \,\}$$

### Step (v): Verification of De Morgan's Law

**LHS: $\overline{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}$**

From Step (i), $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} = \{0.2,\ 0.5,\ 0.6,\ 0.9,\ 1.0\}$. Taking complement:

$$\overline{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}} = \{0.8/1,\ 0.5/2,\ 0.4/3,\ 0.1/4,\ 0.0/5\}$$

**RHS: $\bar{\tilde{A}} \cap \bar{\tilde{B}}$**

From Step (iii): $\bar{\tilde{A}} = \{0.9,\ 0.7,\ 0.4,\ 0.1,\ 0.0\}$.
Computing $\bar{\tilde{B}}$: $\{0.8,\ 0.5,\ 0.6,\ 0.3,\ 0.2\}$.

Apply min:

$$\bar{\tilde{A}} \cap \bar{\tilde{B}} = \{\min(0.9,0.8),\ \min(0.7,0.5),\ \min(0.4,0.6),\ \min(0.1,0.3),\ \min(0.0,0.2)\}$$

$$\bar{\tilde{A}} \cap \bar{\tilde{B}} = \{0.8/1,\ 0.5/2,\ 0.4/3,\ 0.1/4,\ 0.0/5\}$$

**Comparison:**

$$\text{LHS} = \{0.8/1,\ 0.5/2,\ 0.4/3,\ 0.1/4,\ 0.0/5\} = \text{RHS} \quad \checkmark$$

De Morgan's law is **verified** for the max-min-complement fuzzy logic.

## 3.2 Properties of the Example Fuzzy Set $\tilde{A}$

| Property | Computation | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Support | $\{x \in X \,\vert\, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0\}$ | $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ |
| Core | $\{x \in X \,\vert\, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1\}$ | $\{5\}$ |
| Crossover points | $\{x \,\vert\, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 0.5\}$ | $\emptyset$ (no exact 0.5 value) |
| Height $h(\tilde{A})$ | $\max_x \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ | $1.0$ |
| Normality | $h(\tilde{A}) = 1$? | **Normal** |
| Scalar cardinality $\vert \tilde{A} \vert$ | $0.1+0.3+0.6+0.9+1.0$ | $2.9$ |
| $\alpha$-cut at $\alpha = 0.7$ | $\{x \,\vert\, \mu \ge 0.7\}$ | $\{4, 5\}$ |

## 3.3 Derivation — Why $\max$ and $\min$ for Union and Intersection

The operators $\max$ (s-norm) and $\min$ (t-norm) are the **smallest** and **largest** operators, respectively, that satisfy the boundary conditions of fuzzy logic (Zadeh, 1965):

- For **Union**, we want the *largest* plausible degree of belonging. If an element is *partly* in $\tilde{A}$ or *partly* in $\tilde{B}$, the *least* we can claim is the **maximum** of the two — anything less would under-represent membership.

- For **Intersection**, we want the *smallest* plausible degree of *joint* belonging. The intersection cannot exceed the *weaker* of the two memberships, hence the **minimum**.

These choices guarantee the generalisation of classical Boolean logic when memberships collapse to $\{0,1\}$:

$$\max(0,0)=0,\ \max(0,1)=1,\ \max(1,1)=1 \quad \text{(matches OR)}$$

$$\min(0,0)=0,\ \min(0,1)=0,\ \min(1,1)=1 \quad \text{(matches AND)}$$

## 3.4 Worked Example — Max-Min Composition of Fuzzy Relations

Let $X = \{x_1, x_2\}$, $Y = \{y_1, y_2, y_3\}$, $Z = \{z_1, z_2\}$.

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.7 & 0.5 \\ 0.9 & 0.4 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.1 & 0.8 \\ 0.5 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute $T = R \circ S$ where $T_{ij} = \max_k \min(R_{ik}, S_{kj})$.

$$T_{11} = \max(\min(0.2, 0.6),\ \min(0.7, 0.1),\ \min(0.5, 0.5)) = \max(0.2,\ 0.1,\ 0.5) = 0.5$$

$$T_{12} = \max(\min(0.2, 0.4),\ \min(0.7, 0.8),\ \min(0.5, 0.7)) = \max(0.2,\ 0.7,\ 0.5) = 0.7$$

$$T_{21} = \max(\min(0.9, 0.6),\ \min(0.4, 0.1),\ \min(0.3, 0.5)) = \max(0.6,\ 0.1,\ 0.3) = 0.6$$

$$T_{22} = \max(\min(0.9, 0.4),\ \min(0.4, 0.8),\ \min(0.3, 0.7)) = \max(0.4,\ 0.4,\ 0.3) = 0.4$$

Hence:

$$T = R \circ S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.7 \\ 0.6 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of a Fuzzy Logic System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Crisp Input] --> B[Fuzzification]
    B --> C[Knowledge Base]
    C --> D[Rule Base]
    C --> E[Database of MFs]
    D --> F[Inference Engine]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Defuzzification]
    G --> H[Crisp Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Interpretation:**
> - **Fuzzification** converts crisp sensor data into membership degrees using MF lookup.
> - **Knowledge Base** holds both linguistic *rules* and *MF parameters*.
> - **Inference Engine** fires the rules using max-min or product composition.
> - **Defuzzification** (centroid / mean-of-max / weighted-average) yields a single crisp control action.

## 4.2 Mapping of Membership Function Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MF Types] --> B[Piecewise Linear]
    A --> C[Smooth / Curved]
    A --> D[Boundary Functions]
    B --> B1[Triangular]
    B --> B2[Trapezoidal]
    C --> C1[Gaussian]
    C --> C2[Generalised Bell]
    C --> C3[Sigmoidal]
    D --> D1[S function]
    D --> D2[Z function]
    D --> D3[Pi function]
```

## 4.3 Flow of a Fuzzy Rule (Mamdani)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input x1] --> M1[MF1 of x1]
    B[Input x2] --> M2[MF2 of x2]
    M1 --> F1[Firing Strength w1]
    M2 --> F1
    F1 --> C1[Clipped Output MF]
    C1 --> AGG[Aggregation of all rules]
    AGG --> DEF[Defuzzification]
    DEF --> OUT[Crisp Output y]
```

## 4.4 Relationship Among Fuzzy Concepts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Fuzzy Set] --> P1[Support]
    S --> P2[Core]
    S --> P3[Boundary]
    S --> P4[Height h of A]
    P4 --> P5{Normal?}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[h equals 1]
    P5 -- No --> P7[Subnormal]
    S --> P8[Alpha Cut]
    S --> OP1[Union max]
    S --> OP2[Intersection min]
    S --> OP3[Complement 1 minus mu]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a fuzzy set. Differentiate it from a crisp set with a suitable example. (3 marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Answer:**
A *fuzzy set* $\tilde{A}$ on a universe $X$ is a set of ordered pairs $(x, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x))$ where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \in [0,1]$ is the *degree of membership* of $x$ in $\tilde{A}$.

| Crisp Set | Fuzzy Set |
|---|---|
| Boundary is sharp | Boundary is gradual |
| $\mu(x) \in \{0, 1\}$ | $\mu(x) \in [0, 1]$ |
| *Tall* = height $\ge 180$ cm | *Tall* = $\mu$ varies smoothly with height |

**Example:** In a crisp set, a person of height $179$ cm is *Not Tall*; in a fuzzy set, the same person may have $\mu_{\text{Tall}}(179) = 0.85$, smoothly transitioning from *Short* to *Tall*.

---

### Q2. What is a membership function? List any three standard types of membership functions. (3 marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer:**
A *membership function* (MF) is a mathematical mapping $\mu_{\tilde{A}}: X \rightarrow [0,1]$ that assigns a *grade of membership* to each element $x$ in the universe $X$ with respect to the fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$.

**Three standard MFs:**
1. **Triangular MF** — defined by three parameters $\{a, b, c\}$; rises linearly, peaks, then falls linearly.
2. **Trapezoidal MF** — defined by four parameters $\{a, b, c, d\}$; has a flat top (plateau).
3. **Gaussian MF** — defined by centre $c$ and spread $\sigma$; smooth bell-shaped curve: $\mu(x) = \exp(-(x-c)^2/2\sigma^2)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the various properties of fuzzy sets with suitable examples. (7 marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Adapted)]`**

**Model Answer:**

The key properties of a fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ on universe $X$ are:

1. **Support:** $\text{Supp}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \,\vert\, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0\}$.
   *Example:* For $\tilde{A} = \{0/1 + 0.2/2 + 0.6/3 + 1/4 + 0.3/5\}$, support $= \{2,3,4,5\}$.

2. **Core:** $\text{Core}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \,\vert\, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1\}$.
   *Example:* Core $= \{4\}$.

3. **Height:** $h(\tilde{A}) = \sup_x \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1.0$. Since $h = 1$, the set is **normal**.

4. **Crossover points:** Elements where $\mu = 0.5$. None in the example above.

5. **$\alpha$-cut:** For $\alpha = 0.5$, $A_{0.5} = \{3, 4, 5\}$ (crisp set of elements with $\mu \ge 0.5$).

6. **Convexity:** A fuzzy set is convex iff $\mu(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda)x_2) \ge \min(\mu(x_1), \mu(x_2))$ for all $\lambda \in [0,1]$. Triangular and Gaussian MFs are convex; combinations may not be.

7. **Cardinality:** $\vert \tilde{A} \vert = 0 + 0.2 + 0.6 + 1 + 0.3 = 2.1$.

8. **Fuzzy Singleton:** A set with $\mu = 1$ at one point and $0$ elsewhere. *Example:* $\{0/1 + 0/2 + 1/3 + 0/4 + 0/5\}$.

#### (b) Two fuzzy sets are defined on $X = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ as
$$\tilde{A} = \frac{0.2}{1} + \frac{0.5}{2} + \frac{0.8}{3} + \frac{1.0}{4} + \frac{0.6}{5}$$
$$\tilde{B} = \frac{0.3}{1} + \frac{0.7}{2} + \frac{0.4}{3} + \frac{0.9}{4} + \frac{0.1}{5}$$
**Find (i) $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$, (ii) $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$, (iii) $\tilde{A}^c$. Verify De Morgan's law $\overline{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}} = \bar{\tilde{A}} \cap \bar{\tilde{B}}$.** (7 marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Union — use max operator:**

$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(1) = \max(0.2, 0.3) = 0.3$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(2) = \max(0.5, 0.7) = 0.7$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(3) = \max(0.8, 0.4) = 0.8$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(4) = \max(1.0, 0.9) = 1.0$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(5) = \max(0.6, 0.1) = 0.6$$

$$\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} = \frac{0.3}{1} + \frac{0.7}{2} + \frac{0.8}{3} + \frac{1.0}{4} + \frac{0.6}{5}$$

**(ii) Intersection — use min operator:**

$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(1) = \min(0.2, 0.3) = 0.2$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(2) = \min(0.5, 0.7) = 0.5$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(3) = \min(0.8, 0.4) = 0.4$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(4) = \min(1.0, 0.9) = 0.9$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(5) = \min(0.6, 0.1) = 0.1$$

$$\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B} = \frac{0.2}{1} + \frac{0.5}{2} + \frac{0.4}{3} + \frac{0.9}{4} + \frac{0.1}{5}$$

**(iii) Complement:**

$$\tilde{A}^c = \frac{0.8}{1} + \frac{0.5}{2} + \frac{0.2}{3} + \frac{0.0}{4} + \frac{0.4}{5}$$

**Verification of De Morgan's Law:**

LHS — $\overline{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}} = \dfrac{0.7}{1} + \dfrac{0.3}{2} + \dfrac{0.2}{3} + \dfrac{0.0}{4} + \dfrac{0.4}{5}$

RHS — $\bar{\tilde{A}} = \dfrac{0.8}{1} + \dfrac{0.5}{2} + \dfrac{0.2}{3} + \dfrac{0.0}{4} + \dfrac{0.4}{5}$

$\bar{\tilde{B}} = \dfrac{0.7}{1} + \dfrac{0.3}{2} + \dfrac{0.6}{3} + \dfrac{0.1}{4} + \dfrac{0.9}{5}$

$\bar{\tilde{A}} \cap \bar{\tilde{B}}$ (min operator):

$$\min(0.8, 0.7) = 0.7 \ \vert\ \min(0.5, 0.3) = 0.3 \ \vert\ \min(0.2, 0.6) = 0.2 \ \vert\ \min(0.0, 0.1) = 0.0 \ \vert\ \min(0.4, 0.9) = 0.4$$

$$\bar{\tilde{A}} \cap \bar{\tilde{B}} = \frac{0.7}{1} + \frac{0.3}{2} + \frac{0.2}{3} + \frac{0.0}{4} + \frac{0.4}{5}$$

LHS $=$ RHS $\checkmark$ De Morgan's law verified.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain any four types of membership functions with their mathematical formulations and graphs. (7 marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 (Expected)]`**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Triangular MF** — Simplest and most widely used. Parameterised by lower foot $a$, peak $b$, upper foot $c$:

$$\mu_{\text{tri}}(x) = \max\!\left(\min\!\left(\dfrac{x-a}{b-a},\ \dfrac{c-x}{c-b}\right),\ 0\right)$$

*Graph:* Linear ramp-up from $a$ to $b$, linear ramp-down from $b$ to $c$, zero elsewhere.

**2. Trapezoidal MF** — Has a flat top (plateau). Parameterised by $a < b < c < d$:

$$\mu_{\text{trap}}(x) = \max\!\left(\min\!\left(\dfrac{x-a}{b-a},\ 1,\ \dfrac{d-x}{d-c}\right),\ 0\right)$$

*Graph:* Ramp-up, plateau between $b$ and $c$, ramp-down, zero elsewhere. Useful for *approximately constant* regions.

**3. Gaussian MF** — Smooth, infinitely differentiable, parameterised by centre $c$ and standard deviation $\sigma$:

$$\mu_{\text{gauss}}(x) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-c)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

*Graph:* Bell-shaped, symmetric, never exactly zero. Popular in adaptive networks (ANFIS).

**4. Generalised Bell MF** — Generalises Gaussian with extra shape parameter $b$:

$$\mu_{\text{bell}}(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \left\vert \dfrac{x-c}{a} \right\vert^{2b}}$$

*Graph:* Asymmetric bell shape; tunable steepness via $b$.

**Selection Criterion:** Triangular and trapezoidal MFs are preferred for *real-time expert systems* (low compute). Gaussian and bell MFs are preferred for *learning-based systems* (smooth gradients enable optimisation).

#### (b) Discuss the structure of fuzzy IF-THEN rules. Compare the Mamdani and Takagi-Sugeno fuzzy inference methods with examples. (7 marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**Model Answer:**

**Structure of a Fuzzy IF-THEN Rule:**

A fuzzy rule maps a fuzzy input region to a fuzzy output region:

$$\textbf{R}_i:\ \text{IF}\ \tilde{A}_i(\vec{x}) \ \text{THEN}\ \tilde{C}_i(\vec{y})$$

Expanded with multiple antecedents using AND/OR:

$$R_i:\ \text{IF } x_1 \text{ is } A_{i1} \ \text{AND}\ x_2 \text{ is } A_{i2} \ \text{THEN}\ y \text{ is } C_i$$

**Example (Air-Conditioner):**
$$R_1:\ \text{IF Temp is Hot AND Humidity is High THEN FanSpeed is High}$$
$$R_2:\ \text{IF Temp is Warm AND Humidity is Low THEN FanSpeed is Medium}$$

**Mamdani Model** — Consequent is a *fuzzy set*:
$$R:\ \text{IF } x \text{ is Cold THEN Heater is High}$$
- Rule firing uses **min** (clipping) or **product** (scaling) of the output MF.
- Aggregation uses **max**; defuzzification via **centroid / mean of maxima** is mandatory.
- **Strength:** Highly interpretable, intuitive, expert-friendly.
- **Weakness:** Computationally heavy in defuzzification; not differentiable (hard to optimise).

**Takagi-Sugeno (TS) Model** — Consequent is a *crisp function* of inputs:
$$R:\ \text{IF } x \text{ is Cold AND } y \text{ is Dry THEN } z = 2x + 0.5y + 1$$
- First-order TS uses linear functions; zero-order TS uses constants.
- Output is the **weighted average** of rule consequents (no defuzzification).
- **Strength:** Differentiable, optimisable via gradient descent / least-squares; compact; suited to ANFIS.
- **Weakness:** Consequent less interpretable to a human expert.

**Comparative Summary:**

| Aspect | Mamdani | Takagi-Sugeno |
|---|---|---|
| Consequent | Fuzzy set | Crisp function |
| Rule form | `IF ... THEN y is C` | `IF ... THEN y = f(x)` |
| Defuzzification | Required | Not required |
| Computational cost | Higher (centroid) | Lower (weighted average) |
| Optimisation | Difficult | Easy (gradient-based) |
| Typical use | Expert control | Adaptive control, prediction |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $+$ sign in Zadeh's notation is aggregation, not arithmetic addition** — costing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using crisp complement ($\lnot$) instead of $1 - \mu$ for fuzzy complement** — common 2-mark error.
> 3. **Claiming $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{A}^c = X$** (Law of Excluded Middle) — **wrong** for fuzzy sets under standard max-min logic. Always clarify the operator used.
> 4. **Mixing up s-norms and t-norms** in union/intersection: Union = max (s-norm), Intersection = min (t-norm).
> 5. **In De Morgan's verification**, computing the LHS and RHS but not explicitly stating "LHS = RHS, hence verified" — incomplete; valuation key explicitly demands the concluding statement.
> 6. **Confusing fuzzy singleton (one element with $\mu=1$) with a fuzzy set having only one non-zero element** — the former requires $\mu = 1$ exactly.
> 7. **Drawing MF graphs** — examiners expect a labelled sketch (x-axis label, y-axis label, peak value, support boundaries). A textual description alone loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Fuzzy set** = mapping from universe $X$ to $[0,1]$ via membership function $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$.
- [ ] **Crisp set** = fuzzy set restricted to $\{0,1\}$ — special case.
- [ ] **Support** = region with $\mu > 0$; **Core** = region with $\mu = 1$.
- [ ] **Height** $h(\tilde{A}) = \max \mu$; **Normal** if $h = 1$.
- [ ] **$\alpha$-cut** $A_\alpha$ converts a fuzzy set to a crisp set at threshold $\alpha$.
- [ ] **Union** uses $\max$; **Intersection** uses $\min$; **Complement** uses $1 - \mu$.
- [ ] **De Morgan's laws hold** under max-min-complement; **Excluded Middle and Non-Contradiction do NOT**.
- [ ] **Triangular, Trapezoidal, Gaussian, Bell, Sigmoidal** are the five classical MFs.
- [ ] **Fuzzy relation** $R \subseteq X \times Y$ with $\mu_R(x,y) \in [0,1]$.
- [ ] **Max-min composition** $R \circ S$ generalises matrix multiplication with $\max$ and $\min$.
- [ ] **Fuzzy rule** = `IF antecedent THEN consequent`; fired by matching antecedents to inputs.
- [ ] **Mamdani** uses fuzzy consequent + defuzzification; **Takagi-Sugeno** uses crisp function + weighted average.
- [ ] **Zadeh's notation** uses `$+$` and `$\sum$`/`$\int$` to denote aggregation of membership pairs, not arithmetic.
- [ ] **Scalar cardinality** $\vert \tilde{A} \vert = \sum_x \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ — useful for ranking fuzzy sets.
- [ ] **Convexity** of an MF ensures meaningful $\alpha$-cuts as *intervals* (important for optimisation).
- [ ] **Fuzzy singleton** has $\mu = 1$ at exactly one point — the "atom" of fuzzy set theory.
- [ ] **Fuzzy systems** are universal approximators (Wang-Lex theorem) — strong industrial relevance in control, automotive, consumer electronics, and medical diagnosis.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuzzy Rule-Based Systems: Canonical Rule Forms, Composite Rules and Their Decomposition, Aggregation of Fuzzy Rules and Decision-Making
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT635 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fuzzy Rule-Based Systems: Canonical Rule Forms, Composite Rules and Their Decomposition, Aggregation of Fuzzy Rules and Decision-Making

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuzzy Rule-Based System (FRBS):** A Fuzzy Rule-Based System is a knowledge-based expert system that encodes human heuristic reasoning in the form of **IF–THEN linguistic rules**, where the antecedent and consequent contain **linguistic variables** characterized by fuzzy sets and membership functions. It emulates human decision-making by mapping fuzzy inputs through a structured pipeline of **Fuzzification → Knowledge Base → Inference Engine → Defuzzification**.

Mathematically, a fuzzy rule-based system realizes a mapping:

$$F: X \subset \mathbb{R}^n \longrightarrow Y \subset \mathbb{R}$$

through a finite set of $M$ fuzzy IF–THEN rules of the canonical form $R^{(k)}: \text{IF } \mathbf{x} \text{ is } A^{(k)} \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B^{(k)}$, where $A^{(k)} \subseteq X$ and $B^{(k)} \subseteq Y$ are fuzzy sets defined over the input and output universes of discourse respectively.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Shower Thermostat:**
> Imagine you are stepping into a shower. You do **not** say "set the temperature to exactly $42.7^\circ$C". You say things like:
> - *"If the water feels **cold**, turn the knob **slightly right**"*
> - *"If the water feels **hot**, turn the knob **left fast**"*
> - *"If the water feels **lukewarm**, keep the knob **steady**"*
>
> The words **cold, lukewarm, hot, slightly right, left fast, steady** are **linguistic labels** attached to fuzzy sets. A Fuzzy Rule-Based System captures exactly this kind of "fuzzy" expert knowledge in a mathematically rigorous way, replacing the vague human knob-twiddling with a deterministic computational engine.

## 1.3 The Four Pillars of a Fuzzy Rule-Based System

| # | Block | Function |
|---|-------|----------|
| 1 | **Fuzzifier** | Maps crisp input $x_0$ into a fuzzy set $A'$ using membership functions. |
| 2 | **Knowledge Base** | Stores the rule base $\mathcal{R}=\{R^{(1)}, R^{(2)}, \dots, R^{(M)}\}$ and database of MFs. |
| 3 | **Inference Engine** | Matches input fuzzy sets against antecedents, fires rules, and produces a fuzzy output $B'$. |
| 4 | **Defuzzifier** | Converts the aggregated fuzzy output $B'$ into a crisp control action $y^*$. |

## 1.4 Canonical Rule Form — The Foundation

A **canonical fuzzy rule** is the most elementary, syntactically irreducible fuzzy rule. KTU 2024 Scheme mandates familiarity with the following canonical forms:

**Type 1 — Simple Assignment Canonical Form:**
$$\text{IF } x \text{ is } A \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B$$

**Type 2 — Conjunctive Antecedent Canonical Form (AND-combination):**
$$\text{IF } x_1 \text{ is } A_1 \text{ AND } x_2 \text{ is } A_2 \text{ AND } \dots \text{ AND } x_n \text{ is } A_n \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B$$

**Type 3 — Disjunctive Antecedent Canonical Form (OR-combination):**
$$\text{IF } x_1 \text{ is } A_1 \text{ OR } x_2 \text{ is } A_2 \text{ OR } \dots \text{ OR } x_n \text{ is } A_n \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A fuzzy rule in canonical form has **exactly one antecedent clause connected by a single connective (AND/OR) and exactly one consequent assignment**. Composite rules (covered in §2.3) are non-canonical and must be **decomposed** into a set of canonical rules before inference.

## 1.5 The Three Principal Fuzzy Models in the KTU Syllabus

| Model | Consequent Form | Defuzzification |
|-------|------------------|------------------|
| **Mamdani** | Linguistic fuzzy set $B^{(k)}$ | Centroid / Bisector / MOM |
| **Takagi–Sugeno–Kang (TSK)** | Crisp function $y = f(\mathbf{x})$ | Weighted average of rule outputs |
| **Tsukamoto** | Monotonic fuzzy set $B^{(k)}$ | Weighted average of rule firing × singleton |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Canonical Rule — Operational Breakdown

Consider a single canonical rule with a **conjunctive antecedent** and a **Mamdani-style consequent**:

$$R^{(k)}: \text{IF } x_1 \text{ is } A_1^{(k)} \text{ AND } x_2 \text{ is } A_2^{(k)} \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B^{(k)}$$

The operational breakdown is:

- **Step 1 — Fuzzification:** Compute the membership grade of crisp input $x_i = x_i^0$ in each antecedent fuzzy set:
$$\mu_{A_i^{(k)}}(x_i^0), \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

- **Step 2 — Antecedent Aggregation:** Combine multiple antecedent membership values into a single **firing strength** $\alpha_k$. Two principal t-norms are used:
  - **MIN t-norm** (the most common in KTU exams):
$$\alpha_k = \min\{\mu_{A_1^{(k)}}(x_1^0),\, \mu_{A_2^{(k)}}(x_2^0),\, \dots,\, \mu_{A_n^{(k)}}(x_n^0)\}$$
  - **PRODUCT t-norm** (algebraic):
$$\alpha_k = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \mu_{A_i^{(k)}}(x_i^0)$$

- **Step 3 — Implication:** Apply the firing strength to the consequent fuzzy set. For **Mamdani**, the **MIN implication** clips the consequent:
$$\mu_{B'^{(k)}}(y) = \min\{\alpha_k,\, \mu_{B^{(k)}}(y)\}$$
For **Mamdani with product implication** (sometimes called Larsen implication):
$$\mu_{B'^{(k)}}(y) = \alpha_k \cdot \mu_{B^{(k)}}(y)$$

- **Step 4 — Aggregation Across Rules:** Combine $M$ individual rule outputs into one fuzzy set $B'$ (covered in §2.4).

- **Step 5 — Defuzzification:** Convert $B'$ to a crisp value $y^*$ (covered in §2.5).

## 2.2 Why Canonical Forms Matter — The 'Why' Behind the Rules

> [!NOTE]
> Canonical rule forms are the **atomic units** of a fuzzy rule base. They exist because:
> 1. **Computational Tractability:** Inference algorithms (Mamdani, TSK) are mathematically defined for canonical rules. Composite rules with nested connectives create ambiguity in firing strength computation.
> 2. **Compositionality:** Any non-canonical rule can be **mechanically decomposed** into a set of canonical rules (see §2.3) using distributive laws of fuzzy logic.
> 3. **Readability & Expert Elicitation:** Domain experts naturally express knowledge in canonical forms.

## 2.3 Composite Rules and Their Decomposition

A **composite rule** combines multiple antecedents using **mixed AND/OR connectives**, or contains **multiple consequents** joined by AND/OR. KTU 2024 expects you to know the following three families.

### Family A — Mixed-Connective Antecedents

$$R: \text{IF } (x_1 \text{ is } A_1 \text{ AND } x_2 \text{ is } A_2) \text{ OR } x_3 \text{ is } A_3 \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B$$

**Decomposition Strategy** — Apply the **distributive law of fuzzy logic**:
- Replace AND by the **minimum (or product) t-norm** $T(\cdot, \cdot)$.
- Replace OR by the **maximum (or probabilistic sum) t-conorm** $S(\cdot, \cdot)$.

The decomposition produces **two canonical rules**:
$$R^{(1)}: \text{IF } x_1 \text{ is } A_1 \text{ AND } x_2 \text{ is } A_2 \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B$$
$$R^{(2)}: \text{IF } x_3 \text{ is } A_3 \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B$$

### Family B — Multiple Consequents (Conjunctive)

$$R: \text{IF } x \text{ is } A \text{ THEN } y_1 \text{ is } B_1 \text{ AND } y_2 \text{ is } B_2 \text{ AND } \dots \text{ AND } y_m \text{ is } B_m$$

**Decomposition** — A rule with $m$ consequents splits into $m$ canonical rules:
$$R^{(k)}: \text{IF } x \text{ is } A \text{ THEN } y_k \text{ is } B_k, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, m$$

This corresponds to a **multi-output system**; each consequent variable has its own rule base.

### Family C — Multiple Consequents (Disjunctive)

$$R: \text{IF } x \text{ is } A \text{ THEN } y_1 \text{ is } B_1 \text{ OR } y_2 \text{ is } B_2$$

**Decomposition** — A single rule with $m$ disjunctive consequents becomes $m$ rules:
$$R^{(1)}: \text{IF } x \text{ is } A \text{ THEN } y_1 \text{ is } B_1$$
$$R^{(2)}: \text{IF } x \text{ is } A \text{ THEN } y_2 \text{ is } B_2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trap:** The decomposition of a disjunctive consequent rule into $m$ rules is **NOT** equivalent in firing strength to the original. The disjunction is interpreted as **independent causal pathways**, so the rules fire independently with the **same** firing strength $\alpha_k$.

## 2.4 Aggregation of Fuzzy Rules

After all $M$ rules fire, we obtain $M$ individual output fuzzy sets $B'^{(1)}, B'^{(2)}, \dots, B'^{(M)}$. **Aggregation** combines them into a single fuzzy output $B'$.

### Standard Aggregation Operators (t-conorms)

| Operator | Symbol | Formula | Property |
|----------|--------|---------|----------|
| **Maximum** | $S_{\max}$ | $\mu_{B'}(y) = \max_k \mu_{B'^{(k)}}(y)$ | Most common; idempotent, commutative |
| **Bounded Sum** | $S_{bs}$ | $\mu_{B'}(y) = \min\{1, \sum_k \mu_{B'^{(k)}}(y)\}$ | Bounded, never exceeds 1 |
| **Probabilistic Sum** | $S_{ps}$ | $\mu_{B'}(y) = 1 - \prod_k (1 - \mu_{B'^{(k)}}(y))$ | Additive reinforcement |
| **Einstein Sum** | $S_{es}$ | $\mu_{B'}(y) = \frac{\sum_k \mu_{B'^{(k)}}(y)}{1 + (M-1) \sum_k \mu_{B'^{(k)}}(y) - \dots}$ | Rare in KTU; mentioned for completeness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MAX is the default in KTU exams:** Maximum aggregation preserves the "support" of the union of all rule outputs and matches the bounded sum when the rule contributions are disjoint — making it both intuitive and mathematically clean.

## 2.5 Decision-Making — Defuzzification

The aggregated fuzzy set $B'$ must be converted to a **crisp control value** $y^* \in Y$. KTU 2024 Scheme requires fluency with **five** defuzzification methods.

| # | Method | Formula | When Used |
|---|--------|---------|-----------|
| 1 | **Centroid of Area (COA)** | $y^* = \frac{\int_Y y \cdot \mu_{B'}(y)\, dy}{\int_Y \mu_{B'}(y)\, dy}$ | Most common; smooth output |
| 2 | **Bisector of Area (BOA)** | $\int_{y_{\min}}^{y^*} \mu_{B'}(y)\, dy = \int_{y^*}^{y_{\max}} \mu_{B'}(y)\, dy$ | When centroid is hard to compute |
| 3 | **Mean of Maximum (MOM)** | $y^* = \frac{1}{\vert Y_{\max} \vert} \sum_{y \in Y_{\max}} y$ | When only the peak matters |
| 4 | **Smallest of Maximum (SOM)** | $y^* = \min\{y \mid \mu_{B'}(y) = \max\}$ | Pessimistic / cautious control |
| 5 | **Largest of Maximum (LOM)** | $y^* = \max\{y \mid \mu_{B'}(y) = \max\}$ | Optimistic / aggressive control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** For **TSK models**, defuzzification is the **weighted average**:
> $$y^* = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{M} \alpha_k \cdot f_k(\mathbf{x}_0)}{\sum_{k=1}^{M} \alpha_k}$$
> where $f_k(\mathbf{x}_0)$ is the crisp consequent function of rule $k$. For **Tsukamoto models**, the weighted average uses the **monotonic singleton** $y_k^*$ satisfying $\mu_{B^{(k)}}(y_k^*) = \alpha_k$.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Firing strength (AND) | $\alpha_k = \min_i \mu_{A_i^{(k)}}(x_i^0)$ | Mamdani MIN t-norm |
| 2 | Firing strength (AND) | $\alpha_k = \prod_i \mu_{A_i^{(k)}}(x_i^0)$ | Product t-norm |
| 3 | Mamdani implication (MIN) | $\mu_{B'^{(k)}}(y) = \min\{\alpha_k,\, \mu_{B^{(k)}}(y)\}$ | "Clipping" |
| 4 | Larsen implication (PROD) | $\mu_{B'^{(k)}}(y) = \alpha_k \cdot \mu_{B^{(k)}}(y)$ | "Scaling" |
| 5 | Aggregation (MAX) | $\mu_{B'}(y) = \max_k \mu_{B'^{(k)}}(y)$ | Default |
| 6 | Aggregation (Bnd. Sum) | $\mu_{B'}(y) = \min\{1, \sum_k \mu_{B'^{(k)}}(y)\}$ | Bounded |
| 7 | Centroid defuzz. | $y^* = \frac{\int y\, \mu_{B'}(y)\, dy}{\int \mu_{B'}(y)\, dy}$ | Continuous |
| 8 | Centroid defuzz. (discrete) | $y^* = \frac{\sum_j y_j \mu_{B'}(y_j)}{\sum_j \mu_{B'}(y_j)}$ | Sampled |
| 9 | TSK defuzzification | $y^* = \frac{\sum_k \alpha_k f_k(\mathbf{x}_0)}{\sum_k \alpha_k}$ | Weighted average |
| 10 | Tsukamoto defuzz. | $y^* = \frac{\sum_k \alpha_k y_k^*}{\sum_k \alpha_k}$ | With $y_k^* = (\mu_{B^{(k)}})^{-1}(\alpha_k)$ |
| 11 | MOM defuzzification | $y^* = \frac{1}{\vert Y_{\max} \vert} \sum_{y \in Y_{\max}} y$ | Plateau mean |
| 12 | SOM / LOM defuzzification | $y^* = \min/\max\{y \mid \mu_{B'}(y) = \max\}$ | Boundary of plateau |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Adaptive cruise control, anti-lock braking systems, automatic transmission shifting.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Washing machines (Samsung, LG), air conditioners (Mitsubishi), vacuum cleaners.
- **Industrial Process Control:** Cement kiln control, water treatment plant pH control, steel rolling mills.
- **Medical:** Fuzzy-controlled drug infusion pumps, anaesthesia depth control, diagnostic expert systems.
- **Aerospace:** Satellite attitude control, helicopter flight control (NASA/Honeywell).
- **Finance:** Stock market prediction, credit scoring, insurance risk assessment.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Full Mamdani Inference (the KTU Classic)

### Problem Setup

A fuzzy controller regulates a **heater** using two input variables and one output variable.

- Input 1: $x_1 = \text{Temperature}$ in $^\circ$C, universe $X_1 = [0, 100]$.
- Input 2: $x_2 = \text{Humidity}$ in $\%$, universe $X_2 = [0, 100]$.
- Output: $y = \text{Heater Power}$ in $\%$, universe $Y = [0, 100]$.

**Rule Base (Mamdani, two rules, both in canonical form):**

$$R^{(1)}: \text{IF } x_1 \text{ is Cold AND } x_2 \text{ is Dry THEN } y \text{ is High}$$
$$R^{(2)}: \text{IF } x_1 \text{ is Comfortable THEN } y \text{ is Medium}$$

**Membership Functions (triangular):**

- $\text{Cold}(x_1)$: $\mu_{\text{Cold}}(x_1) = \max\{0, \frac{30 - x_1}{30}\}$ for $0 \leq x_1 \leq 30$, else ramp.
- $\text{Comfortable}(x_1)$: $\mu_{\text{Comf}}(x_1) = \max\{0, 1 - \frac{\vert x_1 - 22 \vert}{5}\}$.
- $\text{Dry}(x_2)$: $\mu_{\text{Dry}}(x_2) = \max\{0, \frac{40 - x_2}{40}\}$.
- $\text{High}(y)$: $\mu_{\text{High}}(y) = \max\{0, \frac{y - 50}{50}\}$.
- $\text{Medium}(y)$: $\mu_{\text{Med}}(y) = \max\{0, 1 - \frac{\vert y - 40 \vert}{20}\}$.

**Crisp Input:** $x_1^0 = 15^\circ$C, $x_2^0 = 20\%$.

### Step 1 — Fuzzification

Evaluate each antecedent's membership at the crisp input.

**For Rule 1 antecedents:**
$$\mu_{\text{Cold}}(15) = \max\{0, \tfrac{30 - 15}{30}\} = \max\{0, 0.5\} = 0.5$$
$$\mu_{\text{Dry}}(20) = \max\{0, \tfrac{40 - 20}{40}\} = \max\{0, 0.5\} = 0.5$$

**For Rule 2 antecedent:**
$$\mu_{\text{Comf}}(15) = \max\{0, 1 - \tfrac{\vert 15 - 22 \vert}{5}\} = \max\{0, 1 - 1.4\} = 0$$

### Step 2 — Compute Firing Strengths (AND via MIN t-norm)

$$\alpha_1 = \min\{0.5,\, 0.5\} = 0.5$$
$$\alpha_2 = 0.0$$

### Step 3 — Apply Implication (Mamdani MIN clipping)

**Rule 1 output:** $B'^{(1)}(y) = \min\{0.5,\, \mu_{\text{High}}(y)\}$.

Since $\mu_{\text{High}}(y)$ is zero for $y \leq 50$ and ramps from 0 to 1 over $[50, 100]$:
$$\mu_{B'^{(1)}}(y) = \begin{cases} 0, & y \leq 50 \\ \min\{0.5,\, \tfrac{y - 50}{50}\}, & 50 < y \leq 100 \end{cases}$$

This produces a **flat plateau of height 0.5 from $y = 50$ to $y = 75$** (where $\mu_{\text{High}}(75) = 0.5$), then linearly decreasing to 0 at $y = 100$.

**Rule 2 output:** $B'^{(2)}(y) = 0$ (entirely suppressed).

### Step 4 — Aggregate via MAX

Since Rule 2 contributes zero everywhere, the aggregated output is just Rule 1's clipped output:
$$\mu_{B'}(y) = \max\{\mu_{B'^{(1)}}(y),\, \mu_{B'^{(2)}}(y)\} = \mu_{B'^{(1)}}(y)$$

### Step 5 — Defuzzify Using Centroid

We must compute:
$$y^* = \frac{\int_{50}^{100} y \cdot \mu_{B'}(y)\, dy}{\int_{50}^{100} \mu_{B'}(y)\, dy}$$

**Sub-step 5a — Find the crossover point** where $\mu_{\text{High}}(y) = 0.5$:
$$\frac{y - 50}{50} = 0.5 \;\Longrightarrow\; y = 75$$

So the membership function is **constant at 0.5 for $y \in [50, 75]$** and **linearly decreasing for $y \in [75, 100]$**.

**Sub-step 5b — Numerator integral:** Split at $y = 75$.

**Piece 1: $y \in [50, 75]$**, $\mu_{B'} = 0.5$:
$$\int_{50}^{75} y \cdot 0.5\, dy = 0.5 \cdot \left[\tfrac{y^2}{2}\right]_{50}^{75} = 0.5 \cdot \tfrac{75^2 - 50^2}{2} = 0.5 \cdot \tfrac{5625 - 2500}{2} = 0.5 \cdot 1562.5 = 781.25$$

**Piece 2: $y \in [75, 100]$**, $\mu_{B'}(y) = \frac{y - 50}{50}$:
$$\int_{75}^{100} y \cdot \frac{y - 50}{50}\, dy = \frac{1}{50}\int_{75}^{100} (y^2 - 50y)\, dy$$

Compute the inner integral:
$$\int_{75}^{100} (y^2 - 50y)\, dy = \left[\tfrac{y^3}{3} - 25y^2\right]_{75}^{100} = \left(\tfrac{10^6}{3} - 250000\right) - \left(\tfrac{421875}{3} - 140625\right)$$
$$= \tfrac{1000000 - 421875}{3} - (250000 - 140625) = \tfrac{578125}{3} - 109375$$
$$= 192708.33 - 109375 = 83333.33$$

Dividing by 50:
$$\text{Piece 2} = \tfrac{83333.33}{50} = 1666.67$$

**Total Numerator:**
$$N = 781.25 + 1666.67 = 2447.92$$

**Sub-step 5c — Denominator integral:**

**Piece 1:** $\int_{50}^{75} 0.5\, dy = 0.5 \cdot 25 = 12.5$

**Piece 2:** $\int_{75}^{100} \frac{y - 50}{50}\, dy = \frac{1}{50} \cdot \left[\tfrac{(y-50)^2}{2}\right]_{75}^{100} = \frac{1}{50} \cdot \left(\tfrac{50^2}{2} - \tfrac{25^2}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{50} \cdot \tfrac{2500 - 625}{2} = \frac{1875}{100} = 18.75$

**Total Denominator:**
$$D = 12.5 + 18.75 = 31.25$$

**Sub-step 5d — Final Crisp Output:**
$$y^* = \frac{N}{D} = \frac{2447.92}{31.25} = 78.33\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The heater should be set to **$78.33\%$ power** — consistent with intuition: it's cold ($15^\circ$C) and somewhat dry ($20\%$), so the controller demands high heating.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — TSK vs. Mamdani vs. Tsukamoto

| Property | **Mamdani** | **TSK (Sugeno)** | **Tsukamoto** |
|----------|-------------|------------------|---------------|
| **Consequent type** | Fuzzy set $B^{(k)}$ | Crisp polynomial $y = a_k x + b_k$ | Monotonic fuzzy set $B^{(k)}$ |
| **Implication** | MIN or PROD | Weighted product | MIN or PROD |
| **Aggregation** | MAX (fuzzy union) | Weighted sum | Weighted sum |
| **Defuzzification** | Centroid (integral) | Weighted average | Weighted average (singleton) |
| **Output surface** | Smooth, interpretable | Piecewise linear | Smooth, monotone |
| **Interpretability** | Highest | Lowest | Medium |
| **Computational cost** | Highest | Lowest | Medium |
| **Suitability for control** | Excellent | Excellent | Good |
| **Suitability for modeling** | Good | Excellent | Fair |

## 3.3 Decomposition Worked Example

### Original Composite Rule

$$R: \text{IF } (x_1 \text{ is } A_1 \text{ AND } x_2 \text{ is } A_2) \text{ OR } (x_3 \text{ is } A_3 \text{ AND } x_4 \text{ is } A_4) \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B$$

### Decomposition (Distributive Law: $S(T(a,b), T(c,d)) \equiv S(T(a,b)) \cup S(T(c,d))$)

$$R^{(1)}: \text{IF } x_1 \text{ is } A_1 \text{ AND } x_2 \text{ is } A_2 \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B$$
$$R^{(2)}: \text{IF } x_3 \text{ is } A_3 \text{ AND } x_4 \text{ is } A_4 \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B$$

### Firing Strengths (Original)

For crisp input $(x_1^0, x_2^0, x_3^0, x_4^0)$, the original rule's firing strength is:
$$\alpha = S(T(\mu_{A_1}(x_1^0), \mu_{A_2}(x_2^0)),\; T(\mu_{A_3}(x_3^0), \mu_{A_4}(x_4^0)))$$

Using $T = \min$ and $S = \max$:
$$\alpha = \max\{\min\{\mu_{A_1}(x_1^0), \mu_{A_2}(x_2^0)\},\, \min\{\mu_{A_3}(x_3^0), \mu_{A_4}(x_4^0)\}\}$$

### Firing Strengths (Decomposed)

$$\alpha_1 = \min\{\mu_{A_1}(x_1^0), \mu_{A_2}(x_2^0)\}$$
$$\alpha_2 = \min\{\mu_{A_3}(x_3^0), \mu_{A_4}(x_4^0)\}$$

### Aggregation

The combined output is the MAX over both decomposed rules:
$$\mu_{B'}(y) = \max\{\min\{\alpha_1, \mu_B(y)\},\, \min\{\alpha_2, \mu_B(y)\}\} = \min\{\max\{\alpha_1, \alpha_2\},\, \mu_B(y)\} = \min\{\alpha,\, \mu_B(y)\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The decomposition is **firing-strength preserving** for Mamdani MIN–MAX inference — the aggregated output is **identical** for the original composite rule and the decomposed canonical rules. This is the key result that makes decomposition a sound engineering operation.

## 3.4 Aggregation of Disjunctive Multiple Consequents

### Original
$$R: \text{IF } x \text{ is } A \text{ THEN } (y_1 \text{ is } B_1 \text{ OR } y_2 \text{ is } B_2)$$

### Decomposed
$$R^{(1)}: \text{IF } x \text{ is } A \text{ THEN } y_1 \text{ is } B_1$$
$$R^{(2)}: \text{IF } x \text{ is } A \text{ THEN } y_2 \text{ is } B_2$$

For crisp input $x = x^0$, **both decomposed rules have identical firing strength** $\alpha = \mu_A(x^0)$. The aggregation on each output variable proceeds independently:
- $B'_1(y_1) = \min\{\alpha, \mu_{B_1}(y_1)\}$ (no other rules contributing to $y_1$)
- $B'_2(y_2) = \min\{\alpha, \mu_{B_2}(y_2)\}$ (no other rules contributing to $y_2$)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The disjunction in the consequent is **interpreted as logical OR on the same firing event**, not as "either rule 1 OR rule 2 but not both". Both rules fire **simultaneously with the same strength**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Fuzzy Inference System (FIS) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Crisp Input Vector x0 equals x1 to xn]:::input
    B[Fuzzifier - computes membership grades mu Ai of xi0]:::process
    C[Knowledge Base - stores fuzzy rule base R1 to RM]:::storage
    D[Rule Firing - computes firing strength alpha k using min or product]:::process
    E[Implication - applies alpha k to consequent Bk using min or product]:::process
    F[Aggregator - combines B1 prime to BM prime using max t conorm]:::process
    G[Defuzzifier - converts aggregated B prime to crisp y star]:::process
    H[Crisp Control Output y star]:::output

    A --> B
    C --> D
    B --> D
    D --> E
    C --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H

    classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef storage fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Rule Decomposition Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S([Start - Read Composite Rule R]):::start
    P1{Is there a mixed AND OR structure in antecedent?}:::decision
    P2{Is there a conjunction AND in consequent?}:::decision
    P3{Is there a disjunction OR in consequent?}:::decision
    A1[Apply distributive law - distribute OR over AND]:::proc
    A2[Decompose into m canonical rules - one per consequent]:::proc
    A3[Decompose into m canonical rules - each with same antecedent]:::proc
    V1{Verification: Are firing strengths preserved?}:::decision
    END([End - Output Set of Canonical Rules R1 to Rn]):::finish

    S --> P1
    P1 -- Yes --> A1 --> V1
    P1 -- No --> P2
    P2 -- Yes --> A2 --> V1
    P2 -- No --> P3
    P3 -- Yes --> A3 --> V1
    P3 -- No --> V1
    V1 -- Yes --> END
    V1 -- No --> X[Flag Decomposition Error]:::error

    classDef start fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef finish fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef error fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Defuzzification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN([Aggregated Fuzzy Output B prime]):::input
    Q1{Is the MF symmetric and smooth?}:::decision
    Q2{Is there a single unique peak?}:::decision
    Q3{Is a cautious / pessimistic control desired?}:::decision
    A1[Centroid of Area - smooth output, default choice]:::proc
    A2[Mean of Maximum - average of peak plateau]:::proc
    A3[Bisector of Area - vertical line splits area in half]:::proc
    A4[Smallest of Maximum - leftmost peak]:::proc
    A5[Largest of Maximum - rightmost peak]:::proc
    OUT([Crisp Output y star]):::output

    IN --> Q1
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1 --> OUT
    Q1 -- No --> Q2
    Q2 -- Single peak --> A2 --> OUT
    Q2 -- Plateau --> Q3
    Q3 -- Yes cautious --> A4 --> OUT
    Q3 -- No aggressive --> A5 --> OUT

    classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000

```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Three Fuzzy Models Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Mamdani Model - Consequent is fuzzy set Bk]:::mam
    M2[TSK Sugeno Model - Consequent is crisp function fk of x]:::tsk
    M3[Tsukamoto Model - Consequent is monotonic fuzzy set Bk]:::tsu

    M1 --> I1[Implication - MIN clipping]
    M1 --> AG1[Aggregation - MAX t conorm]
    M1 --> DF1[Defuzzification - Centroid integral]

    M2 --> I2[Implication - weighted product]
    M2 --> AG2[Aggregation - weighted sum]
    M2 --> DF2[Defuzzification - weighted average]

    M3 --> I3[Implication - MIN clipping on monotone MF]
```
