# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a fuzzy rule-based system. Explain any two canonical rule forms with an example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Fuzzy Rule-Based System (FRBS)** is a knowledge-based computational framework that encodes human expert knowledge in the form of linguistic **IF–THEN rules** involving fuzzy sets. It performs approximate reasoning by mapping fuzzy inputs to a crisp output through four blocks: **Fuzzifier, Knowledge Base, Inference Engine, and Defuzzifier**.

**Canonical Rule Form 1 — Simple Assignment:**
$$\text{IF } x \text{ is } A \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B$$
*Example:* **IF temperature IS High THEN fan_speed IS Fast.**

**Canonical Rule Form 2 — Conjunctive Antecedent:**
$$\text{IF } x_1 \text{ is } A_1 \text{ AND } x_2 \text{ is } A_2 \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B$$
*Example:* **IF temperature IS High AND humidity IS Low THEN fan_speed IS Very_Fast.**

Canonical rules are the atomic units; composite rules are decomposed into canonical rules before inference.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List and briefly explain the five defuzzification methods used in fuzzy rule-based systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Method | Description |
|---|--------|-------------|
| 1 | **Centroid of Area (COA)** | Computes the center of gravity of the aggregated fuzzy set: $y^* = \frac{\int y\,\mu(y)\,dy}{\int \mu(y)\,dy}$. Most common. |
| 2 | **Bisector of Area (BOA)** | Finds the vertical line that splits the area under $\mu(y)$ into two equal halves. |
| 3 | **Mean of Maximum (MOM)** | Averages all $y$ values where $\mu(y)$ attains its maximum (plateau average). |
| 4 | **Smallest of Maximum (SOM)** | Returns the **leftmost** $y$ at the peak — used for cautious/pessimistic control. |
| 5 | **Largest of Maximum (LOM)** | Returns the **rightmost** $y$ at the peak — used for optimistic/aggressive control. |

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 🔹 **Question A [14 Marks]** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

**Consider a fuzzy controller for an air conditioner with two inputs and one output. The input variables are $x_1 = \text{Temperature}$ (in $^\circ$C, $0 \leq x_1 \leq 40$) and $x_2 = \text{Humidity}$ (in $\%$, $0 \leq x_2 \leq 100$). The output variable is $y = \text{Cooler Power}$ (in $\%$, $0 \leq y \leq 100$).**

**Rule Base:**
- $R^{(1)}: \text{IF } x_1 \text{ is Hot AND } x_2 \text{ is Humid THEN } y \text{ is High}$
- $R^{(2)}: \text{IF } x_1 \text{ is Warm THEN } y \text{ is Medium}$
- $R^{(3)}: \text{IF } x_1 \text{ is Cold THEN } y \text{ is Low}$

**Membership Functions:**
- $\mu_{\text{Hot}}(x_1) = \max\{0, \tfrac{x_1 - 25}{15}\}$ for $x_1 \geq 25$, else 0
- $\mu_{\text{Warm}}(x_1) = \max\{0, 1 - \tfrac{\vert x_1 - 22 \vert}{7}\}$
- $\mu_{\text{Cold}}(x_1) = \max\{0, \tfrac{15 - x_1}{15}\}$
- $\mu_{\text{Humid}}(x_2) = \max\{0, \tfrac{x_2 - 60}{40}\}$
- $\mu_{\text{High}}(y) = \max\{0, \tfrac{y - 60}{40}\}$
- $\mu_{\text{Medium}}(y) = \max\{0, 1 - \tfrac{\vert y - 50 \vert}{20}\}$
- $\mu_{\text{Low}}(y) = \max\{0, \tfrac{40 - y}{40}\}$

**Crisp Input:** $x_1^0 = 30^\circ$C, $x_2^0 = 75\%$.

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks] — Compute the firing strengths of all three rules and perform the implication step (Mamdani MIN). Sketch the output fuzzy sets from each rule.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Fuzzification at $x_1^0 = 30$, $x_2^0 = 75$:**

$$\mu_{\text{Hot}}(30) = \max\{0, \tfrac{30 - 25}{15}\} = \tfrac{5}{15} = \tfrac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$
$$\mu_{\text{Warm}}(30) = \max\{0, 1 - \tfrac{\vert 30 - 22 \vert}{7}\} = \max\{0, 1 - \tfrac{8}{7}\} = 0$$
$$\mu_{\text{Cold}}(30) = \max\{0, \tfrac{15 - 30}{15}\} = 0$$
$$\mu_{\text{Humid}}(75) = \max\{0, \tfrac{75 - 60}{40}\} = \tfrac{15}{40} = 0.375$$

**Step 2 — Firing strengths (AND via MIN):**

$$\alpha_1 = \min\{\mu_{\text{Hot}}(30),\, \mu_{\text{Humid}}(75)\} = \min\{0.333,\, 0.375\} = 0.333$$
$$\alpha_2 = \mu_{\text{Warm}}(30) = 0$$
$$\alpha_3 = \mu_{\text{Cold}}(30) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Implication (Mamdani MIN clipping):**

**Rule 1:** $B'^{(1)}(y) = \min\{0.333,\, \mu_{\text{High}}(y)\}$

For $\mu_{\text{High}}(y) = \frac{y-60}{40}$, the crossover where $\mu_{\text{High}} = 0.333$ occurs at $y = 60 + 40(0.333) = 73.33$. So:
$$B'^{(1)}(y) = \begin{cases} 0, & y \leq 60 \\ 0.333, & 60 < y \leq 73.33 \\ \tfrac{y-60}{40}, & 73.33 < y \leq 100 \end{cases}$$

**Rule 2:** $B'^{(2)}(y) = 0$ everywhere.

**Rule 3:** $B'^{(3)}(y) = 0$ everywhere.

**Conclusion:** Only Rule 1 contributes. The output is a clipped triangle of peak height $0.333$ from $y=60$ to $y=73.33$, with a linear tail down to 0 at $y=100$.

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks] — Aggregate the three outputs and perform centroid defuzzification to obtain the crisp cooler power.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 4 — Aggregation via MAX:**

$$\mu_{B'}(y) = \max\{B'^{(1)}(y),\, B'^{(2)}(y),\, B'^{(3)}(y)\} = B'^{(1)}(y)$$

**Step 5 — Centroid Defuzzification:**

$$y^* = \frac{\int_{60}^{100} y \cdot \mu_{B'}(y)\, dy}{\int_{60}^{100} \mu_{B'}(y)\, dy}$$

**Sub-step 5a — Numerator:**

**Piece 1: $y \in [60, 73.33]$, $\mu_{B'} = 0.333$:**
$$\int_{60}^{73.33} 0.333\, y\, dy = 0.333 \cdot \tfrac{73.33^2 - 60^2}{2} = 0.333 \cdot \tfrac{5377.29 - 3600}{2} = 0.333 \cdot 888.65 = 295.92$$

**Piece 2: $y \in [73.33, 100]$, $\mu_{B'} = \tfrac{y-60}{40}$:**

$$\int_{73.33}^{100} y \cdot \frac{y-60}{40}\, dy = \frac{1}{40} \int_{73.33}^{100} (y^2 - 60y)\, dy$$

$$\int_{73.33}^{100} (y^2 - 60y)\, dy = \left[\tfrac{y^3}{3} - 30y^2\right]_{73.33}^{100}$$
$$= \left(\tfrac{10^6}{3} - 300000\right) - \left(\tfrac{73.33^3}{3} - 30 \cdot 73.33^2\right)$$

Compute $73.33^2 = 5377.29$ and $73.33^3 \approx 394289.5$:
$$= (333333.33 - 300000) - (131429.83 - 161318.79) = 33333.33 - (-29888.96) = 63222.29$$

So Piece 2 $= \tfrac{63222.29}{40} = 1580.56$

**Total Numerator:** $N = 295.92 + 1580.56 = 1876.48$

**Sub-step 5b — Denominator:**

**Piece 1:** $\int_{60}^{73.33} 0.333\, dy = 0.333 \cdot 13.33 = 4.44$

**Piece 2:** $\int_{73.33}^{100} \tfrac{y-60}{40}\, dy = \tfrac{1}{40} \cdot \left[\tfrac{(y-60)^2}{2}\right]_{73.33}^{100} = \tfrac{1}{40} \cdot \left(\tfrac{40^2}{2} - \tfrac{13.33^2}{2}\right) = \tfrac{1}{40} \cdot (800 - 88.89) = \tfrac{711.11}{40} = 17.78$

**Total Denominator:** $D = 4.44 + 17.78 = 22.22$

**Step 6 — Final Crisp Output:**
$$y^* = \frac{1876.48}{22.22} \approx 84.45\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> - **Common Mistake #1 (1 mark lost):** Using the **full triangle** of $\mu_{\text{High}}$ instead of the **clipped** output $B'^{(1)}$. The MIN implication clips the peak; students often forget this and compute the centroid of the un-clipped triangle.
> - **Common Mistake #2 (1 mark lost):** Computing the **discrete centroid** without showing the integration limits or splitting the integral at the crossover point $y = 73.33$. The KTU board requires **explicit piecewise integration**.
> - **Common Mistake #3 (1 mark lost):** Skipping the aggregation step and writing "we have $B' = B'^{(1)}$" without justification. Always state explicitly that the MAX of the three outputs is dominated by $B'^{(1)}$.

---

### 🔹 **Question B [14 Marks]** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]*

**Part (a) [7 Marks] — With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a fuzzy rule-based system. Discuss the role of the knowledge base and the inference engine.**

**Model Answer:**

A Fuzzy Rule-Based System consists of **four interconnected blocks**:

1. **Fuzzification Interface** — Converts crisp input vector $\mathbf{x}^0 = (x_1^0, \dots, x_n^0)$ into fuzzy sets $A' \subseteq X$ by computing membership degrees $\mu_{A_i}(x_i^0)$ using membership functions stored in the database.

2. **Knowledge Base** — Contains two sub-components:
   - **Database (DB):** Stores the membership functions of all linguistic variables.
   - **Rule Base (RB):** Stores the set of fuzzy IF–THEN rules $\mathcal{R} = \{R^{(1)}, \dots, R^{(M)}\}$ elicited from domain experts or learned from data.

3. **Inference Engine (Decision-Making Unit)** — Performs the reasoning by:
   - **Matching:** Computing the firing strength $\alpha_k$ of each rule.
   - **Combination of Antecedents:** Using the MIN or PRODUCT t-norm.
   - **Implication:** Propagating $\alpha_k$ to the consequent (MIN clipping for Mamdani, weighted product for TSK).
   - **Aggregation:** Combining all rule outputs via MAX (Mamdani) or weighted sum (TSK).

4. **Defuzzification Interface** — Converts the aggregated fuzzy output $B'$ into a crisp value $y^* \in Y$ using Centroid, Bisector, MOM, SOM, or LOM.

The **knowledge base** is the "memory" — it stores static, expert-encoded knowledge. The **inference engine** is the "processor" — it dynamically combines fuzzified inputs with the knowledge base to produce fuzzy outputs.

*(See §4.1 Mermaid diagram for the architecture.)*

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks] — Explain canonical and composite fuzzy rules. Decompose the following composite rule into canonical form, and show that the firing strength is preserved under decomposition:**

$$R: \text{IF } (x_1 \text{ is } A_1 \text{ AND } x_2 \text{ is } A_2) \text{ OR } (x_3 \text{ is } A_3) \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B$$

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions:**

- **Canonical Rule:** A syntactically irreducible fuzzy rule with **at most one connective (AND/OR)** between antecedents and **exactly one consequent assignment**.
- **Composite Rule:** Any fuzzy rule that does **not** satisfy the canonical form — typically containing mixed AND/OR connectives or multiple consequents.

**Decomposition of the Given Composite Rule:**

Using the **distributive law** of fuzzy logic with $T = \min$ (AND) and $S = \max$ (OR):

$$R^{(1)}: \text{IF } x_1 \text{ is } A_1 \text{ AND } x_2 \text{ is } A_2 \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B$$
$$R^{(2)}: \text{IF } x_3 \text{ is } A_3 \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B$$

**Firing Strength — Original Rule (using MIN–MAX):**
$$\alpha = \max\{\min\{\mu_{A_1}(x_1^0), \mu_{A_2}(x_2^0)\},\, \mu_{A_3}(x_3^0)\}$$

**Firing Strengths — Decomposed Rules:**
$$\alpha_1 = \min\{\mu_{A_1}(x_1^0), \mu_{A_2}(x_2^0)\}$$
$$\alpha_2 = \mu_{A_3}(x_3^0)$$

**Aggregated Output (MAX over decomposed rules with MIN implication):**
$$\mu_{B'}(y) = \max\{\min\{\alpha_1, \mu_B(y)\},\, \min\{\alpha_2, \mu_B(y)\}\}$$
$$= \min\{\max\{\alpha_1, \alpha_2\},\, \mu_B(y)\} = \min\{\alpha,\, \mu_B(y)\}$$

Since the **MIN and MAX operators distribute** over each other in the right order, the aggregated output from the decomposed rules is **identical** to that of the original composite rule under MIN implication and MAX aggregation. Hence the firing strength is **preserved**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> - **Common Mistake #1 (1 mark lost):** Forgetting to **state the t-norm and t-conorm** explicitly. Always write "using MIN for AND and MAX for OR" before computing firing strengths.
> - **Common Mistake #2 (1 mark lost):** Decomposing the disjunction by adding rules (treating OR as addition) instead of using the **MAX t-conorm**. This is a fundamental fuzzy-logic error.
> - **Common Mistake #3 (2 marks lost):** Not proving the firing-strength preservation. The KTU board requires the **algebraic identity** $\max\{\min\{a,b\}, c\} = \min\{\max\{a,c\}, \max\{b,c\}\}$ to be invoked explicitly.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fuzzy Rule-Based System** = Fuzzifier + Knowledge Base + Inference Engine + Defuzzifier.
- **Canonical Rule Form** is atomic: single connective, single consequent.
- **Composite Rule** with mixed connectives is decomposed via the **distributive law** of fuzzy logic.
- **Multiple Consequents (AND)** → split into $m$ canonical rules (multi-output system).
- **Multiple Consequents (OR)** → split into $m$ rules with **identical firing strength**.
- **Firing Strength for AND** = $\min$ or $\prod$ of antecedent memberships.
- **Mamdani Implication** = MIN clipping (or PRODUCT scaling for Larsen).
- **Aggregation** = MAX (Mamdani) or weighted sum (TSK/Tsukamoto).
- **Defuzzification methods (5):** Centroid, Bisector, MOM, SOM, LOM.
- **TSK Defuzzification** is the **weighted average** of crisp consequent functions.
- **Tsukamoto** uses **monotonic MFs**, defuzzification = weighted average of **inverses**.
- **Decomposition is firing-strength preserving** for MIN–MAX Mamdani inference.
- **MAX aggregation** is the KTU default; bounded sum and probabilistic sum are alternatives.
- **Real-world applications:** HVAC, automotive, industrial process control, medical devices, aerospace.
- **Mamdani = most interpretable; TSK = best for modeling; Tsukamoto = compromise.**
- **KTU Common Trap:** Always explicitly identify the **crossover point** when computing the centroid of a clipped Mamdani output.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Notes — Fuzzy Rule-Based Systems.** Master the Mamdani worked example (§3.1) and the decomposition proof (§3.3); these two topics account for ~60% of marks in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Genetic Algorithms: Evolutionary Concepts and GA Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT635 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Genetic Algorithms: Evolutionary Concepts and GA Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Genetic Algorithm (GA)**
> A Genetic Algorithm is a **population-based, stochastic, meta-heuristic search and optimization technique** inspired by the mechanics of **natural selection** and **biological evolution**. It operates on a population of candidate solutions (called *chromosomes* or *individuals*), encoded as strings over a finite alphabet, and iteratively applies **selection**, **crossover** (recombination), and **mutation** operators to evolve better solutions over generations.

The canonical formulation credited to **John Henry Holland (1975)** in his book *"Adaptation in Natural and Artificial Systems"* treats GAs as a class of **Evolutionary Algorithms (EAs)** that maintain a population of candidate solutions and use biologically motivated operators to guide the search toward optimality.

## 1.2 The Darwinian Foundation

The entire theoretical scaffolding of GAs rests on **Charles Darwin's Theory of Natural Selection (1859)**, which rests on five postulates:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Darwin's Five Postulates of Natural Selection**
> 1. **Variation:** Individuals within a population exhibit differences in traits.
> 2. **Inheritance:** Many of these variations are heritable (passed from parent to offspring).
> 3. **Struggle for Existence:** Resources are limited, forcing competition.
> 4. **Survival of the Fittest:** Individuals with traits best suited to the environment survive and reproduce.
> 5. **Differential Reproduction:** Favorable traits accumulate in the population over generations (gradual evolution).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Chameleon Colony"

Imagine a colony of 100 chameleons on a green island. Each chameleon has a slight color variation. Green leaves are abundant; white flowers are scarce but richer in nectar. The chameleons with **greener skin** survive longer (better camouflage) and produce more offspring. Over many generations, the entire colony shifts toward green, even though some white chameleons are randomly produced (mutations). The **environment acts as the fitness function**, and the **population self-tunes to the optimum**.

A Genetic Algorithm replicates *exactly* this loop on a mathematical landscape:
- **Chameleons** → Candidate solutions (chromosomes)
- **Color genes** → Variables (decision variables)
- **Environment** → Fitness function $f(x)$
- **Reproduction** → Crossover + Mutation
- **Generations** → Iterations of the algorithm

## 1.4 Core Terminology Map

| Soft Computing Term | Biological Analogue | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Individual / Chromosome** | Organism | A single candidate solution |
| **Gene** | DNA sequence element | A single decision variable |
| **Allele** | Variant of a gene | A specific value a gene can take |
| **Population** | Group of organisms | The set of current candidate solutions |
| **Fitness Function** | Environmental pressure | Objective function $f(x)$ guiding selection |
| **Selection** | Survival of the fittest | Probabilistic choice of parents |
| **Crossover** | Sexual reproduction | Recombining two parents |
| **Mutation** | Random genetic drift | Flipping a gene value at random |
| **Generation** | Reproduction cycle | One full iteration of the GA loop |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** GAs are **parallel-search** algorithms — they evaluate *many* candidate solutions in a single generation, unlike hill-climbing or gradient descent which maintain a single point.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Canonical GA Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

The classical Genetic Algorithm loop follows this **seven-step ritual** in every generation:

1. **Initialization:** Generate an initial population $P_0$ of $N$ chromosomes, each of length $L$, usually via uniform random sampling over the feasible search space.
2. **Fitness Evaluation:** For every individual $x_i \in P_t$, compute its fitness value $f(x_i)$ using the problem-specific fitness function.
3. **Selection:** Form a mating pool by probabilistically choosing individuals; higher-fitness chromosomes are preferred. Common methods: **Roulette Wheel**, **Tournament Selection**, **Rank-Based Selection**.
4. **Crossover (Recombination):** With probability $p_c$ (typically $0.6$ to $0.9$), pair two parents and exchange genetic material to produce two offspring. Standard schemes: **Single-Point**, **Two-Point**, **Uniform**.
5. **Mutation:** With probability $p_m$ (typically small, $\approx 1/L$ to $0.1$), randomly alter gene values to maintain genetic diversity and escape local optima.
6. **Replacement:** Form the next-generation population $P_{t+1}$ using strategies such as *generational replacement*, *elitism*, or *steady-state* replacement.
7. **Termination Check:** Stop when a stopping criterion is met — maximum generations, convergence of the best fitness, or a satisfactory solution is found. Otherwise, return to Step 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these steps?** The selection operator creates **exploitation pressure** (bias toward good solutions), while mutation provides **exploration** (visits new regions of the search space). The interplay between these two is the **central theorem of GAs** — the *Schema Theorem* (Holland, 1975).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do NOT use the vertical bar `|` inside this table — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.**

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning / Use Case | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Population size | $20$ to $200$ |
| $L$ | Chromosome length (number of genes) | Problem-dependent |
| $f(x_i)$ | Raw fitness of individual $x_i$ | $\geq 0$ (often) |
| $F(x_i) = f(x_i) - f_{min}$ | Scaled/windowed fitness | Non-negative |
| $p_i = \dfrac{f(x_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f(x_j)}$ | **Roulette wheel selection probability** of $x_i$ | $0 < p_i \le 1$ |
| $\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i = 1$ | Probability normalization constraint | Always true |
| $p_c$ | Crossover probability | $0.6 \le p_c \le 0.9$ |
| $p_m$ | Mutation probability | $0.001 \le p_m \le 0.1$ |
| $n_{elitism}$ | Number of best individuals copied directly | $1$ to $5$ |
| $\bar{f}(t) = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} f(x_i)$ | Mean fitness of population at generation $t$ | Tracking metric |
| $f_{max}(t) = \max_i f(x_i)$ | Best fitness in generation $t$ | Convergence metric |
| $\sigma_f(t)$ | Standard deviation of fitness at $t$ | Diversity measure |
| Expected count of schema $H$ at $t+1$ | $E[m(H, t+1)] \geq m(H, t) \cdot \dfrac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \cdot \dfrac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right]$ | **Holland's Schema Theorem** |

Where:
- $m(H, t)$ = number of chromosomes matching schema $H$ at generation $t$
- $f(H)$ = average fitness of chromosomes containing schema $H$
- $\bar{f}$ = population mean fitness
- $\delta(H)$ = defining length of $H$ (distance between first and last fixed positions)
- $o(H)$ = order of $H$ (number of fixed positions)
- $L$ = chromosome length

## 2.3 Features of Genetic Algorithms (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU-listed features of GAs (must-know for exams):**

1. **Population-Based Search:** Operates on a *set* of solutions, not a single point — robust against local optima.
2. **Stochastic:** Uses *randomized* operators (selection, crossover, mutation) — non-deterministic.
3. **Fitness-Driven (Objective-Function-Only):** Needs *only* the fitness function — no derivatives, gradients, or auxiliary information required.
4. **Encoding Flexibility:** Works on binary, real-valued, integer, or symbolic representations.
5. **Implicit Parallelism:** Through the Schema Theorem, GAs effectively process $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ schemata per generation.
6. **Black-Box Optimization:** Suitable when the search space is non-differentiable, noisy, or discrete.
7. **Probabilistic Transition Rules:** State transitions are governed by probabilities, not deterministic rules.
8. **Self-Adaptation:** The population itself evolves; no external learning rate required.
9. **Multi-Objective Capable:** Through extensions like **NSGA-II**, GAs can handle multiple conflicting objectives.
10. **Domain Independence:** The same algorithmic framework solves vastly different problems (TSP, NN training, scheduling, VLSI).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | GA Application |
|---|---|
| Aerospace | Trajectory optimization, airfoil design |
| VLSI / Circuit Design | Placement & routing, FPGA synthesis |
| Robotics | Path planning, gait optimization |
| Finance | Portfolio optimization, algorithmic trading |
| Machine Learning | Hyperparameter tuning, feature selection, neural-network weight optimization |
| Bio-informatics | Protein structure prediction, sequence alignment |
| Operations Research | Job-shop scheduling, vehicle routing, knapsack |
| Image Processing | Image segmentation, threshold selection |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Roulette-Wheel Selection Probability

The cornerstone of proportional selection is the **Roulette-Wheel (Fitness-Proportionate) Selection** operator. We derive the selection probability of an individual $x_i$.

**Step 1 — Define raw fitness.**
Let $f(x_i)$ denote the raw (problem-specific) fitness of the $i$-th individual, with $f(x_i) \geq 0$ for all $i$ in the population.

**Step 2 — Compute total population fitness.**
Sum the fitness of every individual to obtain a normalizer:

$$
F_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{N} f(x_j)
$$

**Step 3 — Form the probability ratio.**
The selection probability of individual $x_i$ is the fraction of the total fitness it represents:

$$
p_i = \frac{f(x_i)}{F_{total}} = \frac{f(x_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f(x_j)}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify normalization (consistency check).**
Summing over the entire population:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{f(x_i)}{F_{total}} = \frac{1}{F_{total}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} f(x_i) = \frac{F_{total}}{F_{total}} = 1
$$

This confirms the probabilities form a valid probability mass function.

**Step 5 — Expected number of copies.**
The expected number of times $x_i$ enters the mating pool is:

$$
E[\text{copies of } x_i] = N \cdot p_i = N \cdot \frac{f(x_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f(x_j)}
$$

This means an individual whose fitness is **twice** the population average is **expected to produce two copies** in the next generation's mating pool.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Cumulative Fitness)

Consider a population of $N = 4$ chromosomes with fitness values:

| Individual $x_i$ | $f(x_i)$ | $p_i$ | Cumulative $q_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $x_1$ | $2$ | $2/10 = 0.20$ | $0.20$ |
| $x_2$ | $1$ | $1/10 = 0.10$ | $0.30$ |
| $x_3$ | $4$ | $4/10 = 0.40$ | $0.70$ |
| $x_4$ | $3$ | $3/10 = 0.30$ | $1.00$ |
| **Total** | $\sum f = 10$ | $\sum p = 1.00$ | — |

**Roulette-wheel mechanism:** generate a uniform random number $r \in [0, 1)$ and select the *first* individual whose cumulative fitness $q_i \geq r$.

For example, if $r = 0.55$, then $x_3$ is selected (since $0.30 < 0.55 \leq 0.70$).

## 3.3 Derivation: Holland's Schema Theorem (Foundational)

A **schema** $H$ is a template over the alphabet $\{0, 1, *\}$, where $*$ is the *don't-care* symbol. For example, the schema $H = 1**0$ matches any 4-bit chromosome starting with 1 and ending with 0.

**Step 1 — Reproduction effect under fitness-proportionate selection.**
The expected number of copies of schema $H$ in the next generation, due to selection alone, is:

$$
E[\text{reproduction}] = m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}(t)}
$$

because the expected count is proportional to the ratio of schema fitness to mean population fitness.

**Step 2 — Crossover disruption.**
A single-point crossover at any of the $L-1$ possible cut points disrupts schema $H$ *only if* the cut falls *inside* the schema's defining length $\delta(H)$. The probability of disruption is bounded by:

$$
P_{disrupt, crossover} \leq \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1}
$$

So the survival probability is $\geq \left(1 - p_c \cdot \dfrac{\delta(H)}{L-1}\right)$.

**Step 3 — Mutation disruption.**
Each of the $o(H)$ fixed positions of the schema can be flipped by mutation. The survival probability is approximately:

$$
P_{survive, mutation} \approx (1 - p_m)^{o(H)} \geq \left(1 - p_m \cdot o(H)\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Combine all three operators multiplicatively (independence assumption).**

$$
E[m(H, t+1)] \geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}(t)} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right]
$$

**Interpretation:** *Short, low-order, above-average schemata grow exponentially in the population.* This is the **Building Block Hypothesis** — GAs implicitly combine small useful chunks of information.

## 3.4 Mutation Probability for Binary Strings (Derivational Insight)

For a chromosome of length $L$, if we want the **expected number of flipped bits** to be exactly **1** per chromosome, we set:

$$
E[\text{flips per chromosome}] = L \cdot p_m = 1
$$

Solving:

$$
p_m = \frac{1}{L}
$$

This is the **canonical** mutation-rate heuristic in binary GAs, and is a high-yield exam fact.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical GA Loop (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B[Initialize Population P of N chromosomes]
    B --> C[Evaluate fitness f of every individual]
    C --> D{Termination criterion met?}
    D -- No --> E[Select parents via fitness-proportionate / tournament / rank]
    E --> F[Apply Crossover with probability pc]
    F --> G[Apply Mutation with probability pm]
    G --> H[Form next-generation population P of t plus 1]
    H --> C
    D -- Yes --> I[Return best chromosome as solution]
    I --> J([STOP])
```

> [!NOTE]
> The loop above corresponds exactly to the seven-step ritual outlined in **Section 2.1**. The decision node `D` is the only branching point and corresponds to the termination check.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a GA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[/"Initial Population P of 0"/] --> E1[/"Fitness Evaluation Module"/]
    E1 --> SEL[/"Selection Operator: Roulette or Tournament"/]
    SEL --> POOL[/"Mating Pool: Selected Parents"/]
    POOL --> CX[/"Crossover Operator: Single or Two-Point"/]
    CX --> MUT[/"Mutation Operator: Bit-Flip or Gaussian"/]
    MUT --> REP[/"Replacement Strategy: Generational or Elitist"/]
    REP --> E2[/"Fitness Evaluation for New Pop"/]
    E2 --> CHK{"Converged?"}
    CHK -- No --> SEL
    CHK -- Yes --> OUT[/"Optimal Solution x star"/]
```

## 4.3 Schema-Processing Intuition (Mermaid Subgraph)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SCH[Schema Processing in a Generation]
        D1[Schema H with high fitness f H] --> D2[Exponentially amplified by selection]
        D3[Schema H with short defining length delta] --> D4[Resistant to crossover disruption]
        D5[Schema H with low order o] --> D6[Resistant to mutation disruption]
        D2 --> D7[Building Blocks accumulate in population]
        D4 --> D7
        D6 --> D7
    end
```

## 4.4 Mapping of Biology to Algorithm (Mermaid Mapping Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BIO[Biological Domain]
        B1[Cell and DNA] --> B2[Chromosome]
        B2 --> B3[Gene and Allele]
        B1 --> B4[Population of organisms]
        B4 --> B5[Natural selection pressure]
    end
    subgraph ALG[Algorithmic Domain]
        A1[Solution candidate] --> A2[Encoded string of length L]
        A2 --> A3[Variable and its value]
        A1 --> A4[Set of N candidate solutions]
        A4 --> A5[Fitness function evaluation]
    end
    B1 --- A1
    B2 --- A2
    B3 --- A3
    B4 --- A4
    B5 --- A5
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **List any six distinguishing features of Genetic Algorithms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Genetic Algorithm is characterized by:
1. **Population-based search** — works on a set of solutions in parallel.
2. **Stochastic operators** — selection, crossover, and mutation are randomized.
3. **Fitness-function-driven** — needs only the objective function value, no gradient.
4. **Encoding flexibility** — supports binary, real, integer, and tree representations.
5. **Implicit parallelism** — processes $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ schemata simultaneously (Schema Theorem).
6. **Robustness against local optima** — probabilistic operators prevent premature convergence.
*(1/2 mark per correct feature, full credit for any six.)*

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Define the terms: (i) Chromosome, (ii) Fitness Function, (iii) Schema, in the context of Genetic Algorithms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(i) Chromosome (1 mark):** A chromosome is a string-encoded candidate solution to the optimization problem. It consists of $L$ genes, where each gene represents one decision variable.
- **(ii) Fitness Function (1 mark):** A fitness function $f(x)$ maps each chromosome to a non-negative scalar measure of "how good" that solution is with respect to the problem objective. It is the *sole* driver of evolutionary pressure in a GA.
- **(iii) Schema (1 mark):** A schema is a *similarity template* over the alphabet $\{0, 1, *\}$ that describes a subset of chromosomes sharing a common structural pattern. The $*$ is a *don't-care* symbol.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
> **(a)** Explain the seven basic steps of a Genetic Algorithm with a neat flowchart. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** For a population of $N=5$ chromosomes with fitness values $\{10, 20, 30, 40, 50\}$, perform two iterations of the **Roulette-Wheel Selection** operator. Compute the expected number of copies of each individual. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The seven canonical steps of a Genetic Algorithm are:

1. **Initialization:** Generate initial population $P_0$ of $N$ random chromosomes.
2. **Fitness Evaluation:** Compute $f(x_i)$ for every $x_i \in P_t$.
3. **Selection:** Form mating pool using fitness-proportionate (Roulette) or tournament selection.
4. **Crossover:** With probability $p_c$, recombine pairs of parents to form offspring.
5. **Mutation:** With probability $p_m$, flip bits in offspring chromosomes.
6. **Replacement:** Replace old population with new offspring (with optional elitism).
7. **Termination:** Stop if convergence is reached or maximum generations are exhausted.

Flowchart — refer to **Section 4.1 (Mermaid diagram)** above for the visual representation. *(1 mark for each step, 1 mark for the flowchart.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Total fitness.**

$$
F_{total} = 10 + 20 + 30 + 40 + 50 = 150
$$

**Step 2 — Selection probabilities.**

$$
p_1 = \frac{10}{150} = 0.0667, \quad p_2 = \frac{20}{150} = 0.1333, \quad p_3 = \frac{30}{150} = 0.2000
$$

$$
p_4 = \frac{40}{150} = 0.2667, \quad p_5 = \frac{50}{150} = 0.3333
$$

Verification: $\sum p_i = 0.0667 + 0.1333 + 0.2000 + 0.2667 + 0.3333 = 1.0000$ ✓

**Step 3 — Expected number of copies (for a mating pool of size $N = 5$):**

$$
E[\text{copies of } x_i] = N \cdot p_i
$$

| Individual | $f(x_i)$ | $p_i$ | Expected Copies $N \cdot p_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $x_1$ | $10$ | $0.0667$ | $0.3333$ |
| $x_2$ | $20$ | $0.1333$ | $0.6667$ |
| $x_3$ | $30$ | $0.2000$ | $1.0000$ |
| $x_4$ | $40$ | $0.2667$ | $1.3333$ |
| $x_5$ | $50$ | $0.3333$ | $1.6667$ |

**Step 4 — Two-iteration simulation.**
Over two iterations of proportional selection, multiply the expected-count operator twice. For individual $x_5$ with fitness $50$ (3.33× the mean of $30$):

$$
E[\text{copies after 2 iter}] = N \cdot (p_5)^2 = 5 \cdot (0.3333)^2 = 5 \cdot 0.1111 = 0.5556
$$

Similarly for $x_1$:

$$
E[\text{copies after 2 iter}] = 5 \cdot (0.0667)^2 = 5 \cdot 0.00444 = 0.0222
$$

**Inference:** The fittest individual $x_5$ is expected to dominate the mating pool over successive generations, illustrating *exponential amplification of high-fitness schemata* predicted by the **Schema Theorem**. *(7 marks: 2 for setup, 3 for computation, 2 for interpretation.)*

---

> **Question B.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
> **(a)** Explain the biological foundations of Genetic Algorithms, mapping each evolutionary concept to its algorithmic counterpart. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** State and prove Holland's Schema Theorem. Discuss its significance in GA theory. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The biological-to-algorithmic mapping table (draw the table in the answer sheet for full marks):

| Biological Concept | Algorithmic Counterpart | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Organism / Individual | Chromosome | A candidate solution |
| Population | Population of $N$ solutions | Search-space sampling |
| Environment pressure | Fitness function $f(x)$ | Optimization objective |
| Reproduction | Crossover operator | Solution recombination |
| Genetic mutation | Mutation operator | Stochastic exploration |
| Natural selection | Selection operator | Probabilistic pruning |
| Generation | Iteration index $t$ | Time-step of evolution |
| Gene pool | Search space | The set of all feasible solutions |
| Species | Cluster in the population | Local attractors |
| Survival of the fittest | Bias toward high $f(x)$ | Exploitation pressure |

**Why this mapping works:** The fitness landscape mimics natural selection pressure: just as harsh environments favour organisms with survival-enhancing genes, a well-designed $f(x)$ guides chromosomes toward the global optimum. *(7 marks: 1 per correct row + 1 mark for explanation.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Statement of the Schema Theorem (Holland, 1975):** *Short, low-order, above-average schemata receive exponentially increasing trials in successive generations.*

Mathematically, the expected number of copies of schema $H$ in generation $t+1$ is bounded below by:

$$
E[m(H, t+1)] \geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}(t)} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right]
$$

where the symbols are as defined in the formula sheet of **Section 2.2**.

**Proof Sketch (2 marks):**

*Reproduction term:* Under fitness-proportionate selection, the expected count of $H$ is $m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}(t)}$ (direct application of roulette-wheel selection).

*Crossover survival:* A single-point crossover at any of the $L-1$ cut points disrupts $H$ only if the cut lies between the schema's outermost fixed positions, which happens with probability at most $\frac{\delta(H)}{L-1}$. Hence the survival factor is $\geq 1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1}$.

*Mutation survival:* Each of the $o(H)$ fixed positions survives mutation with probability $(1 - p_m)$, so the schema survives with probability $\geq (1 - p_m)^{o(H)} \geq 1 - p_m \cdot o(H)$.

*Combine (independence):* Multiply the three factors.

**Significance (3 marks):**
1. **Building-Block Hypothesis:** GAs discover good solutions by combining short, high-quality schemata.
2. **Implicit Parallelism:** $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ schemata are processed per generation despite evaluating only $N$ individuals.
3. **Convergence Justification:** Provides the theoretical foundation for *why* GAs work — not merely empirical evidence.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Triggers**
> 1. **Forgetting the schema normalization factor $\frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}}$** — it is *not* $f(H)$ alone but the *ratio* to the mean.
> 2. **Mis-defining $L$:** $L$ is the *chromosome length*, not the schema length. The cut-point count is $L-1$, not $L$.
> 3. **Confusing $\delta(H)$ and $o(H)$:** $\delta$ is the *defining length* (a span), $o$ is the *order* (a count). Reversing them silently changes the entire formula.
> 4. **In numerical problems**, students often compute the *cumulative* probability incorrectly — remember the **roulette wheel uses $q_i \geq r$ (strict-inequality inclusive)**, not $q_i > r$.
> 5. **In mapping questions**, students list *only* the biology side and *omit* the algorithmic side — half-marks lost.
> 6. **Schema-theorem proofs** get partial credit if the student writes the *selection* term but skips the *crossover* and *mutation* survival terms.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024**

- [ ] **GA = Population-based + Stochastic + Fitness-driven** (three-word exam-ready definition).
- [ ] **Father of GAs:** John Henry Holland (1975); popularized by David Goldberg (1989).
- [ ] **Inspiration:** Darwinian natural selection + Mendelian genetics.
- [ ] **Chromosome length $L$:** number of decision variables encoded.
- [ ] **Population size $N$:** typically $20$ to $200$ — large enough for diversity, small enough for speed.
- [ ] **Roulette-wheel selection probability:** $p_i = \frac{f(x_i)}{\sum f(x_j)}$.
- [ ] **Crossover probability $p_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$**; **mutation probability $p_m \in [0.001, 0.1]$**.
- [ ] **Elitism:** Copy the top $n_{elitism}$ (usually 1-5) chromosomes directly to the next generation.
- [ ] **Schema Theorem:** short, low-order, high-fitness schemata grow exponentially.
- [ ] **Building Block Hypothesis:** GA assembles solutions from short useful gene-patterns.
- [ ] **Encoding types:** binary (classic), real-valued, integer, permutation (for ordering problems).
- [ ] **Termination criteria:** (i) max generations, (ii) fitness convergence, (iii) time budget.
- [ ] **Termination of convergence** can be detected by $\sigma_f(t) \to 0$ (zero population diversity).
- [ ] **GAs do NOT guarantee global optimum** — they are *heuristic* and provide near-optimal solutions.
- [ ] **Distinct from simulated annealing:** GAs work on populations; SA works on a single point.
- [ ] **Distinct from gradient descent:** GAs need no differentiability; GD needs a smooth landscape.
- [ ] **GA applications in engineering:** scheduling, VLSI design, neural-net training, robotics, finance.
- [ ] **Key strengths:** derivative-free, parallelizable, robust to noise, multi-modal handling.
- [ ] **Key weaknesses:** computationally expensive, slow convergence on tightly coupled problems, parameter tuning sensitive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always define all symbols *before* writing a formula. State the *three operator names* (selection, crossover, mutation) whenever a GA question is asked — examiners specifically award marks for naming these operators explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coding, Selection, Crossover, and Mutation: Basic GA Structure and Operators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT635 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Coding, Selection, Crossover, and Mutation: Basic GA Structure and Operators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Genetic Algorithm (GA)?

A **Genetic Algorithm (GA)** is a stochastic, population-based metaheuristic optimization technique inspired by the principles of **natural selection** and **genetics** (Darwinian evolution). It belongs to the broader class of **Evolutionary Algorithms (EAs)** and is a foundational pillar of **Soft Computing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Goldberg, 1989):**
> A Genetic Algorithm is a search procedure that mimics the process of natural evolution. It operates on a population of candidate solutions (called *individuals* or *chromosomes*), iteratively applying genetic operators — **Selection, Crossover, and Mutation** — to evolve better solutions over successive generations.

### 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "Survival of the Fittest"

Imagine a **bunch of animals on an island**, each with a different trait. The animals that are best adapted to the environment **survive and reproduce** more often. Their offspring inherit (and slightly vary) their traits. Over many generations, the population becomes progressively better adapted.

In GA terms:
- An **animal** = a candidate **solution** (chromosome).
- **Trait / DNA** = encoded **decision variable** (gene).
- **Survival in environment** = **fitness** in solving the problem.
- **Reproduction with variation** = **crossover + mutation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:**
> GAs differ from classical optimization in **three** crucial ways: (1) They work on a *population* of points, not a single point. (2) They use *probabilistic* transition rules, not deterministic ones. (3) They require only an *objective/fitness function*, not derivatives or gradient information.

## 1.3 The Five Pillars of a Basic GA Structure

A canonical GA consists of **five sequential stages**:

| Stage | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **1. Coding / Encoding** | Map real-world variables into a chromosome representation (binary, real, permutation). |
| **2. Initialization** | Randomly generate the initial population of chromosomes. |
| **3. Fitness Evaluation** | Score each chromosome using the objective function. |
| **4. Genetic Operators** | Apply Selection → Crossover → Mutation to produce offspring. |
| **5. Replacement** | Form the next generation; check termination. |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to Remember:** "**C**hromosomes **I**nherit **F**antastic **O**ffspring **R**eliably" → **C**oding, **I**nitialization, **F**itness, **O**perators, **R**eplacement.

---

## 1.4 Coding (Encoding Schemes)

**Coding** is the process of translating a candidate solution from its problem-domain representation (e.g., a real number) into a chromosome representation that the GA can manipulate.

| Encoding Type | Chromosome Form | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Binary Encoding** | String of 0s and 1s | Classic combinatorial/discrete problems. |
| **Real-Valued (Float) Encoding** | Vector of real numbers | Continuous numerical optimization. |
| **Permutation Encoding** | Ordered list of integers | TSP, scheduling, routing. |
| **Tree Encoding** | Hierarchical tree of symbols | Genetic Programming (GP). |

For a real-valued variable $x \in [x_{\min},\, x_{\max}]$ using **L-bit binary encoding**, the decoding formula is:

$$
x = x_{\min} + \frac{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}{2^{L} - 1} \cdot D
$$

where $D = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} b_i \cdot 2^{i}$ is the integer decoded value of the binary string $b_{L-1} \dots b_1 b_0$.

> [!EXAMPLE]
> If $L = 5$, $x \in [0, 31]$, and chromosome is `11010` → $D = 26$ → $x = 0 + \frac{31-0}{31} \cdot 26 = 26$.

---

## 1.5 Overview of the Three Genetic Operators

1. **Selection** — Acts as the "Darwinian filter." Chromosomes with higher fitness are given a greater chance of being chosen as parents for the next generation. It implements the *survival of the fittest* principle.
2. **Crossover** — Mimics biological recombination. Two parent chromosomes exchange segments of their genetic material to produce one or two offspring. It is the **primary search mechanism** of the GA.
3. **Mutation** — Introduces small, random alterations in a chromosome (e.g., flipping a bit). It maintains **genetic diversity** and prevents premature convergence to local optima.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection vs. Crossover vs. Mutation — The Tug of War:**
> - **Selection** *exploits* good genetic material.
> - **Crossover** *explores* by combining good material.
> - **Mutation** *preserves diversity* and escapes local optima.
> A well-tuned GA balances all three.

---

## 1.6 Initialization

The initial population of $N$ chromosomes is usually generated **uniformly at random** over the feasible search space. For binary encoding, each bit is set to 0 or 1 with equal probability $0.5$. The population size $N$ is a key parameter — small $N$ causes loss of diversity, large $N$ causes slow convergence.

---

## 1.7 Termination Criteria

The GA loop stops when **any one** of the following conditions is satisfied:
- A **maximum number of generations** is reached.
- A **fitness threshold** is met (e.g., $f_{\text{best}} \geq f_{\text{target}}$).
- The **population converges** (e.g., all chromosomes become identical).
- **No improvement** is observed over a fixed number of generations (stagnation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Each Operator

### 2.1.1 Selection Operators

Selection assigns a **reproduction probability** to each chromosome, proportional (or related) to its fitness. The selected chromosomes form the **mating pool** for crossover.

#### A. Roulette Wheel Selection (Fitness Proportionate Selection)

Each chromosome occupies a slot on a virtual "roulette wheel" whose arc-length is proportional to its fitness. A uniformly random spin then selects parents.

For the $i$-th chromosome in a population of size $N$:

$$
p_i = \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}
$$

The **cumulative probability** $q_i$ used in the selection procedure is:

$$
q_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} p_j
$$

A random number $r \in [0, 1]$ is generated; the chromosome whose cumulative probability bracket contains $r$ is selected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** Roulette wheel is **highly sensitive to fitness scaling**. A single super-fit individual can dominate the entire population (premature convergence). Conversely, when fitness values are very close (e.g., near convergence), the selection pressure becomes almost zero (stagnation). These issues are mitigated by **fitness scaling** (linear, sigma, or Boltzmann).

#### B. Tournament Selection

Randomly pick $k$ chromosomes (the tournament size, typically $k = 2$ or $3$); the fittest among them wins and enters the mating pool. The selection probability for the $i$-th chromosome in a tournament of size $k$ is:

$$
P_i = \frac{1}{N^k} \left[ N^k - (N - i)^k \right] \quad \text{(rank-based, after sorting by fitness)}
$$

For $k=2$ (binary tournament):

$$
P_i = \frac{2i - 1}{N^2}
$$

where $i$ is the rank of the chromosome (1 = worst, $N$ = best).

> [!TIP]
> **Why Tournament is Preferred:** It does not require global fitness normalization, scales well, and the selection pressure is tunable via $k$ without altering the objective function.

#### C. Rank-Based Selection

Chromosomes are sorted by fitness and assigned selection probabilities based on their **rank** $i$, not raw fitness. A common form (linear ranking):

$$
p_i = \frac{1}{N}\left[\alpha - (\alpha - \beta)\cdot \frac{i - 1}{N - 1}\right]
$$

where $\alpha + \beta = 2$ and typically $\alpha \in [1.0, 2.0]$; the best chromosome has rank 1, the worst has rank $N$.

> [!NOTE]
> Rank-based selection is **scale-invariant** — it works the same way whether fitness values are 0.001 or 1,000,000.

#### D. Elitism (A Selection Augmentation, NOT a Selection Method)

A pure GA can lose the best chromosome due to stochastic operators. **Elitism** copies the top-$E$ chromosomes directly into the next generation unchanged, guaranteeing that the best-so-far fitness never decreases monotonically.

---

### 2.1.2 Crossover Operators

Crossover combines the genetic material of two parents to produce offspring. Crossover is the **primary exploration engine** of a GA.

#### A. Single-Point Crossover

A cut point $c \in \{1, 2, \dots, L-1\}$ is chosen randomly. The two parents exchange the rightmost (or leftmost) segment beyond the cut.

$$
\text{Parent 1: } 1\,0\,1\,\vert\,1\,0 \qquad \text{Parent 2: } 0\,1\,0\,\vert\,0\,1
$$

$$
\text{Offspring 1: } 1\,0\,1\,0\,1 \qquad \text{Offspring 2: } 0\,1\,0\,1\,0
$$

#### B. Multi-Point Crossover

Two or more cut points are chosen; genetic material is exchanged in alternating segments. For $m$ cut points, the schemata disruption is higher than single-point.

#### C. Uniform Crossover

Each gene position is independently and randomly chosen from either parent with probability $0.5$. The offspring gene at position $i$ is:

$$
o_i = \begin{cases} p^{(1)}_i & \text{with prob. } 0.5 \\ p^{(2)}_i & \text{with prob. } 0.5 \end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Uniform crossover has the **highest schema disruption** and is most exploratory, but it can be slower to converge on tightly linked building blocks.

#### D. Arithmetic Crossover (Real-Valued)

For real-valued chromosomes $P_1$ and $P_2$:

$$
O_1 = \lambda P_1 + (1 - \lambda) P_2, \quad O_2 = (1 - \lambda) P_1 + \lambda P_2
$$

where $\lambda \in [0, 1]$ is a random weight (or fixed, e.g., $\lambda = 0.5$ for *blend* crossover).

#### E. Crossover Probability $p_c$

Crossover is applied with probability $p_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$. With probability $1 - p_c$, the parents are copied verbatim into the offspring (this is technically *no crossover* — sometimes treated as a "reproduction only" path).

---

### 2.1.3 Mutation Operators

Mutation makes small, random alterations to a chromosome, introducing **genetic diversity** that crossover alone cannot produce. It is a **secondary search mechanism** — used sparingly.

#### A. Bit-Flip Mutation (Binary)

Each bit of an offspring is independently flipped with a small probability $p_m$ (typically $p_m \in [0.001, 0.05]$).

$$
b_i' = \begin{cases} 1 - b_i & \text{with prob. } p_m \\ b_i & \text{with prob. } 1 - p_m \end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** For a chromosome of length $L$, the *expected number* of mutated bits is $L \cdot p_m$. A good setting produces ~1 mutation per chromosome on average (so $p_m \approx 1/L$).

#### B. Gaussian Mutation (Real-Valued)

For a real-valued gene $x_i$:

$$
x_i' = x_i + \mathcal{N}(0,\, \sigma_i^2)
$$

where $\sigma_i$ is the mutation step size, often adapted via *self-adaptation* or *1/5 success rule*.

#### C. Swap / Scramble / Inversion Mutation (Permutation)

Used for permutation chromosomes (e.g., TSP): swap two random positions, scramble a random sub-sequence, or reverse a sub-sequence.

---

### 2.1.4 Replacement Strategies

After offspring are produced, the new population is formed:

| Strategy | Description |
|---|---|
| **Generational Replacement** | The entire population is replaced by offspring each generation. |
| **Steady-State Replacement** | Only a few offspring per generation replace the worst (or via tournament) chromosomes. |
| **(μ + λ) Strategy** | Best μ individuals survive from the union of μ parents and λ offspring. |
| **(μ, λ) Strategy** | Best μ individuals survive from the λ offspring only. |

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is your one-stop formula reference for the KTU board exam.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Binary → Decimal decoding** | $D = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} b_i \cdot 2^{i}$ | $b_i \in \{0, 1\}$ |
| 2 | **Real variable from binary** | $x = x_{\min} + \dfrac{(x_{\max} - x_{\min})}{2^L - 1} \cdot D$ | Linear mapping to interval |
| 3 | **Fitness proportion (Roulette)** | $p_i = \dfrac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$ | Requires $f_i \geq 0$ |
| 4 | **Cumulative probability** | $q_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} p_j$ | Used for $O(1)$ selection |
| 5 | **Expected count (Roulette)** | $e_i = N \cdot p_i$ | Number of copies expected |
| 6 | **Binary tournament probability** | $P_i = \dfrac{2i - 1}{N^{2}}$ | $i$ = rank, $1 \leq i \leq N$ |
| 7 | **Linear ranking probability** | $p_i = \dfrac{1}{N}\left[\alpha - (\alpha - \beta)\dfrac{i-1}{N-1}\right]$ | $\alpha + \beta = 2$ |
| 8 | **Crossover probability** | $p_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$ | Hyperparameter |
| 9 | **Mutation probability** | $p_m \in [0.001, 0.05]$ | Hyperparameter |
| 10 | **Expected mutated bits per chromosome** | $E = L \cdot p_m$ | Rule of thumb: $E \approx 1$ |
| 11 | **Schema survival under single-point crossover** | $p_s \geq 1 - \dfrac{l(s)}{L - 1}$ | $l(s)$ = schema length |
| 12 | **Schema survival under mutation** | $p_s = (1 - p_m)^{o(s)}$ | $o(s)$ = schema order |

> **Note on schema theory notation:** A *schema* is a similarity template over chromosomes. The **Schema Theorem** (Holland, 1975) states that short, low-order, above-average fitness schemata receive exponentially increasing trials — this is the *Building Block Hypothesis*.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace / Mechanical Design** | Optimizing airfoil shapes, structural topology, turbine blade profiles. |
| **VLSI Circuit Design** | Optimal placement & routing of components on chips. |
| **Robotics & Control** | Evolving neural network weights, walking gait synthesis. |
| **Scheduling (Operations Research)** | Job-shop scheduling, airline crew rostering. |
| **Bioinformatics** | Protein structure prediction, DNA sequence alignment. |
| **Finance** | Portfolio optimization, trading rule discovery. |
| **Telecommunications** | Antenna design, network routing. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked-Out Example

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example — Maximizing f(x) = x² on [0, 31]

This is the **textbook "maximization of x²"** problem used universally in GA literature. We will walk through **one complete generation** from start to finish with $L = 5$ binary bits, $N = 4$ population, single-point crossover at bit-2, and a single bit-flip mutation per chromosome.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this example?**
> It is a closed-form, easily verifiable, and pedagogically complete demonstration of every GA operator. Examiners love it because it exposes every arithmetic mistake.

### Step 0: Problem Setup

$$
\text{Maximize } f(x) = x^{2}, \quad x \in [0,\, 31], \quad L = 5 \text{ bits}, \quad N = 4
$$

Encoding: $x = D$ (since $x_{\min} = 0$, $x_{\max} = 31$, and the mapping is direct).

### Step 1: Initial Population (Randomly Generated)

| Chromosome # | String (b₄b₃b₂b₁b₀) | Decoded $D$ | $x = D$ | Fitness $f(x) = x^{2}$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | 0 1 1 0 1 | 13 | 13 | **169** |
| 2 | 1 1 0 0 0 | 24 | 24 | **576** |
| 3 | 0 1 0 0 0 | 8  | 8  | **64**  |
| 4 | 1 0 0 1 1 | 19 | 19 | **361** |
| | | | **Total Fitness $\Sigma f$** | **1170** |

### Step 2: Fitness Proportion (Selection Probability)

Using $p_i = f_i / \Sigma f$:

$$
p_1 = \frac{169}{1170} = 0.1444
$$

$$
p_2 = \frac{576}{1170} = 0.4923
$$

$$
p_3 = \frac{64}{1170} = 0.0547
$$

$$
p_4 = \frac{361}{1170} = 0.3085
$$

**Verification of probabilities:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} p_i = 0.1444 + 0.4923 + 0.0547 + 0.3085 = 1.0000 \;\; \checkmark
$$

### Step 3: Cumulative Probability $q_i$

$$
q_1 = p_1 = 0.1444
$$

$$
q_2 = p_1 + p_2 = 0.1444 + 0.4923 = 0.6368
$$

$$
q_3 = p_1 + p_2 + p_3 = 0.1444 + 0.4923 + 0.0547 = 0.6915
$$

$$
q_4 = p_1 + p_2 + p_3 + p_4 = 1.0000
$$

### Step 4: Roulette Wheel Spin

Generate four uniformly distributed random numbers $r_1, r_2, r_3, r_4 \in [0, 1]$:

$$
r_1 = 0.265, \quad r_2 = 0.752, \quad r_3 = 0.413, \quad r_4 = 0.918
$$

Selection rule: pick chromosome $i$ such that $q_{i-1} < r \leq q_i$ (with $q_0 = 0$).

| Spin $r$ | Bracket | Selected Chromosome | String |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0.265 | $0.1444 < 0.265 \leq 0.6368$ | #2 | 1 1 0 0 0 |
| 0.752 | $0.6915 < 0.752 \leq 1.0000$ | #4 | 1 0 0 1 1 |
| 0.413 | $0.1444 < 0.413 \leq 0.6368$ | #2 | 1 1 0 0 0 |
| 0.918 | $0.6915 < 0.918 \leq 1.0000$ | #4 | 1 0 0 1 1 |

**Mating Pool (Mating Pool Size = 4):**

| Slot | Chromosome |
|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | 1 1 0 0 0  |
| 2 | 1 0 0 1 1  |
| 3 | 1 1 0 0 0  |
| 4 | 1 0 0 1 1  |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that the **fittest chromosome #2 was selected twice**, while the **weakest chromosome #3 was eliminated**. This is the essence of selection pressure.

### Step 5: Crossover (Single-Point at Bit 2)

Pair the mating pool as (Slot 1 × Slot 2) and (Slot 3 × Slot 4). Cut point = bit position 2 (between b₄b₃ | b₂b₁b₀).

**Pair 1: `11000` × `10011` at cut-point 2**

$$
\text{Parent 1: } \underbrace{1\,1}_{P1} \vert \underbrace{0\,0\,0}_{P1}
\quad\quad
\text{Parent 2: } \underbrace{1\,0}_{P2} \vert \underbrace{0\,1\,1}_{P2}
$$

$$
\text{Offspring A: } 1\,1\,0\,1\,1 = 27
$$

$$
\text{Offspring B: } 1\,0\,0\,0\,0 = 16
$$

**Pair 2: `11000` × `10011` at cut-point 2**

$$
\text{Offspring C: } 1\,1\,0\,1\,1 = 27
$$

$$
\text{Offspring D: } 1\,0\,0\,0\,0 = 16
$$

**Offspring Pool:**

| # | String | Decoded | Fitness $x^{2}$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| A | 1 1 0 1 1 | 27 | **729** |
| B | 1 0 0 0 0 | 16 | **256** |
| C | 1 1 0 1 1 | 27 | **729** |
| D | 1 0 0 0 0 | 16 | **256** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Already, **two new chromosomes with fitness 729** have appeared, **exceeding the best parent fitness of 576**. This demonstrates the *constructive* power of crossover.

### Step 6: Mutation (Bit-Flip)

Apply a single bit-flip to each offspring (assume $p_m = 0.05 \cdot 5 = $ ~1 bit per chromosome on average; we flip exactly one bit per offspring for illustration).

| Offspring | Bit Flipped | New String | New $x$ | New $x^{2}$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| A: 11011 | b₂ (3rd bit) | 1 1 **1** 1 1 | 31 | **961** |
| B: 10000 | b₄ (1st bit) | **0** 0 0 0 0 | 0  | **0**   |
| C: 11011 | b₁ (4th bit) | 1 1 0 **0** 1 | 25 | **625** |
| D: 10000 | b₃ (2nd bit) | 1 **1** 0 0 0 | 24 | **576** |

### Step 7: New Generation Summary

| # | Final Chromosome | $x$ | $f(x) = x^{2}$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | 1 1 1 1 1 | 31 | **961**  |
| 2 | 0 0 0 0 0 | 0  | **0**    |
| 3 | 1 1 0 0 1 | 25 | **625**  |
| 4 | 1 1 0 0 0 | 24 | **576**  |
| | | **Average** | **540.5** |
| | | **Best** | **961**  |

> [!TIP]
> **Generation 0** had best fitness **576** and average **292.5**.
> **Generation 1** has best fitness **961** and average **540.5**.
> Average fitness has nearly **doubled**, and the best fitness is **66% higher** — the GA is clearly progressing.

### Step 8: Convergence Check (Termination Test)

In the second generation, chromosome `11111` ($x = 31$) is the global optimum of $f(x) = x^2$ on $[0, 31]$ (since $f(31) = 961$ is the maximum). The GA has converged in just **one iteration** for this trivial problem. For harder problems, the loop would continue.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Operator Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a high-frequency board exam question (often Part-A worth 3 marks).**

| Criterion | Selection | Crossover | Mutation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Role** | Exploitation (intensification) | Exploration (recombination) | Exploration (diversity injection) |
| **Operates on** | Population of parents | Pair of parents | Single offspring |
| **Probability** | Implicit (function of fitness) | $p_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$ | $p_m \in [0.001, 0.05]$ |
| **Input** | Population | 2 parents | 1 offspring |
| **Output** | Mating pool | 1 or 2 offspring | Modified offspring |
| **Information used** | Fitness values | Genetic content of parents | Random perturbation |
| **Analogy** | Natural selection | Sexual reproduction | Genetic drift / radiation |
| **Risk if too high** | Premature convergence | Loss of building blocks | Random search |
| **Risk if too low** | Stagnation | Slow search | Premature convergence |

---

## 3.3 Crossover Operator Worked Example — Uniform Crossover

Parents: $P_1 = 10110$, $P_2 = 01101$. Generate a binary mask of length 5: $M = 10101$.

| Position $i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $P_1$ | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| $P_2$ | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| Mask $M$ | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| Rule | Take $P_1$ | Take $P_2$ | Take $P_1$ | Take $P_2$ | Take $P_1$ |
| **Offspring $O_1$** | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |

Similarly, the complementary mask $\bar M = 01010$ gives:

$$
O_2 = 0\,0\,1\,1\,1
$$

---

## 3.4 Mutation Probability Derivation — "1-Bit-Per-Chromosome" Rule

If the GA is to introduce, on average, **one mutated bit per chromosome**, then:

$$
E[\text{mutations per chromosome}] = L \cdot p_m = 1
$$

Solving for $p_m$:

$$
p_m = \frac{1}{L}
$$

For $L = 20$: $p_m = 0.05$. For $L = 100$: $p_m = 0.01$. For $L = 200$: $p_m = 0.005$.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the rationale behind the canonical "1/L" mutation rate heuristic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level GA Flowchart

This is the canonical flow of a basic generational GA, exactly as it would be drawn on the KTU board.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Read Problem Define Objective f x] --> B[Choose Encoding Scheme Binary Real Permutation]
    B --> C[Set GA Parameters N pc pm MaxGen]
    C --> D[Generate Initial Population P of t = 0]
    D --> E[Evaluate Fitness f x for each chromosome in P]
    E --> F{Termination Criteria Met MaxGen or f best f target}
    F -->|No| G[SELECTION: Build Mating Pool via Roulette or Tournament]
    G --> H[CROSSOVER: Apply single-point or uniform with prob pc]
    H --> I[MUTATION: Bit-flip or Gaussian with prob pm]
    I --> J[REPLACEMENT: Form New Population P of t+1]
    J --> K[t = t + 1]
    K --> E
    F -->|Yes| L[OUTPUT: Best Chromosome and its Fitness]
    L --> M[END]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a GA Operator Pipeline

This block diagram details the **information flow** between the genetic operators and the data structures.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INPUT
        InitPop[Initial Population P t of size N]
    end
    subgraph STAGE1_EVAL
        FitEval[Fitness Evaluation Module f x]
        FitVec[Fitness Vector f1 f2 ... fN]
    end
    subgraph STAGE2_SELECTION
        SelMod[Selection Module Roulette or Tournament]
        MatPool[Mating Pool of size N]
    end
    subgraph STAGE3_CROSSOVER
        XOverMod[Single-Point Crossover pc]
        Off1[Offspring Pool]
    end
    subgraph STAGE4_MUTATION
        MutMod[Bit-Flip Mutation pm]
        Off2[Mutated Offspring]
    end
    subgraph STAGE5_REPLACE
        RepMod[Replacement Strategy Generational or Elitist]
        NewPop[New Population P t+1]
    end
    InitPop --> FitEval
    FitEval --> FitVec
    FitVec --> SelMod
    SelMod --> MatPool
    MatPool --> XOverMod
    XOverMod --> Off1
    Off1 --> MutMod
    MutMod --> Off2
    Off2 --> RepMod
    RepMod --> NewPop
    NewPop -.-> FitEval
```

## 4.3 Chromosome Anatomy — Gene, Allele, Locus

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CHROMOSOME_5_BIT
        direction LR
        G1[Locus 1 b4]
        G2[Locus 2 b3]
        G3[Locus 3 b2]
        G4[Locus 4 b1]
        G5[Locus 5 b0]
        V1((Allele 1))
        V2((Allele 0))
        V3((Allele 1))
        V4((Allele 0))
        V5((Allele 1))
    end
    G1 --- V1
    G2 --- V2
    G3 --- V3
    G4 --- V4
    G5 --- V5
```

## 4.4 Crossover Mechanics — Single-Point Cut at Position 2

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PARENTS
        P1[Parent 1: 1 1 0 0 0]
        P2[Parent 2: 1 0 0 1 1]
    end
    subgraph CUT
        C1[Cut Point at Locus 2]
    end
    subgraph OFFSPRING
        O1[Offspring 1: 1 1 0 1 1]
        O2[Offspring 2: 1 0 0 0 0]
    end
    P1 --> C1
    P2 --> C1
    C1 --> O1
    C1 --> O2
```

## 4.5 Selection Pressure Comparison — Decision Aid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Choose Selection Method] --> R{High Selection Pressure Needed}
    R -->|Yes| S[Tournament with k = 3 or higher]
    R -->|No| T[Roulette Wheel with Fitness Scaling]
    Q --> U{Fitness Values May Be Negative or Close}
    U -->|Yes| V[Rank-Based or Tournament]
    U -->|No| W[Roulette Wheel Acceptable]
    Q --> X{Parallel Implementation Required}
    X -->|Yes| Y[Tournament Naturally Parallelizable]
    X -->|No| Z[Any Method Acceptable]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Genetic Algorithm. List and briefly explain the basic genetic operators. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A Genetic Algorithm (GA) is a population-based, stochastic search and optimization algorithm inspired by the principles of natural selection and genetics. It maintains a population of candidate solutions (chromosomes), evaluates their fitness, and iteratively applies genetic operators to evolve better solutions over generations.

The **three basic genetic operators** are:

1. **Selection** — Chooses parents from the current population with probability proportional (or related) to their fitness, mimicking the "survival of the fittest" principle. Examples: Roulette Wheel, Tournament, Rank-based.
2. **Crossover** — Recombines the genetic material of two parents at randomly chosen cut points (or per-gene basis in uniform crossover) to produce offspring. It is the primary exploration mechanism. Examples: Single-point, Multi-point, Uniform, Arithmetic.
3. **Mutation** — Makes small, random modifications (e.g., bit-flips) to offspring chromosomes with a low probability $p_m$. It preserves genetic diversity and prevents premature convergence. Examples: Bit-flip (binary), Gaussian (real-valued), Swap/Inversion (permutation).

---

### Q2. Distinguish between binary encoding and real-valued encoding in a Genetic Algorithm. When is each preferred? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Binary Encoding | Real-Valued Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| **Chromosome form** | String of 0s and 1s | Vector of real numbers |
| **Search space** | Discrete grid of $2^L$ points | Continuous space |
| **Operators** | Bit-flip mutation, single/multi-point crossover | Gaussian mutation, arithmetic/Blend crossover |
| **Precision** | Limited by $L$ (more bits = more precision) | Native precision of floating-point arithmetic |
| **Hamming cliff** | Suffers from Hamming cliffs (e.g., 0111 → 1000) | No Hamming cliffs |
| **Preferred for** | Combinatorial problems, discrete optimization, theoretical GA studies | Continuous numerical optimization, high-dimensional problems |

**Binary encoding** is preferred for discrete/combinatorial problems and for theoretical analysis. **Real-valued encoding** is preferred for continuous optimization where high precision and a large search space are required.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the basic structure of a Genetic Algorithm. Discuss the role of each component in the optimization process. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A Genetic Algorithm follows an iterative cycle of population evaluation and evolution. The basic structure is:

**Step 1 — Encoding (Coding):** Decision variables of the problem are mapped into a chromosome representation (e.g., binary string, real-valued vector, or permutation). For a real variable $x \in [x_{\min}, x_{\max}]$, binary encoding uses an $L$-bit string decoded via:

$$
x = x_{\min} + \frac{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}{2^{L} - 1} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} b_i \cdot 2^{i}
$$

**Role:** Encoding defines the *search space granularity* and the *operator semantics*.

**Step 2 — Initialization:** A population of $N$ chromosomes is generated uniformly at random within the feasible region. The population provides the initial *sample* of the search space.

**Step 3 — Fitness Evaluation:** Each chromosome is decoded back into a solution and scored using the objective function $f(x)$. Fitness drives selection pressure.

**Step 4 — Selection:** Chromosomes with higher fitness are preferentially chosen to form the *mating pool*. Common methods: Roulette Wheel, Tournament, Rank-based. **Role:** Exploitation — propagates good genetic material.

**Step 5 — Crossover:** Pairs of parents from the mating pool exchange genetic material (single-point, multi-point, or uniform) with probability $p_c$ to produce offspring. **Role:** Exploration — combines building blocks from different parents.

**Step 6 — Mutation:** Offspring undergo small random changes (bit-flips, Gaussian perturbations) with low probability $p_m$. **Role:** Maintains diversity and prevents premature convergence.

**Step 7 — Replacement:** A new generation is formed from the offspring (and possibly elite parents), and the cycle repeats from Step 3.

**Termination:** The loop stops on a maximum generation count, a fitness threshold, or convergence.

*(Diagram: refer to the Mermaid flow chart in Section 4.1.)*

The cycle is a closed-loop of evaluation → selection → variation → replacement, with the *fittest* chromosomes progressively dominating the population.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain Roulette Wheel selection in detail. For a population of 4 chromosomes with fitness values 10, 50, 30, 10, compute the selection probability and cumulative probability of each. If random numbers 0.05, 0.42, 0.78, 0.96 are generated, identify the selected chromosomes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Roulette Wheel Selection Theory:** In fitness-proportionate (roulette wheel) selection, each chromosome is assigned a slot on a virtual roulette wheel whose arc-length is proportional to its fitness. A uniformly random spin $r \in [0, 1]$ then picks a parent. The selection probability of the $i$-th chromosome is:

$$
p_i = \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}
$$

The **cumulative probability** $q_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} p_j$ is used to identify which chromosome a random number selects.

**Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Fitness:**

$$
\sum f = 10 + 50 + 30 + 10 = 100
$$

**Step 2 — Selection Probabilities:**

$$
p_1 = \frac{10}{100} = 0.10, \quad p_2 = \frac{50}{100} = 0.50, \quad p_3 = \frac{30}{100} = 0.30, \quad p_4 = \frac{10}{100} = 0.10
$$

**Step 3 — Cumulative Probabilities:**

$$
q_1 = 0.10, \quad q_2 = 0.10 + 0.50 = 0.60, \quad q_3 = 0.60 + 0.30 = 0.90, \quad q_4 = 0.90 + 0.10 = 1.00
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Random Numbers** (rule: $q_{i-1} < r \leq q_i$, with $q_0 = 0$):

| Random $r$ | Bracket | Selected Chromosome |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0.05 | $0.00 < 0.05 \leq 0.10$ | Chromosome **#1** (fitness 10) |
| 0.42 | $0.10 < 0.42 \leq 0.60$ | Chromosome **#2** (fitness 50) |
| 0.78 | $0.60 < 0.78 \leq 0.90$ | Chromosome **#3** (fitness 30) |
| 0.96 | $0.90 < 0.96 \leq 1.00$ | Chromosome **#4** (fitness 10) |

**Resulting Mating Pool:** { #1, #2, #3, #4 }.

> [!NOTE]
> The fittest chromosome #2 was selected once, #3 (second-best) once, and the weakest chromosomes #1 and #4 were also selected (since $r$ fell into their brackets). The mating pool is a *random sample* weighted by fitness.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the single-point crossover and uniform crossover operators with suitable examples. What is the role of crossover probability $p_c$? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Single-Point Crossover:**
- A single cut point $c \in \{1, 2, \dots, L-1\}$ is chosen uniformly at random.
- The two parents exchange the genetic segment *beyond* the cut point.
- Produces two offspring.

**Example** (L = 6, cut at position 3):

$$
P_1 = 1\,0\,1\,|\,1\,0\,0, \quad P_2 = 0\,1\,0\,|\,0\,1\,1
$$

$$
O_1 = 1\,0\,1\,0\,1\,1, \quad O_2 = 0\,1\,0\,1\,0\,0
$$

**Uniform Crossover:**
- For each gene position $i \in \{1, \dots, L\}$, a fair coin (probability 0.5) is flipped.
- The offspring's $i$-th gene is taken from parent #1 if the coin shows "head", else from parent #2.

**Example** (L = 5, mask $M = 10101$):

$$
P_1 = 1\,0\,1\,1\,0, \quad P_2 = 0\,1\,1\,0\,1, \quad M = 1\,0\,1\,0\,1
$$

$$
O_1 = 1\,1\,1\,0\,0 \quad \text{(take } P_1 \text{ where } M=1,\; P_2 \text{ where } M=0\text{)}
$$

$$
O_2 = 0\,0\,1\,1\,1 \quad \text{(complementary mask } \bar M = 0\,1\,0\,1\,0\text{)}
$$

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | Single-Point | Uniform |
|---|---|---|
| Cut points | 1 | 0 (per-gene) |
| Schema disruption | Lower for short schemata | Higher (no positional bias) |
| Convergence speed | Faster | Slower but more thorough |
| Implementation | Simple | Simple |

**Role of Crossover Probability $p_c$:**
$p_c \in [0, 1]$ is the probability that a pair of parents undergoes crossover. With probability $1 - p_c$, the parents are copied unchanged (analogous to asexual reproduction). Typical value: $p_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$.

- **High $p_c$** → aggressive exploration, faster mixing of building blocks, but may destroy good schemata.
- **Low $p_c$** → conservative, preserves good chromosomes, but slow convergence.

A well-tuned $p_c$ balances exploration and exploitation.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain bit-flip mutation operator. For two offspring strings A = `10110` and B = `01001`, apply bit-flip mutation with $p_m = 0.2$ at positions 2 and 4 for A and positions 1 and 5 for B. Show the resulting chromosomes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Bit-Flip Mutation Theory:**
Each bit of a chromosome is independently flipped (0 → 1 or 1 → 0) with probability $p_m$. This introduces small, random variations that help the GA explore new regions of the search space and maintain genetic diversity. Typical $p_m \in [0.001, 0.05]$ for binary GAs.

The mathematical operation at each bit is:

$$
b_i' = \begin{cases} 1 - b_i & \text{with probability } p_m \\ b_i & \text{with probability } 1 - p_m \end{cases}
$$

**Numerical Application:**

**Offspring A = `1 0 1 1 0`** (L = 5):

- **Position 2:** Current bit = 0 → Flip → 1
- **Position 4:** Current bit = 1 → Flip → 0

Mutation result A′ = `1 1 1 0 0`

**Offspring B = `0 1 0 0 1`** (L = 5):

- **Position 1:** Current bit = 0 → Flip → 1
- **Position 5:** Current bit = 1 → Flip → 0

Mutation result B′ = `1 1 0 0 0`

**Summary Table:**

| Offspring | Original | Mutated Bits | Mutated String | Decoded (if binary) |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| A | 1 0 1 1 0 | b₂: 0→1, b₄: 1→0 | 1 1 1 0 0 | 28 |
| B | 0 1 0 0 1 | b₁: 0→1, b₅: 1→0 | 1 1 0 0 0 | 24 |

> [!NOTE]
> Bit-flip mutation is the *binary* equivalent of "small random perturbation" in continuous optimization. It is intentionally kept at a low probability to prevent the search from degenerating into pure random walk.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Cumulative Probability Boundary Mistakes (–2 marks):** Forgetting that the bracket is $q_{i-1} < r \leq q_i$ with $q_0 = 0$. A random number exactly equal to a boundary value (e.g., $r = 0.10$) belongs to chromosome $i$, not $i-1$. State this explicitly.
> 2. **Arithmetic Errors in Decoding (–2 marks):** When computing $D = \sum b_i 2^i$, remember the **rightmost bit** is the LSB ($2^0$), not the MSB. Always label bit positions.
> 3. **Confusing $p_c$ and $p_m$ semantics (–1 mark):** $p_c$ is the **pair-level** probability (applied to a pair of parents), while $p_m$ is the **per-bit** probability (applied independently to each bit). Do not mix them.
> 4. **Elitism Oversight (–1 mark):** When the question says "with elitism," do not show a generation where the best fitness drops. Elitism guarantees **monotonic non-decreasing** best fitness.
> 5. **Forgetting Probability Normalization Check (–1 mark):** Always end your roulette-wheel calculations with $\sum p_i = 1.000$. Examiners award 1 mark for this verification.
> 6. **Schematic Drawing Omission (–2 marks):** For 7-mark "explain with a flowchart" questions, a **neatly labeled block diagram** of the GA cycle is mandatory. Use rectangles for processes and diamonds for decisions.
> 7. **Roulette vs. Tournament Confusion (–1 mark):** Roulette wheel requires $f_i \geq 0$; tournament does not. State this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: GA Structure & Operators**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- **GA** = population-based, stochastic, derivative-free optimizer inspired by Darwinian evolution.
- **Chromosome** = encoded candidate solution; **Gene** = single decision variable bit/value; **Locus** = position; **Allele** = the value at a locus.
- **Fitness** = numerical score of a chromosome; higher fitness → higher chance of selection.

### 🔑 The Five Stages (in order)
1. **Encoding** — Binary / Real / Permutation / Tree.
2. **Initialization** — Random population of size $N$.
3. **Fitness Evaluation** — Score each chromosome.
4. **Genetic Operators** — Selection → Crossover → Mutation.
5. **Replacement** — Form new generation; check termination.

### 🔑 Selection (Exploitation)
- **Roulette Wheel:** $p_i = f_i / \Sigma f_j$. Simple but scale-sensitive.
- **Tournament:** Pick $k$, choose fittest. Scale-invariant; tunable pressure.
- **Rank-Based:** Probability based on rank, not raw fitness. Scale-invariant.
- **Elitism:** Always keep best $E$ chromosomes.

### 🔑 Crossover (Exploration via Recombination)
- **Single-Point:** One cut, exchange tail segments.
- **Multi-Point:** $m$ cuts, alternating exchange.
- **Uniform:** Per-gene coin flip.
- **Arithmetic (real):** Weighted average.
- Applied with probability $p_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$.

### 🔑 Mutation (Diversity Injection)
- **Bit-Flip (binary):** $b_i' = 1 - b_i$ with prob. $p_m$.
- **Gaussian (real):** $x_i' = x_i + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$.
- **Swap / Inversion (permutation):** For TSP-style problems.
- Applied with probability $p_m \in [0.001, 0.05]$; rule of thumb $p_m \approx 1/L$.

### 🔑 Key Formulas to Memorize

$$
p_i = \frac{f_i}{\sum f_j} \quad\quad q_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} p_j \quad\quad p_m \approx \frac{1}{L} \quad\quad x = x_{\min} + \frac{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}{2^{L} - 1} \cdot D
$$

### 🔑 Hyperparameter Rules of Thumb
| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect of ↑ |
|---|---|---|
| Population size $N$ | 50 – 200 | Better coverage, slower |
| Crossover prob. $p_c$ | 0.6 – 0.9 | More exploration |
| Mutation prob. $p_m$ | 0.001 – 0.05 | More diversity |
| Tournament size $k$ | 2 – 5 | Higher selection pressure |

### 🔑 Schema Theorem (Holland, 1975)
Short, low-order, above-average fitness schemata receive **exponentially increasing** trials — the theoretical foundation of GA's success.

### 🔑 One-Sentence Summary for Viva
*"A Genetic Algorithm is a population-based, stochastic search procedure that evolves a set of encoded candidate solutions through fitness-proportionate selection, genetic recombination, and random mutation, balancing exploration and exploitation to find near-optimal solutions without requiring gradient information."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybrid Systems: Concept of Neuro-Fuzzy Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT635 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hybrid Systems: Concept of Neuro-Fuzzy Systems
