# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the energy function of a Boltzmann Machine. Why is it fundamental to the network's operation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The energy function of a Boltzmann Machine with binary units $s_i \in \{0,1\}$ and symmetric weights $w_{ij}$ is given by:

$$E(\mathbf{s}) = -\sum_{i<j} w_{ij} s_i s_j - \sum_i b_i s_i$$

It is fundamental because it (i) defines the **scalar "energy landscape"** over all $2^n$ possible global states, (ii) determines the **probability** of each state through the Boltzmann distribution $P(\mathbf{s}) \propto \exp(-E(\mathbf{s})/T)$, and (iii) governs the **learning rule** — weights are updated to make observed states lower in energy than unobserved ones. Without the energy function, there is no principled way to assign probabilities to network states.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Mean Field approximation used in Boltzmann Machines and write its fixed-point equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Mean Field approximation** replaces each stochastic binary unit $s_i$ with its expected (mean) value $m_i = \langle s_i \rangle \in [-1, +1]$. This converts the intractable stochastic network into a tractable deterministic system. The fixed-point self-consistency equation is:

$$m_i = \tanh\!\left(\frac{1}{T}\sum_{j} w_{ij} m_j + \frac{b_i}{T}\right)$$

These equations are solved iteratively until convergence (when $\vert m_i^{(t+1)} - m_i^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon$ for all $i$). The result is an efficient approximation of the network's equilibrium statistics without Gibbs sampling or partition function evaluation.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** Students must answer ONE question from a choice of TWO. Each carries **14 marks**, typically split into two **7-mark** sub-parts (a) and (b). Show all derivation steps, definitions, and final boxed answers to earn full credit.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model Question]`

**a)** Define a Boltzmann Machine. Derive the Boltzmann distribution from the maximum entropy principle. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Explain the structure of the energy function for a Boltzmann Machine with both visible and hidden units. Discuss why the partition function $Z$ is computationally intractable and how this motivates the Mean Field approximation. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
A Boltzmann Machine is a symmetrically connected network of stochastic binary units whose global states follow the Boltzmann distribution from statistical mechanics. It is an energy-based generative model.

**Derivation (5 marks):**
We maximize Shannon entropy $H[P] = -\sum_{\mathbf{s}} P(\mathbf{s}) \ln P(\mathbf{s})$ subject to $\sum_{\mathbf{s}} P(\mathbf{s}) = 1$ and fixed expected energy $\langle E \rangle = U$.

Form the Lagrangian:
$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{\mathbf{s}} P(\mathbf{s}) \ln P(\mathbf{s}) - \lambda_0 \left(\sum_{\mathbf{s}} P(\mathbf{s}) - 1\right) - \beta \left(\sum_{\mathbf{s}} P(\mathbf{s}) E(\mathbf{s}) - U\right)$$

Setting $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial P(\mathbf{s}) = 0$:
$$\ln P(\mathbf{s}) = -1 - \lambda_0 - \beta E(\mathbf{s})$$

Exponentiating and normalizing with $Z = \sum_{\mathbf{s}} e^{-\beta E(\mathbf{s})}$:
$$\boxed{P(\mathbf{s}) = \frac{e^{-\beta E(\mathbf{s})}}{Z}, \quad \text{with } \beta = 1/T}$$

Identifying $\beta = 1/T$ recovers the **Boltzmann distribution**, which is the maximum-entropy distribution consistent with a given average energy.

#### Part (b) — Energy Structure & Intractability (7 marks)

**Energy with visible/hidden split (3 marks):**
$$E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = - \mathbf{v}^T W_{vv} \mathbf{v} - \mathbf{h}^T W_{hh} \mathbf{h} - \mathbf{v}^T W_{vh} \mathbf{h} - \mathbf{b}_v^T \mathbf{v} - \mathbf{b}_h^T \mathbf{h}$$

This decomposes the energy into four interaction terms: visible-visible, hidden-hidden, visible-hidden, and individual biases.

**Intractability of $Z$ (2 marks):**
The partition function $Z = \sum_{\mathbf{s}} e^{-E(\mathbf{s})/T}$ is a sum over **all $2^n$ global states**, which is exponential in the number of units. For $n=100$, this is $2^{100} \approx 10^{30}$ — completely infeasible to compute directly.

**Motivation for Mean Field Theory (2 marks):**
MFT replaces each stochastic $s_i$ with its deterministic mean $m_i = \langle s_i \rangle$. The fixed-point equations $m_i = \tanh(\sum_j w_{ij} m_j / T + b_i/T)$ are solved iteratively in **polynomial time** (linear in the number of connections). This yields the **mean field free energy** $F^{MF}(\mathbf{m})$ which upper-bounds the true free energy $F$, allowing tractable variational learning.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Model Question]`

**a)** What is Mean Field Theory? Derive the Mean Field fixed-point equation for a Boltzmann Machine starting from the conditional probability $P(s_i = +1 \mid \mathbf{s}_{\setminus i})$. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Write the expression for the Mean Field free energy $F^{MF}(\mathbf{m})$. Explain each term and justify why $F \le F^{MF}(\mathbf{m})$ using Jensen's inequality. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — Mean Field Fixed-Point (7 marks)

**Definition of MFT (2 marks):**
Mean Field Theory is an approximation technique that replaces stochastic binary units $s_i \in \{-1,+1\}$ with their deterministic expected values $m_i = \langle s_i \rangle$, converting the intractable stochastic Boltzmann Machine into a tractable deterministic system of self-consistent equations.

**Derivation (5 marks):**
The conditional activation probability in the $\{-1,+1\}$ convention is:

$$P(s_i = +1 \mid \mathbf{s}_{\setminus i}) = \frac{e^{(\sum_j w_{ij} s_j + b_i)/T}}{e^{(\sum_j w_{ij} s_j + b_i)/T} + e^{-(\sum_j w_{ij} s_j + b_i)/T}}$$

The expectation of $s_i$ is:
$$\begin{aligned}
\langle s_i \rangle &= (+1)P(s_i=+1) + (-1)P(s_i=-1) \\
&= \frac{e^{x/T} - e^{-x/T}}{e^{x/T} + e^{-x/T}} \quad \text{where } x = \sum_j w_{ij} s_j + b_i \\
&= \tanh\!\left(\frac{x}{T}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Mean Field substitution** $s_j \to m_j$:
$$\boxed{m_i = \tanh\!\left(\frac{1}{T}\sum_{j} w_{ij} m_j + \frac{b_i}{T}\right)}$$

This is solved iteratively until $\vert m_i^{(t+1)} - m_i^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon$.

#### Part (b) — Mean Field Free Energy (7 marks)

**Expression (3 marks):**
$$\boxed{F^{MF}(\mathbf{m}) = -\sum_{i<j} w_{ij} m_i m_j - \sum_i b_i m_i + T \sum_i H(m_i)}$$

where $H(m_i) = -\frac{1+m_i}{2}\ln\frac{1+m_i}{2} - \frac{1-m_i}{2}\ln\frac{1-m_i}{2}$ is the binary entropy of unit $i$.

**Term-by-term explanation (2 marks):**
- **First two terms:** Expected energy under the factored distribution — same structure as $E$ but with $m_i$ replacing $s_i$.
- **Third term:** Entropy contribution (positive, scaled by $T$) representing the information content / uncertainty of the distribution.

**Jensen's inequality justification (2 marks):**
For any trial distribution $Q(\mathbf{s})$:
$$F = -T\ln Z = -T\ln\sum_{\mathbf{s}} e^{-E(\mathbf{s})/T} \le \langle E \rangle_Q - T H[Q] = F^{MF}(Q)$$

The inequality follows from concavity of $\ln$: $\ln \mathbb{E}[Y] \ge \mathbb{E}[\ln Y]$. Thus $F^{MF}$ is an **upper bound** on $F$ for all $Q$; choosing $Q$ that minimizes $F^{MF}$ yields the tightest approximation.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly LOSE MARKS on this topic:**
> 1. **Sign convention confusion (–1 to 2 marks lost):** Students mix the $\{0,1\}$ and $\{-1,+1\}$ conventions, leading to wrong expressions. The $\{0,1\}$ version uses **sigmoid**; the $\{-1,+1\}$ version uses **tanh**. Examiners **deduct heavily** if conventions are inconsistent.
> 2. **Forgetting the partition function $Z$ (–2 marks):** Many students write $P(\mathbf{s}) = e^{-E(\mathbf{s})/T}$ *without* the $1/Z$ normalization term. **Always include $Z$**.
> 3. **Confusing "Mean Field Theory" with "Mean Squared Error":** MFT in this context means the *self-consistent mean activation* approximation — not a loss function. Examiners mark this strictly.
> 4. **Skipping derivation steps:** KTU valuation requires showing every algebraic step. Writing only the final boxed answer without intermediate derivation loses **3–4 marks** easily.
> 5. **Omitting the entropy term in $F^{MF}$:** Students often write $F^{MF}$ as just the expected energy (first two terms), forgetting the $+T\sum H(m_i)$ entropy contribution. **This is a frequent 1–2 mark deduction**.
> 6. **Writing $|x|$ instead of $\vert x \vert$ in equations:** Use LaTeX `\vert` to avoid formatting errors.

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> ### 🔑 Must-Know Concepts Before Entering the Exam Hall
>
> ✅ **Boltzmann Machine** = stochastic, symmetric, energy-based neural network with binary units.
>
> ✅ **Energy function** $E(\mathbf{s}) = -\sum_{i<j} w_{ij} s_i s_j - \sum_i b_i s_i$ is the cornerstone.
>
> ✅ **Boltzmann distribution** $P(\mathbf{s}) = e^{-E(\mathbf{s})/T}/Z$ follows from **maximum entropy principle**.
>
> ✅ **Partition function** $Z = \sum_{\mathbf{s}} e^{-E(\mathbf{s})/T}$ is **exponentially hard** — the source of all intractability.
>
> ✅ **Temperature $T$** controls stochasticity: low $T$ = deterministic, high $T$ = random.
>
> ✅ **Mean Field Theory** replaces stochastic $s_i$ with deterministic $m_i = \langle s_i \rangle$.
>
> ✅ **Fixed-point equation** $m_i = \tanh(\sum_j w_{ij} m_j / T + b_i / T)$ — solved **iteratively**.
>
> ✅ **Mean Field free energy** $F^{MF} = -\sum_{i<j} w_{ij} m_i m_j - \sum_i b_i m_i + T \sum_i H(m_i)$ — includes **entropy term**.
>
> ✅ **Variational bound:** $F \le F^{MF}(\mathbf{m})$ for all $\mathbf{m}$ — proven via **Jensen's inequality**.
>
> ✅ **Learning rule:** $\Delta w_{ij} = \eta(\langle s_i s_j\rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle s_i s_j\rangle_{\text{model}})$ — requires expectations that MFT approximates efficiently.
>
> ✅ **Convention awareness:** $\{0,1\}$ uses **sigmoid**; $\{-1,+1\}$ uses **tanh** — **never mix them**.
>
> ✅ **Restricted Boltzmann Machine (RBM)** = BM with **no intra-layer connections** (bipartite) — much faster training.
>
> ✅ **RBMs are the building blocks of Deep Belief Networks (DBNs)** used in modern deep learning.
>
> ✅ **Real-world applications:** image recognition, recommender systems (Netflix), speech processing, combinatorial optimization (simulated annealing).
>
> ✅ **Always write $Z$ in the Boltzmann distribution** — examiners will deduct marks if it's missing.
>
> ✅ **Use $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX** (not bare `|`) when writing absolute values.
